Federal  Election  Commission

42.  Section  208(a)  of  the  FECA  Amendments,  88  Stat.
1280,  amends  the  FECA,  2  U.S.C.  Section  437c,  by  pro-
viding  for  the  establishment  of  a  Federal  Election  Commis-
sion.
Said  Commission  is  to  be  composed  of  six  members,
two  appointed  by  the  President  pro  tempore  of  the  Senate
on  the  recommendations  of  the  majority  and  minority  lead-
ers  of  the  Senate,  two  by  the  Speaker  of  the  House  of

58

Representatives  on  the  recommendations  of  the  majority
and  minority  leaders  of  the  House,  and  two  by  the  Pres-
ident.
All  must  be  confirmed  by  a  majority  of  both
Houses.

It  may,  inter  alia:

43.  The  Commission  has  broad  powers,  Section  208(a)
of  the  FECA  Amendments,  88  Stat.  1282,  2  U.S.C.  Sec-
(a)  investigate  with  the
tion  437d.
aid  of  compulsory  process;  (b)  exercise  primary  jurisdic-
tion  with  respect  to  civil  enforcement  of  the  FECA,  as
amended,  by  bringing  actions  (including  proceedings  for
injunction)  in  federal  district  courts;  (c)  refer  violations
to  the  Attorney  General;  (d)  formulate  general  policy
with  respect  to  the  administration  of the  FECA,  the  FECA
Amendments,  and  certain  provisions  of  Title  18  of  the
United  States  Code;  (e)  render  advisory  opinions;  and
(f)  make  rules  necessary  to  carrying  out  the  FECA  and
the  FECA  Amendments.

44.  Section  209(b)(1)  of  the  FECA  Amendments,  88
Stat.  1287,  amends  Section  316(a)  of  the  FECA  to  em-
power  the  Commission  to  make  rules  and  regulations  un-
der  the  provisions  of  the  FECA  and  FECA  Amendments
relating  to  reporting  and  disclosure.
Any  proposed  rule
or  regulation  dealing  with  required  reports  or  statements
by  a  candidate  for  the  office  of  Senator  and  by  political
committees  supporting  such  candidate  shall  be  transmit-
ted  to  the  Senate.
If  the  Senate  does  not  disapprove
such  a  proposed  rule  or  regulation  within  30  legislative
days,  the  Commission  may  prescribe  it.  Any  proposed
rule  or  regulation  dealing  with  required  reports  or  state-
ments  by a  candidate  for  the  office  of  Representative  and
by  political  committees  supporting  such  candidate  shall
be  transmitted  to  the  House.
If  the  House  does  not  dis-
approve  such  proposed  rule  or  regulation  within  30  legisla-
tive  days,  the  Commission  may  prescribe  it.  If a  proposed

59

rule  relates  to  required  reports  or  statements  by  a  candi-
date  for  the  office  of  President  of  the  United  States,  and
by  political  committees  supporting  such  candidate,  it  shall
Both
be  transmitted  to  both  the  House  and  the  Senate.
the  Senate  and  the  House  may  disapprove  any  such  pro-
posed  rule  or  regulation  within  30  legislative  days.

45.  Section  302  of  the  FECA  Amendments,  88  Stat.
1290,  amends  the  FECA,  2  U.S.C.  Section  456,  to  pro-
vide  that  if  the  Commission  finds  that  a  candidate  for  a
federal  office  failed  to  file  a  report  required  by the  FECA
or  the  FECA  Amendments,  such  person  shall  be  disquali-
fied  from  becoming  a  candidate  in  any  future  election  for
federal  office  until  one  year  after  the  expiration  of  the
term  for  which  such  person  was  a  candidate.

Miscellaneous  Definitions

46.  The  FECA  and  FECA  Amendments  define  in  Sec-
tion  301(d)  a  political  committee  as  “any  committee,  club,
association,  or  other  group  of  persons  which  receives  con-
tributions  or  makes  expenditures  during  a  calendar  year  in
an  aggregate  amount  exceeding  $1,000.”

47.  Section  301(e)  of  the  FECA  and  Section  591(e)  of
Title  18  U.S.C.,  as  amended,  define  a  contribution  as  a
gift,  subscription,  loan,  advance,  or  deposit  of  money  or
anything  of  value  made  for  the  purpose  of  influencing  the
nomination  for  election  of  any  person  to  federal  office  or
for  influencing  the  results  of  a  primary  to  select  delegates
to  a  national  nominating  convention  or  influencing  the  re-
sult  of  an  election  held  for  the  expression  of  a  preference
for  the  nomination  of  persons  for  election  to  the  office
of  the  President  of  the  United  States.  News  stories,  com-
mentaries  and  editorials  are  not  excepted  from  these  defi-
nitions.

-
’
) :
;

60

61

48.  Section  301(f)  of  the  FECA  and  Section  591(f)
of Title  18  U.S.C.  define  expenditures  to  mean  a purchase,
payment,  distribution,  loan,  advance,  deposit  or  gift  of
money  or  anything  of  value  made  for  the  purpose  of in-
fluencing  the  nomination  for  election  of  any  person  to
federal  office  or  for  influencing  the  results  of  a  primary
to  select  delegates  to  a  national  nominating  convention
or  influencing  the  results  of  an  election  held  for  the  ex-
pression  of  a  preference  for  the  nomination  of  persons
for  election  to  the  office  of  the  President  of  the  United
States.  News  stories,  commentaries  and  editorials  are  ex-
cepted  from  these  definitions.

Reporting  and  Disclosure

49.  Under  Section  302(b)  of  the  FECA,  as  amended
by  Section  202(a)  of  the  FECA  Amendments,  88  Stat.
1275,  committees  must  keep  detailed  records  identifying
Under  Section
any  person  who  contributes  $10  or  more.
304(a)  of the  FECA,  as  amended  by  Section  204(a)  of  the
FECA  Amendments,  88  Stat.  1277,  pclitical  committees
receiving  contributions  or  making  expenditures  on  behalf
of a  candidate  must  file  reports  with  said  candidate’s  prin-
cipal  campaign  committee  which  comply  in  all  respects
with  the  reports  required  to  be  filed  with  the  Federal  Elec-
tion  Commission  by  the  principal  campaign  committee.  A
principal  campaign  committee  must,  under  Section  304(b)
of  the  FECA,  report  the  full  name,  mailing  address,  and
occupation  of  each  person  who  has  made  one  or  more
contributions  to  or  for  said  committee  in  an  aggregate
amount  in  excess  of  $100,  together  with  the  amount  and
date  of  said  contribution.

50.  Section  204(c)  of  the  FECA  Amendments,  88  Stat.
1277,  amends  Section  304  of  the  FECA  to  provide  that  a

member  of  Congress  need  not  report  as  contributions  re-
ceived  or  expenditures  made  the  value  of services  furnished
by  the  Senate  or  House  recording  studios,  or  by  an  indi-
vidual  whose  pay  is  disbursed  by  the  Secretary  of  the
Senate  or  the  Clerk  of  the  House  of  Representatives,  and
who  furnishes  such  services  as  his  primary  duty  as  an  em-
ployee  of  the  Senate  or  House  of  Representatives,  or  if
such  services  were  paid  for  by  the  Republican  or  Demo-
cratic  Congressional  Committees.
The  section  does  not
apply  to  recording  services  furnished  during  the  calendar
year  before  the  year  in  which  the  member’s  term  expires.

51.  Section  204(c)  of  the  FECA  Amendments,  88  Stat.
1278,  provides  that  every  person  who  makes  contributions
or  expenditures  other  than  by  contributions  to  a  political
committee  or  candidate  in  an  aggregate  amount  in  excess
of  $100  shall  file  with  the  Commision  a  report  contain-
ing the  information  described  in  paragraph  49  of this  com-
plaint.

52.  Section  208(a)  of the  FECA  Amendments,  88  Stat.

1279,  amends  the  FECA  as  follows:

“Sec.  308.  Any  person  (other  than  an  individual
who  expends  any  funds  or  commits  any  act  di-
rected  to  the  public  for  the  purpose  of  influenc-
ing the  outcome  of an  election,  or  who  publishes
or  broadcasts  to  the  public  any  material  refer-
ring  to  a  candidate  (by  name,  description,  or
other  reference)  advocating  the  election  or  de-
feat  of  such  candidate,  setting  forth  the  candi-
date’s  position  on  any  public  issue,  his  voting
record,  or  other  official  acts  (in  the  case  of  a
candidate  who  holds  or  has  held  Federal  office),
or  otherwise  designed  to  influence  individuals
to  cast  their  votes  for  or  against  such  candidate

62

63

or  to  withhold  their  votes  from  such  candidate
shall  file  reports  with  the  Commission  as  if such
person  were  a  political  committee.
The  reports
filed  by  such  person  shall  set  forth  the  source
of  the  funds  used  in  carrying  out  any  activity
described  in  the  preceding  sentence  in  the  same
detail  as  if the  funds  were  contributions  within
the  meaning  of section  301(e),  and  payments  of
such  funds  in  the  same  detail  as  if they  were  ex-
penditures  within  the  meaning  of section  301(f).”

53.  Section  208  of  the  FECA  Amendments,  88  Stat.
1284,  amends  the  FECA  to  provide  that  the  Federal  Elec-
tion  Commission  may  institute  civil  actions  for  relief,  in-
cluding  injunctions,  if  there  is  reason  to  believe  that  a
person  has  engaged,  or  may  engage,  in  acts  or  practices
which  violate  the  Act.  The  Commission  may  also  request
the  Attomey  General  to  bring  civil  actions  for  relief,  in-
cluding  injunctions,  against  violations  of  the  Act.

Penalties

54.  Title  2  U.S.C.  Section  441  provides  that  any  per-
son  who  violates  the  registration,  reporting  and  disclosure
provisions  specified  in  paragraphs  49,  50,  51,  or  52  of
this  complaint  shall  be  fined  not  more  than  $1,000  or
imprisoned  not  more  than  one  year,  or  both.

CAUSES  OF  ACTION

55.  The  FECA  and  the  FECA  Amendments  violate  the
constitutional  rights  of  one  or  more  of  the  plaintiffs  in
that  they  restrict  and  inhibit  political  activity  by  limiting
the  solicitation,  receipt  or  making  of  contributions  on  be-
half  of  political  candidates,  in  violation  of  the  rights  of

freedom  of  speech  and  association  guaranteed  by  the  First
Amendment  to  the  Constitution.

56.  The  FECA  and  the  FECA  Amendments  violate  the

rights  of one  or  more  of the  plaintiffs  in  that  they  restrict
and  inhibit  political  activity  by  preventing  candidates  or
their  immediate  family  from  expending  personal  funds
where  available  and  necessary,  in  violation  of  their  rights
of  freedom  of  speech  and  association  guaranteed  by  the
First  Amendment  to  the  Constitution.

57.  The  FECA  and  the  FECA  Amendments  violate  the
constitutional  rights  of  one  or  more  of  the  plaintiffs  in
that  they  restrict  and  inhibit  political  activity  by  iimiting
the  incidental  expenses  which  volunteers  working  on  be-
half  of  political  candidates  may  incur,  in violation  of their
rights  of freedom  of speech  and  association  guaranteed  by
the  First  Amendment  to  the  Constitution.

58.  The  FECA  and  the  FECA  Amendments  violate  the
constitutional  rights  of  one  or  more  of  the  plaintiffs  in
that  they  limit  expenditures  by  a  candidate  for  federal  of-
fice,  and  thereby  restrict  and  inhibit  the  candidate’s  ability
to  communicate  his  ideas  and  positions,  in  vic:ation  of the
rights  of  freedom  of  speech  and  association  guaranteed  by
the  First  Amendment  to  the  Constitution.

59.  The  FECA  and  the  FECA  Amendments  violate  the
constitutional  rights  of  one  or  more  of  the  plaintiffs  in
that  they  are  designed  to  have,  and  have,  the  effect  of
favoring  most  incumbent  officeholders  and  thereby  disad-
vantaging  most  challengers,  in  violation  of  their  rights  of
freedom  of  speech  and  association  guaranteed  by  the  First
Amendment  to  the  Constitution  and  the  Due  Process  Clause
of  the  Fifth  Amendment  to  the  Constitution.  Incumbents
have,  at  the  government’s  expense,  permanent  offices,  the

64

65

frank,  expense  allowances,  an  established  staff,  access  to
Congressional  television  recording  services,  and  a  variety
of  office  supplies  and  expenses,  none  of  which  constitute
expenditures  within  the  FECA  and  the  FECA  Amend-
ments  when  used  by  them  for  all  but  blatantly  political
purposes.
Challengers  can  employ  such  valuable  resources
only  by  making  expenditures  which  the  Act  limits  to  an
amount  far  below  the  value  of what  is  available  to  incum-
bents  by  virtue  of their  office  alone.
This  disparity  is  in
addition  to  the  political  advantages  which  naturally  inhere
in  incumbency,  such  as  ability  to  make  news  and  wider
name  recognition.

60.  The  FECA  Amendments  deprive  one  or  more  plain-
tiffs  of their  constitutional  rights  in  that  they  allow  incum-
bent  officeholders  and  successful  candidates  to  employ
surplus  campaign  funds  to  defray  the  expenses  of  their
offices,  thus  discriminating  invidiously  against  candidates
challenging  incumbents  and  against  unsuccessful  candidates
for  federal  office,  in  violation  of  the  rights  of  freedom  of
speech  and  association  guaranteed  by  the  First  Amend-
ment  to  the  Constitution  and  the  Due  Process  Clause  of
the  Fifth  Amendment  to  the  Constitution.

61.  The  FECA  and  the  FECA  Amendments  violate  the
constitutional  rights  of  one  or  more  of  the  plaintiffs  in
that  they  discriminate  against  third  parties,  minor  parties,
and  independent  candidacies  which  rely  heavily  on  large
contributions,  in  violation  of  their  rights  of  freedom  of
speech  and  association  guaranteed  by  the  First  Amend-
ment  to  the  Constitution  and  the  Due  Process  Clause
of  the  Fifth  Amendment  to  the  Constitution.

62.  The  FECA  and  the  FECA  Amendments  violate  the
constitutional  rights  of  one  or  more  of  the  plaintiffs  in
that  they  discriminate  against  third  parties,  minor  parties,

and  independent  candidacies  which  have  not  previously
demonstrated  their  ability  to  capture  a  large  percentage
of  the  popular  vote  or  which  run  candidates  on  a  “‘bal-
ance  of  power”  or  “spoiler”  strategy  hoping  to  influence
the  policies  of the  major  parties,  in violation  of their  rights
of  freedom  of  speech  and  association  guaranteed  by  the
First  Amendment  to  the  Constitution  and  the  Due  Process
Clause  of  the  Fifth  Amendment  to  the  Constitution.

63.  The  FECA  and  the  FECA  Amendments  violate  the

constitutional  rights  of  one  or  more  of  the  plaintiffs  in
that  they  limit  the  expenditures  of  national  committees
of  a  political  party  in  the  case  of  campaigns  for  federal
office  in  violation  of  the  rights  of  freedom  of  speech  and
association  guaranteed  by the  First  Amendment  to the  Con-
stitution  and  the  Due  Process  Clause  of  the  Fifth  Amend-
ment  to  the  Constitution.

64.  The  FECA  and  the  FECA  Amendments  violate  the
constitutional  rights  of  one  or  more  of  the  plaintiffs  in
that  they  discriminate  against  political  parties  lacking  a
national  committee,  in  violation  of  the  rights  of  freedom
of  speech  and  association  guaranteed  by  the  First  Amend-
ment  to  the  Constitution  and  the  Due  Process  Clause  of
the  Fifth  Amendment  to  the  Constitution.

65.  The  FECA  and  the  FECA  Amendments  violate  the
constitutional  rights  of  one  or  more  of  the  plaintiffs  in
that  they  discriminate  against  political  parties  sponsoring
a  candidate  for  President  in  only  one  State,  in  violation
of  their  rights  of  freedom  of  speech  and  association  guar-
anteed  by  the  First  Amendment  to  the  Constitution  and
the  Due  Process  Clause  of  the  Fifth  Amendment  to  the
Constitution.

66

67

66.  The  FECA  Amendments  deprive  one  or  more  of
the  plaintiffs  of their  rights  in  that  the  method  of appoint-
ment  of  the  Federal  Election  Commission  violates  the  con-
stitutional  separation  of  powers  and  discriminates  invidi-
ously  against  them  in  violation  of  the  Due  Process  Clause
of  the  Fifth  Amendment  to  the  Constitution.

67.  The  FECA  Amendments  deprive  one  or  more  of
the  plaintiffs  of  their  right  to  a  hearing  before  an  impar-
tial  tribunal  by  entrusting  administration  and  enforcement
of  the  FECA,  as  amended,  to  the  Federal  Election  Com-
mission,  in  violation  of  the  constitutional  separation  of
powers,  Article  III,  and  the  Due  Process  Clause  of  the
Fifth  Amendment  to  the  Constitution.

68.  The  FECA  Amendments  deprive  one  or  more  of
the  plaintiffs  of  their  rights  in  that  the  Federal  Election
Commission  is empowered  to  make  rules  under  the  FECA
in  violation  of the  constitutional  separation  of powers  and
the  Due  Process  Clause  of  the  Fifth  Amendment  to  the
Constitution.

69.  The  FECA  Amendments  deprive  one  or  more  of
the  plaintiffs  of  their  rights  in  that  the  Federal  Election
Commission  has  power  to  disqualify  a  person  from  be-
coming  a  candidate  for  election  to  federal  office  in  vio
lation  of  the  First,  Fifth,  and  Sixth  Amendments  to  the
Constitution.

70.  The  FECA  and  the  FECA  Amendments  violate  the
constitutional  rights  of  one  or  more  of  the  plaintiffs  in
that  they  provide,  through  auditing  procedures,  for  the
Federal  Election  Commission  to  inspect  lists  and  records
required  to  be  kept  by  political  committees  of  individuals
who  contribute  more  than  $10,  in  violation  of their  rights
of  freedom  of  speech  and  association  and  privacy  guaran-
teed  by  the  First,  Fourth,  and  Ninth  Amendments  to  the

Constitution  and  of their  right  to  due  process  of the  Fifth
Amendment  to  the  Constitution.

71.  The  FECA  Amendments  and  the  FECA  violate  the
rights  of  one  or  more  of  the  plaintiffs  in  that  they  re-
quire  political  committees  to  register  and  disclose  the
names  of  those  of  their  contributors  who  contribute  in
excess  Of  $100,  in  violation  of  their  rights  of  freedom  of
speech,  association  and  privacy  guaranteed  by  the  First
Fourth,  and  Ninth  ..mendments  to  the  Constitution  and
of  their  right  to  due  process  of  the  Fifth  Amendment  to
the  Constitution.

72.  The  FECA  and  the  FECA  Amendments  violate  the
constitutional  rights  of  one  or  more  of  the  plaintiffs  in
that  they  discriminate  invidiously  against  them  by  not  re-
quiring  the  same  disclosure  from  incumbent  officeholders
of  all  resources  available  to  such  incumbents,  such  as  the
value  of  services  furnished  by  the  Senate  or  House  record-
ing  studios  or  the  services  of  certain  individuals  on  their
staffs,  in  violation  of the  right  to  due  process  of the  Fifth
Amendment  to  the  Constitution.

73.  The  FECA  and  the  FECA  Amendments  violate  the
constitutional  rights  of  one  or  more  of  the  plaintiffs  in
that  they  provide  that  every  person  contributing  or  ex-
pending  more  than  $100  other  than  by  contribution  to  a
political  committee  or  candidate  (including  volunteers  with
incidental  expenses  in  excess  of  $600)  must  make  disclo-
sure  to  the  Federal  Election  Commission,  in  violation  of
the  rights  of  freedom  of  speech,  association  and  privacy
of  the  First,  Fourth,  and  Ninth  Amendments  to  the  Con-
stitution  and  of  their  right  to  due  process  of  the  Fifth
Amendment  to  the  Constitution.

68

69

74.  The  FECA  and  the  FECA  Amendments  violate  the
constitutional  rights  of  one  or  more  of  the  plaintiffs  in
that  they  require  any  person  who  expends  funds  or  com-
mits  any  act  for  the  purpose  of  influencing  the  outcome
of  an  election  or  who  publishes  or  broadcasts  to  the  pub-
lic  any  material  advocating  the  election  or  defeat  of  a
candidate,  setting  forth  a  candidate’s  position  on  any  pub-
lic  issue,  his  voting  record  or  any  official  acts  to  file  re-
ports  with  the  Commission  as  if such  person  were a polit-
ical  committee,  in  violation  of  the  rights  of  freedom  of
speech,  association  and  privacy  of  the  First,  Fourth,  and
Ninth  Amendments  to  the  Constitution.

75.  The  FECA  and  the  FECA  Amendments  violate  the
constitutional  rights  of  one  or  more  of  the  plaintiffs  in
that  they  provide  criminal  penalties  for  violation  of  the
disclosure  provisions  of  the  statutes,  in  violation  of  the
rights  of  freedom  of  speech,  association  and  privacy  of
the  First,  Fourth,  and  Ninth  Amendments  to  the  Consti-
tution.

76.  The  FECA  and  the  FECA  Amenuments  violate  the
constitutional  rights  of  one  or  more  of  the  plaintiffs  in
that  they  restrain  and  inhibit  individual  political  activity
by  limiting  to  $1,000  the  independent  (not  on  behalf  of
a  candidate)  expenditures  of  any  person  relative  to  an
identified  candidate,  in  violation  of  the  rights  of  freedom
of  speech  and  association  of  the  First  Amendment  to  the
Constitution.

77.  The  FECA  and  the  FECA  Amendments  violate  the
constitutional  rights  of  one  or  more  of  the  plaintiffs  in
that  they  exclude  from  the  definition  of  “political  com-
mittee””  committees  registered  for  less  than  the  period  of
time  prescribed  in  the  statute,  thus  invidiously  discrimi-
nating  against  such  committees  by  prohibiting  them  from

participating  in  the  political  process  for  a  six-month  per-
iod,  in  violation  of  the  rights  of  freedom  of  speech  and
association  guaranteed  by  the  First  Amendment  to  the
Constitution.

78.  The  FECA  and  the  FECA  Amendments  provide  that

the  government  may  bring  criminal  prosecutions  against
anyone  who  violates  or  is  about  to  violate  Section  608
of  the  FECA  and  Section  9008  of  the  Internal  Revenue
Code  of  1954.  The  risk  of  criminal  prosecution  deters
and  restrains  one  or  more  of  the  plaintiffs  and  in  certain
instances  constitutes  a  prior  restraint  of  their  exercise  of
their  rights  of  freedom  of  speech  and  of  association  in
violation  of the  First  Amendment  to  the  Constitution  and
deprives  one  or  more  of  the  plaintiffs  of  due  process  of
law  in  violation  of  the  Due  Process  Clause  of  the  Fifth
Amendment  to  the  Constitution.

79.  The  FECA,  the  FECA  Amendments,  and  Subtitle
H  of  the  Internal  Revenue  Code  of  1954  deprive  one  or
more  of the  plaintiffs  of their  constitutional  rights  in  that
federal  tax  money  is  thereby  used  to  support  political
movements  and  organizations,  in  violation  of  the  restric-
tions  imposed  on  the  federal  taxing  and  spending  power
by  the  First  Amendment  to  the  Constitution.

80.  The  FECA,  the  FECA  Amendments,  and  Subtitle
H  of  the  Internal  Revenue  Code  of  1954  deprive  one  or
more  of  the  plaintiffs  of  their  constitutional  rights  in  that
federal  tax  money  is  thereby  used  to  support  only  certain
political  parties  in  violation  of  the  restrictions  imposed  on
the  federal  taxing  and  spending  power  by the  First  Amend-
ment  to  the  Constitution.

81.  The  FECA,  the  FECA  Amendments,  and  Subtitle
H  of  the  Internal  Revenue  Code  of  1954  deprive  one  or
more  of  the  plaintiffs  of  their  constitutional  rights  in  that

™

71

federal  taxpayers  are  permitted  to  designate  neither  their
preference  for  parties  nor  for  candidates,  in  violation  of
the  rights  of  freedom  of  speech  and  association  guaran-
teed  by  the  First  Amendment  to  the  Constitution.

82.  The  FECA,  the  FECA  Amendments,  and  Subtitle
H  of  the  Internal  Revenue  Code  of  1954  violate  the  con-

they  establish  officially  supported  political  parties  and

discriminate  against  third  parties,  minor  parties  and  inde-

for  President  in  lees  than  ten  States,  ageinst  candidates

'

with  substantial  support  in  less  than  twenty  States,  against
parties  who  have  neither  nominating  conventions  nor  pri-
maries,  and  against  independent  candidates  not  nominated
These  provisions  violate  the

rights  of  freedom  of  speech  and  association  of  the  First
Amendment  to  the  Constitution  and  the  right  to  due  proc-
ess  of  law  of  the  Fifth  Amendment  to  the  Constitution.

83.  The  FECA  and  the  FECA  Amendments  violate  the
constitutional  rights  of  one  or  more  of  the  plaintiffs  in
that  they  empower  the  Federal  Election  Commission  and
the  Attorney  General  to  bring  civil  actions,  including  pro-
ceedings  for  injunctions  against  any  person  who  has  en-
gaged  or  who  may  engage  in  acts  or  practices  which  vio-
late  the  Act.
The  risk  of being  subject  to  civil  injunction
proceedings  deters  and  restrains  one  or  more  of  the  plain-
tiffs  and  in  certain  instances  constitutes  a  prior  restraint
of  the  exercise  of  their  rights  of  freedom  of  speech  and
association  of  the  First  and  Fifth  Amendments  to  the
Constitution.

84.  The  FECA  and  the  FECA  Amendments  violate  the
constitutional  rights  of  one  or  more  of  the  plaintiffs  in
that  they  limit  the  freedom  of  the  press  by  exempting

news  stories,  commentaries,  and  editorials  from  the  statu-
tory  definitions  of  “expenditure”  but  not  from  the  statu-
tory  definitions  of  “‘contribution,”  thus  subjecting  those
press  functions  to  regulation,  in  violation  of  the  rights  of
a  free  press  guaranteed  by  the  First  Amendment  to  the
Constitution.

‘

85.  Titles  I, Il,  III,  and  IV  of  the  FECA  Amendments
deprive  one  or  more  of  the  plaintiffs  of  the  rights  of  free-
dom  of  speech  and  association  and  of  due  process  of  law
under  the  First  and  Fifth  Amendments  to  the  Constitution

ae

because  they*are  excessively  vague  and  overbroad.

86.  Titles  I, II,  II],  and  IV  deprive  one  or  more  of the
plaintiffs  of  their  retained  rights  under  the  Ninth  Amend-
ment  to  the  Constitution.

87.  The  FECA,  the  FECA  Amendments,  and  Subtitle

H  deprive  one  or  meee  of the plaintiffs  of their rights  in
that  these  statutes  will  diminish  the  financial  and  other
political  support  received  by  them  from  adherents  and
supporters,  all  in  violation  of  the  constitutional  provision
enumerated  in  paragraphs  55-86  of  this  complaint.

88.  Unless  application  of the  FECA,  the  FECA  Amend-
ments,  and  Subtitle  H  of  the  Internal  Revenue  Code  of
1954  against  the  plaintiffs  is enjoined  by  this  Court,  plain-
tiffs  will  suffer  irreparable  injury  and  will  be  unable  to
exercise  their  constitutional  rights  to  participate  effectively
in  the  political  process,  to  communicate  their  views  on  is-
sues  of  public  policy,  to  support  candidates  for  federal
office,  and  to  compete  without  discrimination  in  the  elec-
toral  process.

72

Prayer  for  Relief

73

Dated:

February  7,  1975

WHEREFORE,  plaintiffs  pray  that  the  Court  make  ap-
plication  for  the  convening  of  a  three-judge  district  court
and  that  said  three-judge  court  hear  this  action,  and  upon
such  hearing  to:

1.  Order,  adjudge,  decree  and  declare  that  the  FECA,
the  FECA  Amendments,  and  Subtitle  H  are  repugnant  to
the  Constitution  of  the  United  States,  and  that  said  stat-
utes  violate  plaintiffs’  rights  under  the  Constitution  of the
United  States;  and  further

2.  Permanently  enjoin  and  restrain  defendants,  their
agents  and  assistants  from  the  use,  operation,  enforce-
ment,  execution  and  application  of the  FECA,  the  FECA
Amendments,  and  Subtitle  H  in  any  and  all  respects  in
which  the  same  may  be  found  te  be  repugnant  to  the
Constitution  of  the  United  States;  and  further

3.  Grant  such  order  and  further  relief  as  to  the  Court
may  seem  just  and  proper,  together  with  the  costs  and

disbursements  of  this  action.

Plaintiffs  further  pray,  pursuant  to  Section  31 5(a)  of
the  FECA,  88  Stat.  1285,  2 U.S.C.  Section  437h,  that  this
Court  immediately  certify,  to  the  United  States  Court  of
Appeals  for  the  District  of  Columbia  Circuit  all  questions
of  constitutionality  raised  herein.

of  constitutional  questions  from  the  District  Court,  Cor-

Court  shall  proceed  to  consider  the  instant  matter  en  banc,
pursuant  to  Section  315(a),  28  U.S.C.  846(c)  and  Rule  35
(a),  Fed.  R.  App.  P.

It  is

the  hearing  of  witnesses,  so  that  appropriate  procedures
may  be  considered  for  adoption  by  the  Court.

IN  RE:  No.  75-1061  —  James  L.  Buckley,  United  States

Senator  from  the  State  of New
York,  et  al  v.  Honorable  Francis  R.
Valeo,  Secretary,  United  States
Senate,  et  al

Brice  M.  Clagett,  Esquire
888  16th  Street,  N.W.

Gentlemen:

By  order  of  the  Court  entered  on  February  19,  1975,  the
Clerk  is instruct.d  to  “inquire  of each  of the  parties  by  let-
ter  as  to  their  expectations  regarding  the  need  for  the  taking
of  evidence,  or  the  hearing  of  witnesses,  so  that  appropri-
ate  procedures  may  be  considered  for  adoption  by  the
Court.”

This  preliminary  inquiry  made  in  the  interest  of  expedi-
tion,  of  course  does  not  indicate  any  stance  of  the  Court
vis-a-vis  the  currently  pending  motions.

Depending  upon  the  foreseen  need  for  adducing  evidence,
such  procedures  may  include  appointment  of  a  special  mas-

79

in  whole  or  in  part  not  admitted,  each  party  will  that  day
file  documented  offers  of  proof  establishing  what  it  sub-
mits  the  fact  to  be.

It  is

FURTHER  ORDERED  by  the  court  that  oral  argument
on  the  pending  motions  to  remand  and  to  dismiss  will  be
heard  on  April  2,  1975.  Counsel  will  be  notified  as  to  the
time  for  oral  argument  at  a  later  date.

Per  Curiam

For  the  Court

ORDER

This  matter  is  before  the  court  for  consideration  of  fact-
finding  procedures  to  be  adopted  pursuant  to  the  order  of
this  court  dated  February  19,  1975,  and  further  pursuant
to  the  parties’  responses  to  the  inquiry  of  our  Clerk  dated
It  appears  to
February  20,  1975  on  the  instant  subject.
the  court  from  the  responses  to  the  Clerk’s  letter  filed  that
all  parties  approve  of  commencement  of  fact-finding  prior
to  disposition  of  the  pending  motions  to  remand  and  to
dismiss.

Therefore,  it  is

ORDERED  by  the  court  that  the  parties  file  proposed

findings  of fact,  and  in  conjunction  with  each  such  sepa-
rate  proposed  finding  shall  file  a documented  offer  of
proof  in  support  thereof,  such  documented  offer  to  con-
sist  of  actual  documents,  affidavits,  depositions,  or  other
evidence  in  tangible  form,  these  filings  to  be  made  within

twenty  (20)  days  of  this  order.
of  the  above  filing  date,  the  parties  will  file  responses  in-
dicating  which  of  the  first  round  of  submitted  proposed

which  they  deny.  As  to  those  proposed  findings  of  fact

MEMORANDUM

By the  attached  order,  we seek  to con-
form  our  treatment  of  this  matter  with
the special  review  procedure  set forth  in
Section  315(a)  of  the  Federal  Election
Campaign  Act,  as  amended  (2  U.S.C.
§ 437h).  That  section  requires  that  after
the instituting
of an action  “as may  be
appropriate
to construe  the constitution-
ality  of any  provision  of this  Act or of
section  608,  610,  611,  613,  614,  615,  616,
or  617  of  title  18,  United  States  Code,”

82

BUCKLEY  v.  VALEO

same  result,  at  least  at  this  juncture,
even  assuming  that  Section  315(a)  estab-
lishes  a  sui  generis  procedure  and  con-
templates  that,  in  the  interest  of expedi-
tion,  the Court  of  Appeals  properly  pro-
ceeds  by  appointing
its  own  master  to
make  a  report  of  proposed  findings.  Our
order  is  consonant  with  both  these  ap-
proaches,  in  practical  aspect,  and  it  is
unnecessary  to  make  a  choice  between
them  at  this  time.
It  is  also  consistent
with  the  intention  of  Congress  for  expe-
dition  in  appellate  disposition.’

Certain  of  the  parties  have  questioned
whether  Subtitle  H of  the  Internal  Reve-
nue  Code  (the  public  financing  of  presi-
dentia!  elections  provisions)  is  subject  to

BUCKLEY  v.  VALEO

marily  with  adjudicatory  facts,  or  events
which  are  themselves  in  dispute  between

which  disputes  by the  trier  of  fact,  appli-
cation  of  law  might  well  turn.  And  the
certification  involved,  as  I  just  noted,
was  of  independent  legal  questions  not
the  interrelated  questions  presented  by
this  major  constitutional  case.
At  issue
in  this  case  is  whether  statutory  prohibi-
tions  actually  infringe  upon  constitution-
al rights.  The  facts  bearing  upon  these
questions  are—we  should  assume  absent
specific  evidence  of  particular  adjudica-
tory  disputes—legislative  facts.
In  no
way  is  the  trial  court  better  qualified
than  we  to  isolate  and  to  decide  those
will
many  questions  whose  resolution
turn  on  such  legislative  facts.
Further,
if  the  court  below  chooses  to  immerse
itself  in  the long  record  made  while  the
matter  was  on  our  docket,  even  more
time  may  be  lost.

The  court  tells  us  it  does  not  decide
whether  these  Supreme  Court  cases  and
their  gioas  on  “certification”  requires  a
formal  certification  procedure.
But  I
perceive  no  other  explanation  for  the
court’s  action.
Even  if  we  ignore  the
term  “remand,”  the  practical  effect  of
the  court’s  decision  is  still  to  require
compliance  with  these  decisions  on  “cer-
tification.”
As  to  the  majority's  appar-
ent  view  that  this  case  must  conform  to
norma!  judicial  procedures  of  initial  deci-
sion  and  appellate  review,  I  think  that
the  entire  thrust  of  the  review  provision
added  by  the  1974  Amendments  was  in-

ferent  outcomes  will  turn  on  interpretation
rather  than  disputed  events  shared  by the par-

tentionally  to  make  this  case  sui  generis.
In  that  regard,  I  would  prefer  selection
as  Special  Master  of  an  Article  III  judge
to  consider  discovery  requests  and  organ-
ize  the  already  bulky  factual  presenta-
tions.  The  parties
could  then  make  mo-
tions  for  judgment  in  this  court  on  the
basis  of  the  factual  submissions.
The
majority's  desire  to  “regularize”  proceed-
ings  seems  based  on  an  unnecessarily
technical  view  of  our  jurisdiction,  a  view
motivated  more,  I take  it, by  a  fear  that
the  Supreme  Court  might  reverse  on
such  grounds  than  on  a  serious  concern
that  assumption  of  jurisdiction  would
tamper  with  some  important  policy  of
or  impair  the
judicial  administration
rights  of  any  party  or  potential  interve-
nor.

when  he  stated  at  oral  argument  that
any  such  fears  should  not  be  given  sig-
nificant  weight.  The  Supreme  Court,  as
well  as  we,  can  perceive  the  sui generis
nature  of  this  case  and  of  Congress’  com-
mand  of  expedition.
And  it  will  surely
conclude,  as  I  think  we  must,  that  no
policy  of judicial]  administration  prevents
a  “nonregular”  treatment  of  the  case.
If this  case  is remanded  to  the  District
Court,  and  if discovery  proceeds  there,  it
may  be  utterly  impossible  for  the  May

to be met.  As  it is, that
date  would  only  give  this  court  a  bare
six  weeks  to hear  objections
to the Dis-
trict  Court’s  formulation  of  questions
and  briefing  and  arguments  on  the  mer-

trict  Court  to  mean  that  the  case  should

That  being  the case,  I  am of  the view

appointing  a  Special  Master  to  organize

would  provide  a  sufficient  framework  to

a  procedure
would  also  be  more  in  harmony  with  the
statute’s  call  for  expedition  and  avoid

wasteful  duplication.
I would  deny  the  motion  for  remand  and

remand.

Such

to

26

pursuant

conventions
U.S.C.
§ 9008&e).  And,  of  course,  this  court  and
its  personnel  would  have  to  re-immerse
itself  in  the  case  and  duplicate  work  per-
formed  by the  District Court.  A brief-
ing  schedule  which  contemplated  ap-
pointment  of  a  special  master  by  this
court  would  leave  little  time  to  spare  for
decisions  of this  matter,  and | totally  fail
to  perceive  how  remand  will  speed
things  up.  This  delay  is  particularly  un-
fortunate  considering  the  fact  that  the
court  has  had  the  motion  for  remand
under  advisement  officially  since  early
February.
The  delay  in  ruling  on  the
motion  to  remand  is.in  my  view  cause
enough  to  deny  it,  considering  the  fur-
ther  delay  granting  it  will  cause.

Further,  I  lay  great  emphasis  upon
section  315(a)'s  use  of  the  word  “immedi-
ately”  in  the  phrase  “immediately  shall
certify.”
Some  have  argued  that  “im-
mediately”  here  means  the  equivalent  of
“certify  prior  to  deciding  those  constitu-
tional  questions  it has  formulated.”
But
I  would  read the use of  the word  “imme-
diately,”  in  conjunction  with  the  absence
of any time  provision  relative  to the Dis-

Order  of  District  Court
(April  25,  1975)

UNITED  STATES  DISTRICT  COURT
FOR  THE  DISTRICT  OF  COLUMBIA

[Caption  Omitted  in  Printing]

ORDER

This  three-judge  Court,  having  been  convened  to  con-
sider  the  complaint  filed  herein  for  a  declaratory  judgment
and  an  injunction  restraining  the  enforcement  or  operation
of  provisions  of  Subtitle  H  of  the  Internal  Revenue  Code
of  1954  for  repugnance  to  the  Constitution  of  the  United
States,  and  having  determined  that  the  proceedings  herein
should  parallel  proceedings  pending  in  the  United  States
Court  of  Appeals  for  the  District  of  Columbia  Circuit  as
to  Subtitle  H  and  other  Acts  of  Congress,

It  is,  this  25th  day  of  April,  1975,  by  the  Court,  sua

sponte,

ORDERED  that,  pursuant  to  28  U.S.C.  8 2284(5),  the
Honorable  Howard  F.  Corcoran,  as  one  of  the  judges  of
this  three-judge  Court,  be  and  hereby  is  designated  to

1.

2.

Identify  the  constitutional  issues  raised  with  re-
spect  to  Subtitle  H;

Take  whatever  may  be  necessary  in  the  form
of  evidence  —  over  and  above  submissions  that
may  suitably  be  handled  through  judicial  notice,
as  Of  legislative  facts,  supported  by  legislative
history  or  works  reasonably  available,  to  the
extent  not  controverted  in  material  and  sub-
stantial  degree;

87

3.

4.

Make  findings  of  fact  with  reference  to  those
issues;  and

Report  to  this  Court  the  constitutional  ques-
tions  arising  from  steps  1,  2, and  3.

Re 8 ny he at aa

The  action  of  the  single  judge  shall  be  reviewable  by
the  full  three-judge  court  at  any  time  before  final  hearing.
The  case  will  be  heard  and  determined  on  the  merits  by
the  three  judges  at  the  earliest  practicable  date.

/s/  David  L.  Bazelon

United  States  Circuit  Judge

Letter  from  William  T.  Lake  to  John  R.  Bolton
(May  1,  1975)

WILMER,  CUTLER  &  PICKERING
1666  K  Street,  N.W.
Washington,  D.C.  20006
May  1, 1975

John  R.  Bolton,  Jr.,  Esquire
Covington  &  Burling
888  16th  Street,  N.W.
Washington,  D.C.  20006

Dear  John:

You  have  asked  whether  Marian  Pearlman  can  supply  a
further  breakdown  of  the  contribution  figures  for  the  1972

88

89

McGovern  campaign  contained  in  her  affidavit  submitted
in  connection  with  the  Buckley  Case.
Specifically,  you
asked  whether  the  figures  for  contributions  over  and  under
$1,000  can  be  broken  down  into  shorter  time  periods.

Marian  informs  me  that  her  records  do  not  make  it  pos-
sible  to  break  down  further  the  figures  for  the  pre-January
1971  period  or  the  January  1971-February  1972  period.
However,  it  is  possible  to  provide  the  following  further
breakdown  of  the  February  1972-April  7,  1972  period  de-
scribed  in  paragraph  9  of  the  affidavit.

First,  the  January  1971-February  1972  period  for  which
figures  are  given  in  paragraph  8 of  the  affidavit  is  the  peri-
od  up  to  February  1,  1972.

Second,  between  February  1,  1972  and  March  10,  1972,

a  total  of  2,984  contributions  were  received,  in  the  total
amount  of  $240,278.  The  average  contribution  was  $80.52.
Of  these  contributions,  21  exceeded  $1,000,  for  a  total  of
$173,571.  Considering  the  only  first  $1,000  of  any  con-
tribution,  the  total  received  during  this  period  was  $87,707.

Third,  between  March  11,  1972  and  April  6,  1972,  a
total  of  9,951  contributions  were  received,  in  the  total
amount  of  $271,405.  The  average  contribution  was  $27.27.
Of  these  contributions,  ten  exceeded  $1,000,  for  a  total  of
$29,704.
Considering  only  the  first  $1,000  of  any  contrib-
ution,  the  total  received  during  this  period  was  $251,701.

For  the  period  after  April  7,  1972,  the  figures  in  the  af-

fidavit  for  contributions  of  $100.00  or  less  were  taken  from
the  McGovern  campaign  records  and  are  broken  down  in
paragraph  11(a)  of  the  affidavit  according  to  reporting
period.  The  figures  in  the  affidavit  for  contributions  over
$100.00  were  taken  from  the  General  Accounting  Office

compilation  attached  to  the  affidavit,  which  gives  only  the
totals  for  the  period  between  April  7,  1972  and  December
31,  1972.

Sincerely,

/s/  °  Bill
William  T.  Lake

Order  of  District  Court
(August  15,  1975)

UNITED  STATES  DISTRICT  COURT
FOR  THE  DISTRICT  OF  COLUMBIA

[Caption  Omitted  in  Printing]

ORDER

This  Court  having,  by  order  filed  April  25,  1975,  desig-

nated  the  Honorable  Howard  F.  Corcoran,  one  of  the
judges  of  this  three-judge  Court,  to  identify  the  constitu-
tional  issues  raised  herein  with  respect  to  Subtitle  H  of
the  Internal  Revenue  Code  of  1954,  to  take  evidence  and
make  findings  of  fact  with  reference  to  those  issues,  and
to  report  to  this  Court  the  constitutional  questions  arising
from  those  steps;  and

Judge  Corcoran  having  duly  engaged  in  the  activities
aforesaid,  and  having  by  order  filed  May  28,  1975,  duly
reported  to  this  Court  his  action  and  the  constitutional
questions  which  this  Court  is  called  upon  to  consider  in
connection  with  Subtitle  H,  and  no  party  having  objected
to  Judge  Corcoran’s  action  or  report  or  having  sought  re-
view  thereof  by  the  full  court;

90

It  is,  this  15th  day  of  August,  1975,  by  the  Court

ORDERED  that  the  aforesaid  action  and  report  of Judge

Corcoran  be  and  hereby  are  confirmed  in  all  respects.

/s/  David  L.  Bazelon
United  States  Circuit  Judge

/s/  Spottswood  W.  Robinson,  III
United  States  Circuit  Judge

/s/  Howard  F.  Corcoran
United  States  District  Judge

This  Court  was  ordered  to  return  the  augmented  record

to  the  Court  of  Appeals  no  later  than  May  19,  1975.

A.  FINDINGS  OF  FACT

The  findings  of  fact  of  the  Court  are  submitted  in  three

parts.

1  Buckley  v.  Valeo,  No.  75-1061  (D.C.  Cir.  April  14,  1975)  (en
banc).  Compare  Buckley  v  Valeo,  387  F.  Supp.  135  (D.D.C.  1975).

[2]

PART I includes  “Findings  of  Fact  Agreed  to  by  the
Parties”  which  were  compiled  by  counsel.  They  are  hereby
adopted  by  the  Court.?

PART  II  includes  “Statistical  Findings  of  Fact’?  which

were  also  compiled  by  counsel  and  agreed  by  them.?
This  Part  primarily  consists  of  statistics  drawn  from  the
doctoral  dissertation,  deposition  and  affidavit  of  Roland  J.
Cole,  the  plaintiffs’  expert,  and  from  two  affidavits  of  J.J.
Conway,  the  defendants’  expert,  and  the  ten-volume  Com-
All  of  the  un-
mon  Cause  Campaign  Monitoring  Project.
derlying  factual  data  in  this  Part  is  in  the  record  of  this
case.

The  Court  adopts  these  findings.

In  addition  to  assembling  the  material  in  PARTS  I and

II,  counsel  for  the  parties  have  conducted  extensive  dis-
covery  through  interrogatories,  admissions,  production  of
Additionally,  numerous  affi-
docurrents  and  depositions.
In  view  of  the  limitations  of
davits  have  been  submitted.
time  imposed  by  the  remand  order  counsel  considered  such

2

discovery  techniques  to  be  more  expeditious  and  productive
than  the  conduct  of  evidentiary  hearings,  and  so  informed
the  Court.
Accordingly,  no  in-court  evidentiary  hearings
were  conducted,  although  they  were  permitted  (but  not  re-
quired)  by  the  remand  order.
Based  upon  the  evidence
adduced  through  the  foregoing  discovery,  counsel  for  the
parties  submitted  proposed  findings.

2 In  submitting  “agreed”  findings  of  fact,  the  parties  have  agreed

; See  note  2 supra.

B.  CONSTITUTIONAL  QUESTIONS

In  the  same  cooperative  spirit  with  which  they  arrived
at  agreed  findings  of  fact,  counsel  for  the  parties,  in  con-
junction  with  the  Court,  have  agreed  to  the  constitutional
questions  in  the  case.  Those  questions,  keyed  to  the  First
Amended  Complaint,  are  set  forth  in  PART  IV  hereof  and
are  hereby  certified  to  the  Court  of  Appeals.

C.  ORDER

The  Clerk  of  the  District  Court  is  hereby  ordered  to  re-
turn  forthwith  to  the  Clerk  of the  Court  of Appeals  the  aug-
mented  record  of  the  above-captioned  action.

Findings  of  Fact
(May  19,  1975)

[1]

I.

FINDINGS  OF  FACT  AGREED  TO  BY  THE
PARTIES!

1.  The  American  political  process  encompasses  a  wide

variety  of  activities  by  governmental  units  and  by  indi-
viduals,  groups,  and  organizations  perceiving  an  interest  in

the  actions  of  such  units.
for  elective  office,  the  formulation  of  proposals,  plans,  and
programs  for  legislative  or  executive  action,  debate  over

Such  activities  include  campaigns

4

whether  or  not  and  in  what  form  such  proposals,  plans,  and
programs  should  be  implemented,  and  debate  over  the  ef-
fects  of  programs  and  plans  eventually  implemented.  Such
activities  are  relatively  continuous,  although  particular  ac-
tivities  may  be  engaged  in  more  at  one  time  than  another.

2.  Activities  occurring  during  political  campaigns  affect
the  proposals,  plans  and  programs  formulated  and  debated
for  legislative  or  executive  action,  and  also  affect  the  debate
over  the  effects  of programs  and  plans  already  implemented.
Activities

1 Federal  defendants  do  not  join  in. submitting  as  agreed  facts  para-
graphs  28,  101-03,  105,  108-111,  118,  124,  128,  130-32,  136-45,  147,
and  158-64.

[2]

occurring  between  campaigns  and  involving  debate  over  pro-
posals,  plans  and  programs  for  legislative  and  executive  ac-
tion,  and  the  effects  of  programs  and  plans  already  imple-
mented,  affect  the  outcome  of  political  campaigns.

3.  Important  shifts  in  public  opinion  on  political  issues

occur  between  campaigns  for  elective  office.

4.  Electoral  outcomes  are  affected  by,  inter  alia:

the
personality  of  each  of  the  candidates,  incumbency,  the  re-
lative  availability  of  campaign  money,  the  campaign  organi-
zation  of  each  candidate,  coverage  of  each  candidate  by
the  media,  international  events,  domestic  events,  the  struc-
ture  of  each  Party,  each  candidate’s  identification  with  par-
ticular  issues,  the  issue  orientation  of  each  candidate,  voter
predisposition,  the  organization  of  interested  groups,  and
the  personal  life  of  each  candidate.

Each  factor  will  have

a  different  effect  in  different  elections.

r)  appearing  on  radio  or  television  programs;

5.  Citizens  desiring  to  participate  in,  and  affect  the  out-
come  of,  the  political  process  in  order  to  further  their  ideo-
logical.  economic  or  political  goals  may  engage  in  a  variety
of  activities  such  as,  inter

S)  making  speeches;

[4]

[3]

alia:

a)  joining  an  organization  dedicated  to  those  gaols:

b)  contributing  money  to  such  organizations:
c)  engaging  in  demonstrations  over  appropriate  issues:
d)  organizing  and  attending  rallies  intended  to  publi-

cize  particular  viewpoints:

t)  organizing  or  sponsoring  seminars  or  teach-ins;

u)  running  for  elective  office;

v)  contributing  financial  support  to  candidates  for  elec-

tive  office;

w)  contributing  personal  services  to  candidates  for  elec-

tive  office;

x)  contributing  other  things  of  value  to  candidates  for

elective  office;

y)  using  personal  resources  to  campaign  for  elective

e)  making  financial  contributions  to  those  organizing

office;

such  rallies;

f)  attending  and  speaking  at  public  hearings;

z)  exercising  the  powers  of  elective  or  appointive  office:

aa)  employing  resources  available  to  elected  officials  to

g)  distributing  handbills  stating  appropriate  views;

do  the  above;

h)  paying  for  the  distribution  of  such  handbills;

i)  lobbying  for  or  against  legislation;

j)  writing  letters  to  officeholders  expressing  one’s  views:

bb)  engaging  in  litigation;

cc)  financing  litigation;

dd)  doing  favors  for  voters.

k)  writing  books:

1)  financing  books;

m)  organizing  like-minded  citizens:

n)  drafting  and  circulating  petitions;

0)  publishing  a  newspaper  or  periodical:

p)  producing  a  radio  or  television  program:

q)  buying  advertisements  in  periodicals,  newspapers.  or

the  electronic  media:

The  extent  to  which  an  individual  or  group  engages  in
such  activities  depends  on  the  individual  or  group’s  poli-
tical,  social  and  economic  views,  needs,  interests,  intensity
of  conviction  and  access  to  appropriate  resources.
Such
views,  needs,  interests,  intensity  of  conviction  and  access
to  resources  differ  from  individual  to  individual,  from
group  to  group  and  from  time  to  time.
gaging  in  one  kind  of  activity  may  induce  an  individual
to  engage  instead

Difficulty  in  en-

[5j
in  a  different  activity.  The  activities  described  above  will
affect  the  results  of  the  political  process.

6.  Political  campaigns  seek  to  gather  support  for  poli-
tical  candidates  and  parties,  and  to  translate  such  support
into  sufficient  votes  to  win  elections.  They  may  serve  to
reinforce  the  voting  intentions  and  party  loyalties  (where
applicable)  of  those  whose  votes  the  candidates  could  nor-
mally  expect  to  receive.  They  arouse  persons  indifferent
at  the  outset  of  the  campaign  and  induce  them  to  vote.
Finally,  they  convert  some  voters  whose  initial  intentions
were  to  vote  for  the  candidate’s  opponent.

7.  Political  campaigns  provide  information  to  the  voting

public  on  matters  such  as:  candidate  and  party  identifica-
tion:  issues  of  public  importance  and  concern;  the  candi-
dates’  philosophy,  ideas,  positions  on  issues  of  public  im-
portance  and  concern,  and  programs;  the  candidates’  re-
actions  to  the  statements  and  positions  of  his  or  her  op-
ponents;  and  current  events  relevant  to  the  impending  elec-
tion.

8.  A  vigorous  campaign  can  increase  the  name  recogni-

tion  of  a  candidate  for  federal  office.

[6]

9.  Until  April  7,  1972,  the  effective  date  of  the  Fed-
eral  Election  Campaign  Act  of  1971,  reports  concerning
contributions  to  candidates  for  federal  elective  office  were
incomplete  in  many  cases.

10.  The  following  table  shows  (a)  the  estimated  resi-
dent  population  eligible  to  vote  in  federal  elections  for
the  period  1960-1972,  (b)  the  total  number  of  votes  cast
for  candidates  for  President  and  for  the  House  of  Repre-

8

sentatives,  and  (c)  the  percentages  of  the  eligible  popula-
tion  actually  voting  for  those  offices  in  those  years:

11.  The  Consumer  Price  Index  was  4.7%  higher  in  1964

In  October,  1974,  the  month  the  FECA  Amendments  were
passed,  the  Consumer  Price  Index  was  72.5%  higher  than  it
was  in  1960.

14.  The  total  amount  spent  on  elections  for  all  offices  in
1972  (estimated  at  $400,000,000)  was  less  than  the amount
spent  in  1972  by the  two  largest  commercial  advertisers.

15.  Campaign  costs  as  a  percent  of  overall  government

costs  are  approximately  one-tenth  of  one  percent.

bent  U.S.  Representatives  and  only  15  percent  of  incum-
20

percent  of  the  300  incumbents  eligible  to  run  for  reelec-
tion  for  the  Senate  between  1958  and  1974  did  not  run.

21.  The  percentage  of  adults  who  could  name  their
Congressman  rose  from  46  to  53  percent  between  1966
and  1970.

22.

Since  World  War  II,  the  importance  of  party  in

election  results  has  declined,  while  the  importance  of  in-
cumbency  has  increased.

cluded  from  this  table.
number  of  districts  analyzed  may  exceed  the  total  number  of  incum-

b  Four  of  the  five  districts  covered  in  these  units  were  in  Ala-

bama  in  1948,  where  President  Truman,  the  regular  Democratic

©  In  1964,  Robert  E.  French,  a  nonincumbent  Republican  House

in  Alabama’s  Sth  District.

[15]

24.  Incumbent  officeholders  have  numerous  resources
at  their  disposal  which  are  made  available  at  the  govern-
Such  resources  for  Senators  and  Repre-
ment’s  expense.
sentatives  include:

a.  $409,440  per  term  for  salaries  for  a  staff  of  up  to
18  employees  for  Representatives,  and  a  variable  allowance
for  Senators,  the  highest  of  which  is  $1,503,960  per  term.

b.  $2,000  per  term  for  four  student  interns.

16

c.

a  three-room  rent-free  suite  of  offices  in  Washington,
and  “no  more  than  three  district  offices  outfitted  with  of-
fice  equipment,  carpeting,  and  draperies  at  the  expense  of
the  General  Services  Administration,”  the  value  of  which
per  term  has  been  estimated  at  $18,000.

d.  $13,000  per  term  ($6,500  per  regular  session)  for
Representatives  and  $10,000  per  term  ($5,000  per  regular
session)  for  Senators  to  cover  the  cost  of  Stationery  and
office  supplies.

e.  $4,000  for  office  expenses  per  term  in  the  home

district.

f.  37  round-trips  each  term  for  Representatives  and  up
to  45  such  trips  for  Senators;  6 round-trips  for  staff  travel
to  the  State  or  District.

g.  $2,280  per  term  ($1,140  per  year)  for  Representa-
tives  and  $3,480  per  term  ($1,740  per  year)  for  Senators
for  air  mail  and/or  special  delivery  postage  stamps.

h.  Telephone  service  (installation  of  six  lines  and  one
recorder  coupler)  valued  at  $3,536  per  term;  use  of  the
Federal  Telecommunications  System  (unlimited  calls  week-
days after  office  hours  and  on  weekends,  and  100,000  mes-
Sage  units  per  regular  session  (one  minute  of  a  long  dis-
tance  call  equals  four  units;  one  word  of  a  telegram  equals
two  units;  one  word  of  a  night  letter  equals  one  unit),  all
valued  at  $5,160  per  term;  District  phone

[16]

allowance  valued  at  $4,200  per  term  ($600  per  quarter).

i.  $5,500  per  term  for  the  purchase  of  electric  and
mechanical  office  equipment,  and  an  allowance  of  $15,600

|

17

per  term  ($650  per  month)  for  the  rental  of  office  equip-
ment;  furnishings  for  the  Washington  offices,  maintenance
and  repair  are  provided  free  —  and  valued  at  $15,000  per
term.

j.  Congressional  research  assistants  are  available  at  the
Library  of  Congress  for  work  on  speeches,  to  answer  in-
quiries  from  constituents,  and  for  other  matters.

k.  480,000  heavy-duty  brown  envelopes,  valued  at
$6,000  and  labeled  ‘Public  Document  —  Official  Business”
per  year;  (960,000  envelopes  per  term  valued  at  $12,000).

|.  free  and  unlimited  use  of  the  frank  for  first-class
mail  relating  to  the  “conduct  of  official  business,  activi-
ties  and  duties  of’  Congress.  The  value  of  the  franking
privilege  is  estimated  at  about  $100,000  per  term.

m.

a  publication  allowance,  including  68  complimen-

tary  subscriptions  to  the  Congressional  Record,  plus  three
for  personal  use.  The  value  of  these  is  $3,195.

n.

other  publications,  useful  both  to  the  Member’s  of-

fice  and  to  constituents,  including  3,000  wall  calendars
per  term,  thirty-three  copies  of  the  Congressional  Direc-
tory,  a  set  of  the  United  States  Code,  five  subscriptions
to  the  Federal  Register,  and  thousands  of  copies  of  sev-
eral  pamphlets:  “How  Our  Laws  Are  Made”  (2,000  copies
per  term),  “Our  American  Government”  (2,500  copies  per
term),  “Our  Flag”  (200  per  term),  debate  topic  books
(200  per  term),  “Capital:  Symbol  of  Freedom”  (1,000
per  term),  and  reprints  of  the  Constitution  in  large,  small,
and  pocket  sizes.

0.

publications  from  Federal  agencies;  Agriculture  Year-

books  (800  per  term);  Agriculture  Bulletins  (20,000  per
term);  maps  (10,000  per  term)  from  the  Geological  Survey;

materials  from  the  Department  of  Health,  Education  and
Welfare  such  as  “Your  Child  1-6”  (6,000  per  term),  “‘In-
fant  Care”  (6,000  per  term),  drug

[17]

abuse  pamphlets  (20,000  per  term),  and  publications  from
the  Children’s  Bureau  (50  per  month  of  each).

p.

use  of  the  radio  and  television  recording  studio,  and

the  services  of  “folding  room”  personnel.

q.  free  underground  parking  on  Capitol  Hill.

rt.

free  emergency  medical  care  from  physicians  and

nurses  at  the  Capitol  Hill  medical  center  (which  contains
an  €¢xamination  room  and a laboratory).

s.

free  drugs  if  prescribed  by  Capitol  Hill  physicians.

t.  government-subsidized  health  insurance  plans.

u.

a  $45,000  life  insurance  policy  at  special  rates  plus

benefits  for  the  surviving  spouse.

v.

free  swimming  pools  and  gym  (one  for  each

Chamber).

Ww.

free  steam  rooms,  masseurs,  and  physiotherapy.

x.

free  haircuts  for  Senators;  haircuts  at  $2  for  House
members  at  special  barber  shops  (beauty  shops  at  reduced
rates  for  female  members).

y.

free  plants  and  flowers.

2.  reduced-rate  meals  (free  of  sales  taxes)  at  private
dining  rooms  in  the  Capitol.

18

19

aa.

a  salary  of  $42,500  per  year.

bb.

liberal  retirement  benefits  after  five  years  of  service

(maximum  of  $34,000  a  year  after  32  years  service).

cc.

the  use  of  the  computer  for  the  maintenance  of

mailing  lists  and  the  selection  of  names  for  specialized
mailings.

25.  Resources  available  to  incumbent  Presidents  are  as

follows:

[18]

a.

free  residence  (The  White  House)  and  all  facilities

appurtenant  thereto;

b.

free  offices  (The  White  House  and  the  Executive

Office  Building);

c.

use  of  penalty  mail;

d.

use  of  the  Army  Special  Corps  for  any  communica-

tions  needs;

e.

use  of  the  Secret  Service;

f.  use  of  military  transportation  (sixteen  airplanes  and

sixteen  helicopters)  as  Commander-In-Chief,  and  use  of
Air  Force  One  for  all  but  avowedly  explicit  political  trips;

g.  an  extensive  staff  (the  total  size  of  which  is  be-
tween  500  and  600)  in  the  Executive  Office  of  the  Presi-
dent,  including  those  performing  executive  functions  and
also

(a)  speechwriters;
(b)  photographers;  and
(c)  researchers.

20

h.

a  staff  of  approximately  one  hundred  domestic  ser-

vants,  gardeners,  and  maintenance  workers;

i.  free  medical  care;

j.  use  of  the  Presidential  yacht;

k.

free  access  to  the  Library  of  Congress’  research  serv-
ices  for  help  on  speeches,  answering  inquiries  from  citizens,
and  other  matters;

1.  similar  use,  when  desired,  of  employees  of  the  Fed-
eral  Government  from  the  various  departments  and  agen-
cies;

;

m.

a  salary  of  $200,000  per  year;  and

n.

an  expense  account  of  $50,000.

26.

In  the  last  five  years,  the  number  of  House

[19]

and  Senate  employees  has  grown  from  approximately
10,000  to  approximately  17,000.

27.  Many  incumbent  Senators  and  Representatives  have

the  use  of  the  staff  assigned  to  the  committees  on  which
The  use  of  such  staff  personnel  includes  the
they  serve.
time  and  resources  expended  by  those  individuals  to  ac-
quaint  incumbents  with  the  issues  before  the  committees,
the  subject  of  proposed  or  scheduled  hearings,  the  import
of  iegislation  being  considered,  and  the  progress  of  such
legislation  through  the  committee.

28.  The  use  of  such  staff  personnel  may  include  time

and  effort  for  matters  which  may  be  unrelated,  or  only
partially  related,  to  Committee  affairs.

21

29.  Some  incumbent  Senators  and  Representatives  are.
able  to  schedule  hearings  of  such  Committees,  or  of  cer-
tain  of  the  Subcommittees  thereof,  in  their  home  districts
or  States.

30.  The  longer  an  incumbent  remains  in  Congress,  the
more  choice  he  is  likely  to  have  to  serve  on  Committees
dealing  with  subject  matters  of  interest  to  him,  his  district
or  his  State.

[20]

31.  Incumbent  officeholders  enjoy:

a.  name  recognition  in  the  incumbent’s  district  or  State;

b.  the  ability  to  make  news  while  acting  in  an  official
capacity,  such  as  announcing  grant-in-aid  programs  for  their
district  or  State;

c.

the  ability  to  respond  to  inquiries  and  requests  from
constituents,  answering  questions  and  assisting  constituents
as  part  of  their  duties  (“‘casework’’);

d.  being  asked  to  perform  ceremonial  functions  (e.g.,
participation  in  parades  or  dedication  ceremonies)  in  the  in-
cumbent’s  district  or  State  because  of  the  office  held;  and

e.

giving  assistance  to  constituent  visitors  to  Washing-

ton,  seats  in  the  Congressional  galleries,  and  similar  matters.

32.  Incumbent  Presidents  enjoy:

a.  name  recognition  across  the  country;

b.  the  ability  to  make  news  while  acting  in  an  official

capacity;

c.

the  ability  to  obtain  massive  TV  and  radio  coverage

of  a  Presidential  speech  on  short  notice:

d.  wide  discretion  to  affect  national  policy;

e.  wide  discretion  to  travel  around  the  country  for
speeches,  ceremonial  functions,  and  fact-finding  trips;

f.  the  ability  to  confer  honorary  awards,  to  appoint
officers  of  the  United  States,  ambassadors,  and  members
of  the  judiciary;

g.  discretion  in  the  allocation  of  the  resources  available

in  the  Federal  budget;  and

h.  the  ability  to  direct  the  Executive  Branch  of  Govern-

ment,  and  specifically  the  ability  to  determine

[21]

policy  for  Cabinet  level  officers,  including  the  Secretary
of  the  Treasury  and  the  Attorney  General.

33.  Incumbent  officeholders  often  desire  and  are  con-

cerned  about  their  own  reelection.

34.  The  peak  mailing  periods  for  use  of  franked  mail

are  just  prior  to  the  dates  of  primary  and  general  elec-
tions.

35.  The  flow  of  franked  mail  more  than  sextupled

over  the  sixteen-year  period  from  1954  to  1970.

36.

.
In  1973,  Congress  spent  approximately  $23,000,000
in  exercising  the  franking  privilege.  The  budgeted  cost  for
1974  was  approximately  $38,000,000,  an  average  cost  of
$70,000  per  Member  of  Congress.

37.  Incumbents  have  their  years  in  office  and  access  to

22

23

the  frank  to  develop  their  mailing  list.  They  may  also
get  in  the  habit  of  dealing  with  their  electorate  through
the  mail.  Consequently,  during  their  campaigns  they  can
easily  and  comfortably  use  their  mailing  lists  to  make  the
contacts  they  want  to  make.

[22]

38.

In  1972,  out  of  the  thirty-three  Senate  races,

voters  in  seventeen  states  chose  Senators  from  a  party
other  than  the  party  that  carried  their  state  on  the  Presi-
In  sixteen  of  those  states,  Democratic  Sena-
dential  level.
tors  were  elected  as  the  Republican  Presidential  nominee
carried  the  state;  in  one  a  Republican  Senator  was  elected
as  the  Democratic  Presidential  nominee  carried  the  state.

39.

In  1972,  out  of  eighteen  gubernatorial  races,  voters

in  eleven  states  chose  Governors  from  a  party  other  than
the  party  that  carried  their  State  on  the  Presidential  level.
All  eleven  were  Democratic  candidates  elected  as  the  Re-
publican  Presidential  nominee  carried  their  States.

40.

In  1974,  out  of  twenty-five  states  electing  both

Senators  and  Governors,  voters  in  eleven  states  split  their
tickets  to  elect  members  of  different  parties  for  these  two
offices.
If  the  Democratic  candidate  for  the  Senate  from
New  Hampshire  is  declared  the  winner,  the  figure  will  be
twelve  states.

[23]

41.

In  his  1968  Massachusetts  Presidential  primary
campaign,  Eugene  McCarthy  took  in  as  total  receipts
$743,879.
$773,985  (both  figures  through  November  30,  1968.)

His  total  expenditures  in  that  campaign  were

42.  Campaign  officials  of  George  Wallace’s-968  Presi-
dential  campaign  estimated  that  the  organizational  and  le-
gal  efforts  to  get  Wallace  on  the  ballot  in  50  states  cost
$3,000,000.

43.  The  average  cost  per  vote  Eugene  McCarthry  re-
ceived  in  his  1968  New  Hampshire  Presidential  primary
campaign  was  $12.

44.

In  the  New  Hampshire  primary  in  1968,  Eugene

McCarthy  was  running  against  an  incumbent  President  and
was  relatively  unknown  as  compared  to  President  Johnson.
In  the  final  voting,  President  ‘Johnson  won  by just  under
4000  votes  (7.2  percent).

47.  The  voting  age  population  of  the  nineteen  smallest

24

25

states  is  less  than  the  voting  age  population  of  New
York.

48.  The  voting  age  population  of  the  twenty  smallest
states  is  less  than  the  voting  age  population  of  California.

49.  New  York  and  California  have  twice  as  many  voters

as  the  20  smallest  states  combined.

50.  New  York  and  California  together  have  more  elec-

toral  votes  than  the  20  smallest  states  combined.

51.  The  electoral  votes  of  the  11  largest  states

(25)

52.  The  electoral  votes  of  the  20  smallest  states  do  not

constitute  a  majority  of  the  electoral  college.

53.  The  cost  of  communicating  with  voters  by  various
media  differs  among  campaigns  as  well  as  among  election
districts.

57.  Between  November,  1973,  when  Intervening  Defend-
ant  John  Gardner  said  that  at  least  $90,000  was  needed  to
run  an  effective  House  campaign,  and  the  time  of  the  pas-
sage  of  the  FECA  Amendments,  the  Consumer  Price  Index
rose  11.2%.
To  spend  an  amount  of  money  in  October,
1974  equivalent  to  the  $90,000  figure  in  November,  1973,
would  require  spending  $100,800.

$1,000  and  under.

loids,  and  other  materials.

67.

In  the  1972  U.S.  Senate  campaigns,  a  campaign’s

percentage  of  money  from  small  contributions  was  closely
correlated  with  its  spending  for  direct  mail.

68.  Many  campaigns  for  the  U.S.  Senate  in  1972  solic-

ited  volunteer  or  paid  legal  and  accounting  help  to  file

b.  Joan  D.  Aikens.  Mrs.  Aikens  has  been  active  in  Re-
publican  Party  affairs  on  the  local,  state,  and  national  lev-
els  for  fifteen  years.
At  the  time  her  name  was  submit-
ted  by  Senate  Minority  Leader  Scott,  she  was  President
of  the  Pennsylvania  Council  of  Republican  Women.

c.

Neil  O.  Staebler.

Mr.  Staebler  has  been  active  in
Democratic  Party  politics  at  least  since  1948.  He  served
one  term  in  the  House  of  Representatives  (1963-65),  and
was  the  unsuccessful  1964  Democratic  candidate  for  Gov-
ernor  of  Michigan.
He  was  appointed  by  President  Ford.
At  the  time  of  his  appointment,  he  was  a  member  of  In-
tervening  Defendant  Common  Cause.

tic  Representative  from  Rhode  Island  from  1967  to  1975.

75.  Nomination  by  a  national  convention  of  a  political
party  is  neither  a  constitutional  nor  a  legal  requirement  for
an  individual  to  be  a  candidate  for  election  as  President

34

of  the  United  States.
Not  all  candidates  for  President  of
the  United  States  are  nominated  by a national  nominating
convention.

76.  Some  national  nominating  conventions  have  far
fewer  major  or  controversial  issues  to  resolve  than  others.

[37]
77.  Some  national  conventions  are  more  costly  than

others.

78.

Public  funding  of  national  nominating  conventions

does  not  relieve  individual  candidates  for  federal  offices  of
reliance  on  private  financing.

79.  Some  Members  of  Congress  have  more  power  over

legislation  providing  public  financing  than  others.

80.  Incumbent  Presidents  eligible  for  reelection  often

desire  to  be  reelected.

81.  A  potential  applicant  for  matching  grants  —  is  an

incumbent  President  running  for  reelection  in  1976.

82.

Potential  applicants  for  matching  grants  are  candi-

dates  running  against  an  incumbent  President  in  party
primaries,

83.  On  January  1,  1968,  Hubert  Humphrey  was  not  a

candidate  for  President  of  the  United  States.

|  84.  Hubert  Humphrey  was  nominated  by  the  Democra-
tic  Party  for  President  of  the  United  States  in  1968.

[38]

Party  designations  pose  special  problems  in  the  early
years,  when  national  political  parties  in  the  sense  under-

tions  called  Anti-Federalist,  later  Republican  and  eventu-
ally  known  as  Democratic  by  the  1820s.

General  government........-.-  a  .

52

91.

The  number  of states  holding  primary  elections  for

federal  office  has  increased  from  15  to  30  since  1968.
There  has  also  been  an  increase  in  the  number  of local  cau-
cus  meetings  used  to  select  candidates  for  federal  offices
and  delegates  to  national  nominating  conventions;  and  the
expenses  of major  party  regulation  of selection  procedures
for  delegates  to  national  nominating  conventions  has  in-
creased.

wo

92.  $400  million  is  less  than  the  amount  spent  in  1972

by  the  two  largest  commercial  advertisers  in  the  United
States.

It  is  one  half  the  cost  of  one  Trident  submarine.

93.

It has  been  estimated  that  $400,000,000  was  spent
in  1972  for  all  elective  and  party  politics  in  the  United
States  at  all  political  levels,  in  campaigns  for  nomination
This  represents  a  33  percent  increase
and  for  election.
from  1968.
cent  since  1952.

It represents  an  increase  of almost  300  per-

rose  10  percent  between  1962  and  1964,  36  percent  be-
tween  1964  and  1966,  35

95.

In  the  Presidential  races,  spending  rose  40  percent

between  1960  and  1964  and  46  percent  between  1964

and  1968.

96.  Total  budgetary  outlays  for  the  United  States  gov-

ernment  in  fiscal  year  1974  were  $268,400,000,000.
timated  total  outlays  for  1975  are  $313,400,000,000,  and
for  1976  are  $349,400,000,000.
The  “net  impact”  (de-
fined  as  the  net  amount  of  interest  to  be  paid  from  re-
ceipts  or  other  means  of  financing)  in  interest  on  the  na-
tional  debt  for  1974  was  $16,640,000,000.  The  estimated

Es-

53

“net  impact”  for  1975  is  $17,863,000,000  and  for  1976
The  requested  appropriations  for
it  is  $20,014,000,000.
Civil  Works  projects  for  the  Army  Corps  of  Engineers  for
1976  is  $1,946,000,000,  an  increase  of  $240  million  from
fiscal  1975.

97.  Candidates  for  federal  elective  office,  political  com-

mittees,  and  political  parties  employed  private  campaign
contributions  to  finance  their  election  campaigns.  To  the
extent  they  spent  more,  they  also  solicited  and  received
more.

98.

In  1972,  Nixon’s  Finance  Committee  determined  that

it was  not  possible  to  raise  $40  million

[57]
by  local  group  fund  raising  programs  aimed  at  corporation
employees,  associations,  state  committees,  store  front  offices,
and  womens  clubs.  The  1972  Nixon  Reelection  Committee
focused  its  efforts  on  a  fund  raising  effort  directed  to  people
The  1972
that  had  means  to  contribute  substantial  sums.
Nixon  Finance  Committee  received  $100,000  or  more  from
75  individuals.

99.  Parties  may  encounter  difficulties  in  raising  adequate

funds  at  the  proper  time.

100.

90  percent  of  the  money  contributed  to  candi-
dates  for  federal  elective  offices  or  to  political  commit-
tees  and  political  parties  to  finance  campaigns  comes  from
1  percent  of  the  people.

101.  The  following  charts  reflect  Labor  contributions

to  federal  candidates  in  the  1972  elections.

[58]

Top Recipients

Listed  below  are  candidates  for  the  presidency,
Senate  and  House  receiving  the  largest  campaign
financial  support  to  date  in  each  category  from
organized  labor  interests  checked  by  Congressional
Quarterly.  Also  shown  are  total  contributions  reported
by the  groups  for each  candidate.

Business  Political  Contributions
Listed  below  are  campaign  contribution  totals
for  presidential,  Senate  and  House  candidates  based
on  reports  filed  Sept.  10  or  later  by  political  com-
mittees  of  identifiable  business-related  interests.  Not
included  are  groups  overtly  founded  on  political
partisanship  or  on  the  promotion  of  a  particular
party  orc

andidate.
te

Federal  law  prohibits  contributions  by  corpora-
tions  or  labor  unions  but  provides  for  political
activities  by  committees  using  voluntary  donations.
In  many  cases,  business  committees  handle  donations
by  employees.  The  listing  is  not  comprehensive,
although  an  effort  has  been  made  to  include  the  most
active  and  a sampling  of others.

[97]
104.  To  the  extent  that  campaign  contributions  over
a  certain  limit  are  made  illegally,  candidates  will  lessen
their  reliance  on  them.  Thus,  reliance  on  large  contribu-
tions  may  be  replaced  by  extensive  activities  such  as  those
listed  in  finding  of  fact  no.  5, supra.

105.  Under  the  campaign  laws  in  effect  prior  to  the
1974  FECA  Amendments,  some  part  of  the  money  raised
from  the  business  community  for  political  purposes  was
given  in  fear  of  what  would  happen  if it  were  not  given.

106.  A  significant  percentage  of  ihe  revenues  used  to
finance  political  conventions  is  paid  in  the  form  of  adver-
tising  revenue  derived  from  a  small  percentage  of  the  pop-
ulation.

107.

All  varieties  of  political  activity  in  America,  such

as  those  listed  in  finding  of  fact  no.  5, supra,  is  carried
on  by  a  small  percentage  of  the  population.  Only  about
four  or  five  percent  of  the  population  is  active  in  a  party,
a  campaign,  or  attend  political  meetings.  About  10  per-
cent  make  monetary  contributions,  about  13  percent  con-
tact  public  officials,  and  about  15  percent  display  a  but-
ton  or  a  sticker.  Ownership  of  newspapers,  magazines,

[98]

The  Harris  Poll  indicates  that

and  radio  stations  is  also  concentrated  in  a  “small”  per-
centage  of  the  population.
33  percent  of  the  individuals  surveyed  had  ‘contributed
financially  to  a  political  campaign”.
paigned  or  worked  actively  for  a  candidate  for  Congress”
or  “for  a  candidate  for  President.”
eted  or  took  part  in  a  street  demonstration”.

11  percent  had  “pick-

14  percent  had  ‘“‘cam-

108.

Certain  individuals  and  organizations,  by  offering
or  making  large  campaign  contributions,  have  sought  to  in-
fluence  and  have  influenced  the  decisions  of, and  have  sought
and  received  governmental  benefits  or  special  consideration

146

147

ult

from,  candidates  for  federal  elective  office,  federal  office
holders,  and  persons  associated  with  them,  in  return  for
or  as  a  result  of  such  campaign  contributions.

109.

Corporations,  other  organizations  and  individuals

have  made  illegal  campaign  contributions.

110.  Corporations,  other  organizations  and  individuals
have  determined  that  it  furthered  their  business  and  priv-
ate  interests  to  make  legal  and  illegal  campaign  contribu-
tions.

[99]

111.  Corporations,  other  organizations  and  individuals
have  determined  that  it  furthered  their  business  and  private
interests  to  make  legal  and  illegal  campaign  contributions
to  more  than  one  candidate  for  the  same  federal  elective
office  in  the  same  election.

to  18  U.S.C.  Section  610,  the  federal  statute  prohibiting
the  receipt  of  corporate  campaign  contributions.

a.
