118.  Some  business  executives  made  illegal  corporate
camapign  contributions  in  an  attempt  to  assure  access  to
government  Officials  merely  to  advance  their  points  of
view.

119.  The  Goodyear  Tire  and  Rubber  Company  main-
tained  funds  in  a  foreign  bank  account  from  which  con-
tributions  were  made  from  time  to  time  for  political  pur-
poses  in  the  United  States.  The  account  was  initiated  in
1964  and  terminated  in  1970.  Over  the  six  years  of  its
existence,  approximately  $522,000  was  entered  in  the  ac-
Slightly  more  than  half  of  this  amount  was  ulti-
count.
mately  entered  as  income  in  the  books  of  foreign  subsi-
diaries  of  the  company  and  used  by  those  foreign  subsi-
diaries  in  the  ordinary  course  of  their  business.  The  re-
mainder,  $260,000,  was  transferred  to  the  United  States
at  various  times  between  1964  and  1970,  and  was

[107]
used  for  political  contributions.  There  is no  evidence  that  any

153

of  the funds  were  ever  used  for  any  other  purpose  than
political  contributions.

|  120.  Gulf  Oil  Corporation  has  admitted  using  $5.4  mil-
lion  for  political  campaign  contributions  in  the  United
States.  The  Securities  and  Exchange  Commission  has
charged  that  a  substantial  portion  of  this  sum  was  unlaw-
ful.  Gulf  has  entered  into  a  consent  .>cree  under  which
it  agreed  not  to  use  corporate  funds  for  illegal  corporate
contributions  in  the  future.

121.

Herbert  W.  Kalmbach,  formerly  Vice  Chairman  of
the  Finance  Committee  to  Re-Elect  the  President  and  per-
sonal counsel  to  former  President  Nixon,  pleaded  guilty  to
a  violation  of  18  U.S.C.  Section  600,  for  promising  an  am-
bassadorship  to  J.  Fife  Symington,  Jr.  in  return  for  a
$100,000  campaign  contribution.

122.  Kalmbach  testified  that  he  reached  an  agreement
with  Vincent  deRoulet,  then  Ambassador  to  Jamaica,  that
deRoulet  would  receive  a  European  ambassadorship  in  re-
turn  for  a  $100,000  contribution.

123.  Robert  Gray,  of  the  public  relations  firm  of  Hill

&  Knowlton,  testified  concerning  the  interest  in

[108]
an  ambassadorship  of Roy Carver,  who  contributed  $25 7,000  to
the  Finance  Committee  to  Re-Elect  the  President,  as  fol-
lows:

Did  his  [Carver’s]  desire  to  give

“Mr.  Dorsen.
the  largest  contribution  in  the  campaign  have  any-
thing  to  do  with  his  desire  to  become  an  ambas-
sador?

“Mr.  Gray.  With  his  desire  to  be  considered  as
an  ambassador?
Yes,  I am  sure  that,  it  did.  |
can  not  imagine  that  he  would  have  given  those

154

kinds  of  monies  without  that  belief.”

124.

Over  $1.7  million  in  Presidential  campaign  con-
tributions  can  be  attributed  in  whole,  or  in  part,  to  per-
sons  who  held  or  are  holding  ambassadorial  appointments
from  former  President  Nixon,  as  shown  in  the  following
table.

als  a  ls

[110]  °
125.  Disclosure  of  the  sources  and  amounts  of  cam-
paign  contributions  will  deter,  among  others,  contributions
considered  corrupt  by  the  community.

126.  A  substantial  percentage  of  the  illegal  corporate
campaign  contributions  discovered  in  the  1972  election
was made  prior  to  the  effective  date  of  the  disclosue  pro-
visions  of  the  1971  FECA.
.  127.  A substantial  number  of  the  largest  contributions
rom  individuals  and  Organizations  were  contributed  prior

128.  The  “Watergate  break-in,”  and  campaign  ‘dirty
tricks”  undertaken  by  Donald  Segretti,  Anthony  Ulasewicz
and  other  individuals  employed  by  the  Committee  to  Re-
Elect the  President  in  1971  and  1972  were  funded  en-
tirely  by campaign  contributions  made  to  the  Finance
Committee  to  Re-Elect  the  President  and  excluded  from
the  ledgers  of  receipts  and  expenditures  maintained  by
the  Finance  Committee  to  Re-Elect  the  President  prior  to
the  effective  date  of  the  disclosure  provisions  of  the  197]
FECA.  Discovery  of  the  persons  responsible  for  the  Water-
gate break-in  was  facilitated  by  tracing  the  campaign  con-
tributions  used  for  these  purposes.

[111]
129.  Illegal  practices  in  connection  with  federal  elec-
tions’  campaign  financing  practices,  and  governmental  pro-
cesses are more  likely  to  occur  and  will  occur  if the  legal
restrictions  and  requirements  applicable  to  campaign  prac-
tices  are  not  fairly,  vigorously  and  effectively  enforced.

130.  Assistant  Attorney  General  Will  W.  Wilson  wrote
to  Senator  George  Murphy  on  May  22,  1970  the  following:
“The  Federal  Corrupt  Practices  Act  was  passed  in

In  each  election  since  that  time  there  have

1925.
been  allegations  that  various  candidates  for  Congress
and  various  political  committees  failed  to  file  re-
ports  of  campaign  contributions  and  expenditures
or  failed  to  file  them  on  time.
In  only  one  in-
stance  was  a  prosecution  undertaken  by  the  De-
partment  of  Justice.
That  prosecution  occurred
in  the  early  1930’s  and  involved  a  committee  re-
port  in  which  campaign  contributions  which  had
been  made  were  omitted  from  the  report  which
had  been  filed.
brought  charging  absolute  failure  to  file  on  time.
In  view  of  this  record,  fair  play  demands  that  be-
fore  prosecutions  are  undertaken  for  alleged  vio-
lations  of  the  filing  provisions  of  the  Federal  Cor-
rupt  Practices  Act,  the  Department  of  Justice  has
an  obligation  to  notify  all  candidates  and  all  com-
mittees  that  henceforth  violators  will  be  proceeded
against.
It  has  been  concluded,  therefore,  to  take
no  further  action  with  respect  to  the  committees
and  the  individual  congressional  candidates,  who,

Thus,  no  case  has  ever  been

[112]

in  connection  with  the  1968  election,  did  not
file  their  reports  on  time.”

131.

Richard  G.  Kleindienst,  former  Attorney  General

of  the  United  States,  has  testified:

“Mr.  Devine....  During  the  24%  years  you  have
served  as  Deputy  Attorney  General,  has  the  Jus-
tice  Department  or  anyone  under  your  supervision
instituted  any  prosecutions  for  violation  of  the  Cor-
rupt  Practices  Act?

“Mr.  Kleindienst.  We  have  in  one  limited  area,
under  the  banking  laws,  where  there  has  been  a
technical  violation  for  a  bank  to  have  loaned  a

156

1$7

sla  i  are,  eee  ee

candidate  for  Federal  office  money,  and  in  those
cases  we  have  only  proceeded  against  the  bank
and  not  the  individuals.

“The  reasons  why  we  haven’t  initiated  prosecu-

tion,  Mr.  Devine,  is  for  the  reason  that  these
laws,  as  they  exist  and  as  they  have  been  ap-
plied  and  as  they  have  been  enforced  in  the
past,  have  been  inadequately  enforced.  They
are  so  full  of  the  loopholes  that  Congressman
Dent  referred  to,  that  traditionally  Democrats
and  Republicans,  either  administration,  just  have
not  done  it.”

132.

Elliot  Richardson  has  stated  in  an  affidavit  dated

June  17,  1974:

“On  July  3,  1973  General  Haig,  the  President’s

Chief  of  Staff,  called  me  about  a  Los  Angeles
Times  story  that  Mr.  Cox  was  investigating  ex-

I called  Mr.  Cox,  who  said

[113]
penditures  related  to  the  ‘Western  White  House’
at  San  Clemente.
that  he  was  not  investigating  San  Clemente.  Mr.
Cox  explained  that  he  had  asked  his  press  offi-
cer  to  assemble  press  clippings  on  San  Clemente
after  Mr.  Cox  was  questioned  about  San  Cle-
mente  at  a  press  conference.  The  press  officer
requested  clippings  from  the  Los  Angeles  Times,
which  had  carried  most  of  the  articles.  I called
General  Haig  back  and  told  him  this.  He  said
that  I ought  to  get  a  statement  from  Mr.  Cox
saying  that  Mr.  Cox  was  not  investigating  the
matter.
General  Haig  said  that  he  was  not  sure
the  President  was  not  going  to  move  on  this  to
discharge  Mr.  Cox,  and  that  it  could  not  be  a

158

matter  of  Cox’s  charter  to  investigate  the  Presi-
I called  Mr.  Cox,  who
dent  of  the  United  States.
Some  time  after
agreed  to  make  a  statement.
1:00  p.m.  I called  back  General  Haig,  who  said
the  statement  was  inadequate.
The
President  broke  in  on  the  conversation.
President  said  that  he  wanted  a  statement  by
Mr.  Cox  making  it  clear  that  Mr.  Cox  was  not
investigating  San  Clemente,  and  he  wanted  it  by
two  o'clock.

At  this  point  the

General  Haig  told  me  that  ‘if  we

“On  July  23,  1973  General  Haig  called  and
told  me  that  the  ‘boss’  was  very  ‘uptight’  about
Cox  and  complained  about  various  of  his  activi-
ties,  including  letters  to  the  IRS  and  the  Secret
Service  from  the  Special  Prosecutor’s  office  seek-
ing  information  on  guidelines  for  electronic  sur-
veillance.
have  to  have  a  confrontation  we  will  have  it.’
General  Haig  said  that  the  President  wanted  ‘a
tight  line  drawn  with  no  further  mistakes,’  and
that  ‘if  Cox  does  not  agree,  we  will  get  rid  of
In  this  instance  Mr.  Cox  agreed  that  the
Cox.’
requests  for  information  contained  in  the  letters
sent  by  his  office  to  Treasury  Department  agen-
cies  had  been  over-broadly  stated.”

[114]

[129]

134.

Disclosure  of  the  sources  of  some  campaign  con-

tributions  received  by  candidates  for  federal  elective  office,
political  parties,  and  political  committees  would  improve
some  voters’  ability  to  evaluate  such  candidates.

135.

Disclosure  of  the  sources  and  amounts  of  cam-

paign  contributions  will  deter  illegal  contributions,  among

others.

136.

In  the  past,  labor  unions  have  made  independent

expenditures  on  behalf  of  candidates  in  order  to  avoid  the
application  of  18  U.S.C.  Section  610,  the  statute  which
prohibits  contributions  by  labor  unions.

137.

Under  the  Federal  Corrupt  Practices  Act  some

political  campaigns  organized  numerous  committees  which
facilitated  avoidance  of  the  contribution  and  expenditure
limits  imposed  by  the  statute.

138.

Dr.  Martin  Peretz,  listed  by  Common  Cause  as

an  individual  who  contributed  more  than a  total  of
$25,000  to  political  candidates  in  1972,  recently  purchased
the  New  Republic  Magazine  for  $380,000.
Peretz  had  ear-
lier  helped  finance  Ramparts  Magazine  and  the  Students
for  a  Democratic  Society.

[130]

139.  The  Secretary  of  the  Senate,  the  Clerk  of  the
House  of  Representatives,  and  the  Department  of  Justice
have  largely  failed  to  enforce  prior  campaign  financing
practices  legislation.

140.

In  1954,  then  Attorney  General  Herbert  Brown-

ell  wrote  an  Order  addressed  “To  All  United  States  Attor-
neys,  the  Federal  Bureau  of  Investigation,  and  Attorneys
of  the  Criminal  Division,’  which  stated,  in  part:

“The  pertinent  provisions  of  Title  2  (originally
Sections  303  to  309  of  the  Corrupt  Practices

190

Act)  require  the  filing  of  financial  statements  by
political  committees,  candidates  for  the  House  and
Senate  and  individual  contributions  with  the  Clerk
of  the  House  of  Representatives  or  the  Secretary
of  the  Senate.
Although  the  filing  provisions  of
Title  2  carry  a  criminal  penalty,  they  are,  never-
It  is,
theless,  primarily  administrative  in  nature.
therefore,  the  Department’s  policy  not  to  institute
investigations  into  possible  violations  of  the  laws
in  question  in  the  absence  of  a  request  from  the
Clerk  of  the  United  States  House  of  Representa-
tives  or  the  Secretary  of  the  Senate,  whose  in-
terest  in  the  administration  of  these  laws  is  con-
sidered  paramount  to  that  of  the  Department  of
Justice.  The  Federal  Bureau  of Investigation  and
the  United  States  Attorneys  should,  therefore,  re-
fer  all  complaints  involving  possible  failure

[131]

to comply  with  the filing provisions  of these  statutes  di-
rectly  to  the  Criminal  Division  of  the  Department
and  should  take  no  further  action  in  the  absence
of  express  instructions.”

141.  The  Clerk  of  the  House  of  Representatives  does
not  make  referrals  to  the  Department  of  Justice  for  pro-
secution  under  the  Federal  Corrupt  Practices  Act  because
he  believes  that  “neither  of  these  ‘reporting’  sections  nor
any  Other  section  of  the  FCPA  imposes  a  duty  on  the
Clerk  to  take  any  action  other  than  to  receive  the  state-
ments,  preserve  them  for  two  years  and  make  them  open
for  public  inspection.”

142.  On  February  28,  1972,  the  House  adopted  House

Resolution  819  which  created  the  Special  Committee  to
Investigate  Campaign  Expenditures,  1972.
Committee,  which  had  been  used  to  oversee  the  conduct

This  Special

19]

ee

of  House  campaigns  during  each  Congressional  election
year  since  1946,  was  given  new  responsibilities  in  1972
when  a  provision  was  added  requiring  the  Clerk  to  consult
the  Special  Committee  before  taking  any  enforcement  ac-
tion.
took  no  action  during  1972  without  receiving  the  counsel
of  the  Special  Committee.
As  to  instances  involving  late
filing  or  failure  to  file  prescribed  reports,  the

As  a  result  of  the  resolution,  the  Clerk  of  the  House

[132]
Committee  advised  the  Clerk  that,  under  the  language  of the
FECA  of  1971,  he  had  “‘no  choice  but  to  refer  (them)  to  the
Justice  Department,  as  apparent  violations  ..
.”’  Accord-
ingly,  the  Clerk  referred  to  the  Justice  Department  4900
“apparent  violations”  involving  2702  candidates  and  2198
political  committees.
the  Special  Committee  18  cases  within  its  jurisdiction,  12
of  which  had  arisen  as  complaints  filed  with  the  Clerk  in
Most,  though  not  all,  of  these  com-
auditing  filed  reports.
plaints  involved  violations  of  the  FECA  of  1971.
In  only
one  of  the  18  cases  did  the  Committee  refer  the  case  back
to  the  Clerk  for  action.

In  addition,  the  Clerk  referred  to

143.  The  Secretary  of  the  Senate,  as  of  March  20,
1973,  referred  565  apparent  violations  of  the  FECA  of
1971  to  the  Justice  Department  pursuant  to  2  U.S.C.  Sec-
These  involved  19  candidates  and  3  po-
tion  438(d)(1).
litical  committees  for  failure  to  file  reports  and  173  can-
didates  and  369  committees  for  late  filing.  The  Secretary
did  not  undertake  the  audit  of  the  reports  submitted  by
candidates  during  the  1972  reporting  cycle;  audits  were
only  conducted  after  March  20,  1973.

144.  Between  June  1972  and  July  1974,  the  General

Accounting  Office  referred  over  100  violations

to  the  Attorney  General.
of  the  GAO  referrals.

{142}

148.  There  are  no  public  records  disclosing  the  extent

to  which  individual  Senators  or  members  of  the  House
employ  the  frank.

149.

Trust  in  government,  or  political  trust,  as  these
terms  are  used  by  political  scientists  in  the  tables  in  para-
graphs  150-154,  infra,  refer  to  the  basic  evaluative  or  af-
fective  orientation  of  the  public  towards  the  government.
The  converse  of  political  trust  is  political  cynicism  and
alienation.

150.  The  following  chart  depicts  political  trust  and

party  identification  in  the  period  1964-1972.

ee  —

[146]

153.  The  following  report  reflects  the  result  of a  survey

conducted  by  the  Gallup  Poll  in  mid-1973.

[147]

At  Mid-Point  in  1973

PUBLIC’S  MOOD  REVEALS  SERIOUS  LACK  OF
CONFIDENCE  IN  KEY  U.S.  INSTITUTIONS
by  George  Gallup

Copyright  1973,  Field  Enterprises,  Inc.
Republication  in  whole  or  part  strictly  prohibited,  except
with  the  written  consent  of  the  copyright  holders.

All  rights  reserved.

PRINCETON,  N.J.,  June  30  —  The  mood  of  America  at
mid-point  in  1973  is  one  of disillusionment,  revealing  a  seri-
ous  lack  of  confidence  in  key  American  institutions.

For  example,  it  is  doubtful  that  at  any  time  in  this  cen-
tury  politics  has  commanded  so  little  respect,  although  the
American  people  have  never  held  politics  or  politicians  in
particularly  high  esteem.  A  key  contributing  factor  has  un-
doubtedly  been  Watergate.

In  the  latest  Gallup  survey,  two  persons  in  three  express

the  belief  that  there  are  Senators  and  Representatives  in
Congress  who  won  election  dy  using  “unethical  and  illegal
methods  in  their  campaigns.”  In  addition,  one-fifth  of  per-
sons  interviewed  estimate  that  half  or  more  of  the  535
legislators  now  in  Congress  have  attained  office  through
questionable  means.

The  following  questions  were  asked  in  the  latest  nation-

wide  Gallup  survey:

214

“Do  you  believe  there  are  Senators  and  Representatives
now  serving  in  Congress  who  won  election  by  using  unethi-
cal  and  illegal  methods  in  their  campaigns?”

“‘Just  your  best  guess  —  of the  535  present  members  of
Congress,  how  many  got  there  by  using  unethical  or  ille-
gal  campaign  methods?”

Here  are  the  findings  based  on  both  questions:

[148]

PUBLIC’S  ESTIMATE  OF
NUMBER  WHO  USE  UNETHICAL
OR  ILLEGAL  METHODS

SO  ae  eee
One-quarter  to  one-half.  ........  12
One-half  to  three-quarters  ........  Ii
Three-quarters  to  al]
-.........  9
Can’t  say  how  many  ..........  14
Se ee  ee

The  findings  in  the  table  above  are  based  on  in-person

interviews  with  1,552  adults,  18  and  older,  interviewed
during  the  period  June  1-4  in  more  than  300  scientifi-
cally  selected  localities  across  the  nation.

The  public’s  lack  of  confidence  is  also  apparent  in  their
attitudes  regarding  other  key  institutions  in  American  life,
as  revealed  by  a  recent  survey  in  which  eight  institutions
were  rated.

“Big  business”  is  rated  lowest  of  the  eight,  with  only
one  person  in  four  (26  per  cent)  saying  they  have  “a
great  deal”  or  “quite  a  lot”  of  respect  or  confidence  in
big  business.

154.

The  following  reports  reflect  surveys  conducted

by  the  Harris  Poll:

[150]

Sail

Labor  unions  fare  little  better  in  terms  of  winning  re-
spect  among  the  American  people,  with  only  30  per  cent
saying  they  have  “‘a  great  deal”’  or  “quite  a  lot”  of respect

or  confidence  in  business.

Only  “the  church”  and  “the  public  schools”  are  accorded
a  high  confidence  rating  from  a  majority  of  persons  inter-
However,  at  least  one-third  of  survey  respondents
viewed.
express  Only  some  or  little  confidence  in  or  respect  for
these  two  institutions.

Following  is  the  question  asked  in  a  recent  survey  and
the  national  findings,  excluding  the  “no  opinion”  percent-
ages:

“I  am  going  to  read  you a  list  of  institutions  in  Amer-

ican  society.  Would  you  tell  me  how  much  respect  and
confidence  you,  yourself,  have  in  each  one  —  g, great  deal,
quite  a  lot,  some,  or  very  little?”

[171]

156.

Gulf  Oil  Corporation  made  illegal  corporate  con-

tributions  to  the  Nixon,  Jackson  and  Mills  Presidential
campaigns  in  1972.

157.  Time  Oil  Corporation  made  illegal  corporate  con-
tributions  to  the  Nixon  and  Jackson  campaigns  in  1972.

253

158.

The  American  Milk  Producers  Incorporated  and
other  dairy  organizations  attempted  to  gain  access  to  and
influence  governmental  decisions  by  making  large  legal
and  illegal  campaign  contributions.
The  Senate  Select  In-
vestigation  also  produced  evidence  that  the  milk  producers
sought  to  influence  the  course  of  a  Justice  Department
antitrust  investigation  against  AMPI.

159.

In  1972,  one  person  offered  $100,000  to  the  Fi-
nance  Committee  to  Re-Elect  the  President  on  the  condi-
tion  that  he  would  be awarded  certain  government  contracts
on  which  he  had  bid.  The  Finance  Committee  replied
that  it  would  not  accept  the  contribution  that  was  con-
tingent  upon  government  business.

160.

In  1972,  one  person  offered  contributions  to  the
Finance  Committee  to  Re-Elect  the  President  on  the  con-
dition  that  he  receive  FHA  financing.

[172]

161.

In  1972,  the  Finance  Committee  to  Re-Elect  the

President  received  letters  offering  contributions  in  return
for  government  assistance.

162.

Maurice  Stans,  Chairman  of  the  Committee  to
Re-Elect  the  President  has  stated  that  when  in  1972  the
Finance  Committee  to  Re-Elect  the  President  dealt  with
individuals  who  offered  contributions  and  also  intimated
that  they  desired  something  in  return,  such  individuals
left  without  any  further  dealings.

163.

In  1972,  one  person  offered  to  make  a  $100,000
contribution  to  the  Finance  Committee  to  Re-Elect  the
President  only  because  that  person  thought  it  would  as-
sist  his  attempts  to  have  HUD  lift  a  suspension  it  had
imposed  on  him,  and  not  because  that  person  was  inter-
ested  in  assisting  the  Nixon  Re-Election  Campaign.

164.  Howard  Hughes  authorized  large  contributions  to
the  Nixon  campaign  through  an  aide  because  Hughes  thought
it  would  insure  access  to  President  Nixon.

165.  American  Milk  Producers,  Inc.  made  campaign
contributions  during  the  1972  Presidential  campaign  to  the
Nixon  campaign  (approximately  $632,500),  the  Humphrey

[173]

campaign  (approximately  $42,225)  and  the  Mills  campaign
(approximately  $55,600).

166.

Morris  K.  Udall  is  a  member  of  the  United  States
House  >f  Representatives  and  a  declared  candidate  for  the
Democratic  nomination  for  President  of  the  United  States.
In  1974  he  voluntarily  limited  contributions  to  his  Presi-
dential  campaign  to  the  maximum  which  would  be  allow-
able  after  January  1,  1975,  the  effective  date  of  the  Fed-
eral  Election  Campaign  Act  Amendments  of  1974  ($1,000
per  individual  and  $5,000  per  organization).
He  has  been
able  to  raise  sufficient  funds  to  make  preliminary  sound-
ings  in  many  of  the  United  States.
In  the  early  months
of  1975,  Representative  Udall  was  able  to  open  a campaign
headquarters,  assemble  a  paid  political  staff  of  a  dozen
people,  finance  all  of his  current  travel  and  travel  by  staff
(to  over  forty  states  as  of  April  21,  1975)  and  finance
four  major  preliminary  direct-mail  fund  raising  efforts  on
behalf  of  his  Presidential  campaign.

167.

Morris  K.  Udall  states  in  his  affidavit  that  no  seri-
ous  student  of  the  political  process  has  ever  stated  that  an
individual  with  $200,000  available  for  expenditures  would
be  able  to  capture  the  Presidency.

254

255

[174]

168.

Charles  Mathias,  Jr.  is  a  United  States  Senator
He  has  held  elected  public
He  was  elected  as  a  delegate  to  the

from  the  state  of  Maryland.
office  for  16  years.
Maryland  House  of Delegates,  representing  Frederick  County,
In  1960  he  was  elected  to  the  United  States
in  1958.
House  of  Representatives,  and  was  re-elected  to  that  posi-
In  1968,  he  was  elected
tion  in  1962,  1964  and  1966.
to  the  United  States  Senate,  and  he  was  re-elected  in  No-
In  addition  to  his  personal  campaigns
vember  of  1974.
for  these  offices,  he  is  familiar  with  numerous  campaigns
for  other  offices  in  Maryland  and  other  states  and  with
several  campaigns  for  the  office  of  the  President  of  the
He  has  attended  a  very  large  number  of
United  States.
meetings  involving  political  campaigns  and  fund  raisings.

169.

In  the  1974  campaign,  and  in  other  campaigns
in  which  Senator  Mathias  has  been  involved,  the  principal
fund  raising  effort  has  been  designed  to  appeal  to  citizens
The  greatest  effort  has  been  put  where
of  relative  wealth.
the  greatest  return  can  be  achieved,  and  in  the  campaigns
in  which  Senator  Mathias  has  been  involved  that  effort
has  almost  always  centered  on  soliciting  large  contributions.

[175]

170.  Candidates  in  campaigns  in  which  Senator  Mathias
has  been  involved  usually  know  and  remember  the  names
of  individuals  who  make  large  contributions.
In  the  Sen-
ator’s  experience,  federal  office  holders  will  agree  to  meet
with  an  individual  who  has  made a large  contribution  so
the  official  can  hear  the  contributor’s  concerns  and  make
the  contributor  aware  these  concerns  have  been  considered.
Moreover,  on  the  many  matters  which  come  before  elected
officials  that  Senator  Mathias  is  aware  of and  which  do  not
relate  directly  to  a  specific  vote,  the  official  will  often

256

agree  to  meet  with  the  large  contributor,  listen  to  his
views,  and  to  the  extent  feasible,  respond  either  positively
or  negatively.

171.  Some  individuals  who  know  or  suspect  such  spe-
cial  treatment  for  big  contributors  have  told  Senator  Ma-
thias  that  they  feel  the  average  American  has  no  significant
role  in  the  political  process  and  that  they  therefore  do  not
participate.

172.

Senator  Mathias’  1974  campaign  undertook  spe-
cial  fund  raising  appeals  mailings,  distributed  special  enve-
lopes  for  contributions  at  social  or  community  affairs,
provided  speakers  to  address  community  groups  interested
in  the  campaign,  and  organized  special

[176]

door-to-door  efforts.  These  efforts  were  designed  to  make
it  easier  for  the  citizen  of  limited  or  moderate  means  to
participate  in  the  campaign.

173.

Beginning  December  21,  1973,  Senator  Mathias
imposed  the  following  limitations  on  his  1974  Senate  re-
election  campaign:

(a)  He  accepted  no  cash  contributions;

(b)  He  permitted  no  cash  expenditures  by his  campaign

or  by  others  on  his  behalf;

(c)  He  solicited  and  accepted  only  contributions  of

$100  or  less;  and

(d)  He  accepted  contributions  from  groups  or  organi-
zations  only  if such  contributions  represented  donations
of  $100  or  less  from  any  single  individual.

257

174.

Before  Senator  Mathias  announced  these  limita-

tions,  his  campaign  organization  had  raised  $118,745  from
(After  paying  1968  cam-

$25,045,  and  239  contributions  of  more

175.

After  Senator  Mathias  imposed  these  limitations,
his  campaign  organization  raised  approximately  $187,000
from  approximately  6,370  individuals.  No  individual  gave
more  than  $100.  The  average  contribution  was  $29.35.

These  “pooled”  contributions

176.  The  number  and  size  of  the  contributions  to  Sen-
ator  Mathias’  1974  campaign  is substantially  different  than
the  number  and  size  of contributions  to  his  1968  senatorial
In  1968,  his  campaign  organization  raised  a
campaign.
total  of  approximately  $402,000  from  approximately  1,860
Only  5  percent  of  this  amount,  $20,852,
contributors.
was  raised  from  821  contributions  of less  than  $100.  All

These  represented  the  contribu-
35  percent  of  the  total.
tions  of  1697  individuals,  or  91  percent  of all  contributors.
The  remaining  9  percent  of  the  contributors  (including

[178]

totalling  $268,000.  In  the  1974  campaign,  at  least  8500

for  his  1974  campaign  and  100  percent  of  the  $264,000
raised  after  he  imposed  the  $100  limit  on  contributions
from  any  individual.

177.

In  Senator  Mathias’  1974  senatorial  campaign  in
which  he  imposed  a  $100  limit  on  contributions  on  De-
cember  21,  1973,  more  individuals  contributed  to  his  cam-
paign  than  had  contributed  to  his  1968  campaign  for  the
same  Office.

178.

In  his  1974  campaign,  Senator  Mathias  received
letters  from  contributors  which  said  that  they  contributed
because  of  the  $100  limitation  he imposed  on  his campaign.

179.

It  is  difficult  to  state  how  well  an  experiment
similar  to  that  conducted  by  Senator  Mathias  would  fare
if it  were  conducted  at  another  time,  in  another  state,  and
by another  candidate,  for  the  following  reasons:

(a)

In  1974,  the  media  stated  frequently  that

[179]

Senator  Mathias’  re-election  was  a  sure  bet.  This  had  the
effect  of discouraging  citizens  from  becoming  involved  in
his campaign  because  many  felt  he would  win  without  their
participation.

(b)  Senator  Mathias  was  a  well-known  incumbent  who

began  with  a  very  high  rating  in  the  public  pools.

258

259

(c)  Senator  Mathias’  campaign  took  a  number  of  ac-
tions  to  make  it  easier  for  the  average  citizen  to  become
involved  in  and  contribute  to  the  campaign.

180.

Both  in  Senator  Mathias’  1974  senatorial  cam-
paign  and  in  Eugene  McCarthy’s  1968  campaign  for  the
Democratic  Presidential  primary,  many  people  participated
in  political  campaigns  for  the  first  time  by  contributions
either  of  time  or  money.

181.

During  his  1968  campaign  for  the  Presidential

nomination  of  the  Democratic  paity,  Eugene  J.  McCarthy
received  contributions  of  more  than  $100,000  apiece  from
individuals.  Others  contributed  more  than  $50,000  apiece.
Through  the  time  of the  1968  New  Hampshire  Democratic
Presidential  primary,  at  least  24  persons  contributed  more
than  $1,000  to  Senator  McCarthy’s  campaign.  The  con-
tributions  ranged  from

[180]

$1,069  to  $15,000.  The  total  amount  received  from  the
24  large  contributors  who  can  be  identified  was  $95,869.
The  excess  over  $1,000  received  from  these  24  persons
In  addition,  one  couple  loaned  $10,000  to
was  $71,869.
Senator  McCarthy’s  campaign  approximately  1  month  be-
fore  the  New  Hampshire  primary.

182.

In  Ramsey  Clark’s  1974  campaign  for  the  U.S.

Senate  in  New  York,  Clark  imposed  a  limit  of  $100  on
contributions.  From  December  1973  through  March  1974,
Clark  sent  personal  letters  seeking  contributions  of  $100
or  less  to  400  friends  and  acquaintances.  Between  $12,000
In  March  1974,
and  $14,000  was  raised  in  that  fashion.
the  Clark  campaign  spent  approximately  $6,000  on  a  half-
page  advertisement  in  the  New  York  Times  stating  that
Clark  would  run  for  the  Senate  if  he  received  enough

support  from  small  contributors.
The  Clark  campaign  re-
ceived  $17,000  in  response  to  this  advertisement.
The
Clark  campaign  used  this  $17,000  to  initiate  a  direct  mail
campaign.
Beginning  in  1974,  Clark  planned  and  partici-
pated  in  a  series  of  events,  including  small  receptions,
where  Clark  would  speak  on  the  issues  and  solicit  funds.

183.

Direct  mail  solicitations  were  the  major  fund

raising  activities  of  the  Clark  campaign.

The  Clark

[181]

campaign  never  lost  money  on  a  direct  mail  solicitation
effort.
Several  mailings  produced  return  rates  of  5%  to
7%,  although  the  Clark  campaign  had  been  advised  that
it  could  expect  only  a  1-1/2%  to  2%  response  rate  at
best.

184.  The  Clark  campaign  spent  $180,000-$200,000  on
television,  including  several  15-30  minute  programs,  three
5-minute  commercials  and  several  60  second  commercials.
The  television  programs  included  direct  requests  for  con-
tributions,  the  response  to  which  paid  for  these  programs.

185.  From  March  to  November  1974,  the  Clark  cam-
paign  raised  over  $864,000,  $225,000  of  which  was  raised
during  the  primary  campaign.  One  of  Clark’s  opponents,
Senator  Javits,  who  won  the  election,  reported  contribu-
tions  of  over  $1.2  million.

186.  The  average  contribution  to  the  Clark  campaign
The  average
The  .ecords  of

from  all  sources  was  approximately  $20.00.
from  direct  mail  solicitation  was  $12.00.
the  Clark  campaign  indicate  that  fewer  than  1,000  indi-
viduals  contributed  $100.

187.  The  Clark  campaign  began  with  no  money,  no

lists  of  prior  contributions  to  the  candidate,  and

260

[182]

Scores  of  prsons
without  any  advantages  of  incumbency.
told  the  Clark  campaign  that  they  had  never  contributed
to  a  political  campaign  before.

188.  The  Clark  campaign  permitted  persons  to  contrib-
ute  up  to  $100  during  the  primary  and  contribute  up  to
another  $100  after  the  primary.

189.  The  Clark  campaign  started  with  a  relatively  well-

known  person  identified  with  a  number  of  causes.

190.  The  two  principal  handicaps  caused  by  the  $100
limitation  on  campaign  contributions  imposed  by  the  Clark
campaign  were:

(a)  The  inability  to  obtain  money  when  needed.
Starting  was  very  slow  as  the  Clark  campaign  waited
After  the  primary  a  month
for  early  contributions.
passed  before  the  Clark  campaign  could  estimate  even
very  roughly  how  much  money  might  be  available.

(b)  The  cost  of raising  money  greatly  reduced  the
amount  available  for  direct  campaigning.  The  cost  of
raising  large

[183]

contributions  is  much  lower  than  direct  mail  costs
and  limited  contributions  efforts.

191.  Ramsey  Clark  received  38.2%  of the  vote  for  Sen-

ator  in  the  1974  campaign.

192.  Ramsey  Clark  believes  that,  despite  the  problems

caused  by  the  $100  limitation,  he  had  an  adequate  op
portunity  to  communicate  and  campaign  and  that  he  did
run  an  effective  statewide  campaign  in  New  York  in  1974.

193.  The  $100  limitation  was  a  very  popular  issue  in

and  of  itself.

194.

Senator  James  L.  Buckley  is  of  the  opinion  that

the  Clark  campaign  was  not  effective  in  communicating
its  positions  to  the  voters.

195.

During  the  1972  elections  for  state-wide  offices
and  mayoralties  in  the  State  of  Washington,  which  were
conducted  under  a  law  requiring  disclosure  of  campaign
contributions,  there  was  no  reduction  in  the  total  amount
of  funds  collected  by  major  candidates  for  these  offices
and  there  was  a  greater  percentage  of  smaller  contribu-
tions  than  there  had  been  prior  to  the  disclosure  statute.
Further,  there  was  no  reduction  in

[184]

the  amount  of  funds  available  to  the  major  candidates
for  the  mayoralties.
Fewer  companies  gave  $1,000  to
both  sides  in  a  race  than  they  had  prior  to  the  disclosure
Statute.

197.  Most  politicians  agree  that  one-half  of the  campaign

[000001 |

II.

Statistical  Findings  Agreed  to  by  the  Parties

A.  CONWAY  FINDINGS

1.

During  the  period  from  April  7,  1972  through  the
present,  Common  Cause  has  conducted  a  program  to  moni-
tor  campaign  finances  in  the  federal  elections,  with  partic-
ular  emphasis  on  Congressional  races.  Common  Cause  ob-
tained  copies  from  the  Clerk  of the  House  and  Secretary
of  the  Senate  of each  report  filed  between  April  7,  1972
and  February  |, 1975  by  Congressional  candidates  and  by
political  committees  supporting  Congressional  candidates.
Data  extracted  from  these  reports  have  been  computerized.

Common  Cause  conducted  a  project  to  monitor  campaign
finances  in  the  1972  and  1974  federal  elections.  The  Com-
mon  Cause  Campaign  Finance  Monitoring  Project  has  fo-
cused  on  Congressional  races  but  its  activities  also  include
monitoring  campaign  finances  in  Presidential  races.

In  both  1972  and  1974,  the  disclosure  reports  obtained
from  the  Clerk  and  Secretary  covered  close  to  2000  can-
didates,  the  political  committees  supporting  those  candi-
dates  and  some  600  interest  group  and  political  party
committees.

During  the  period  from  April  7,  1972  to  the

[000002 }

present,  the  monitoring  project  has  prepared  and  made
public  a  series  of  reports  on  the  1972  and  1974  cam-
paign  finance  activities  of  House  and  Senate  candidates,
on  the  effects  of party  and  incumbency  on  political  fund-
ing  and  on  th  distribution  of  contributions  to  federal
candidates  by  organized  “‘special”  interest  committees  and
political  party  committees.

Common  Cause  in  1973  released  a  thirteen-volume  se-
ries  setting  forth  Congressional  campaign  finance  data  for
all  major  party  candidates  who  ran  in  the  1972  general
elections  plus  significantly  financed  minor  party  and  in-
dependent  candidates  and  for  interest  group  committees
and  national  political  party  committees  which  supported
Congressional  candidates.  A  similar  series  for  1974  Con-
gressional  cnadidates  probably  will  not  be  ready  until  July
Certain  reports  on  the  1974  election  have
1975  or  later.
been  prepared  and  publicly  released,  and  are  reflected  in
these  findings.

In  both  1972  and  1974,  Congressional  candidates  were
given  the  opportunity  to  review  and  correct  the  campaign
finance  totals  set  forth  for  them  by  the  Monitoring  Proj-
ect,  including  the  total  contributions  and  total  expendi-
iure  figures.

264

265

=

[000003 }

Common  Cause  has  made  public  an  enormous  amount
of  campaign  finance  data  during  the  past  three  years  deal-
ing  with  the  thousands  of  candidates  and  hundreds  of  reg-
istered  interested  groups.  It has  rarely  received  any  requests
for  correction  of  such  campaign  finance  data  following  its
publication.

John  J.  Conway  has  been  Associate  Director  of  the
Common  Cause  Campaign  Finance  Monitoring  Project  since
June  1973,  and  has  been  on  the  project  staff  since  Novem-
ber  1972.

As  a  staff  member  and  Associate  Director  of  the  proj-
ect,  he  has  been  involved  in  the  supervision  of  the  many
volunteers  who  work  on  the  project,  quality  control  of
the  analysis,  and  development  and  design  of  the  computer
program  for  analyzing  the  data  extracted  from  the  reports
filed  with  the  Clerk  of  the  House  and  Secretary  of  the
Senate  under  the  Federal  Election  Campaign  Act  of  1971.
He  has  also  been  involved  in  supervising  the  analysis  of
the  reports,  filed  by  the  Committee  to  Re-elect  the  Pres-
ident  concerning  contributions  and  expenditures  during  the
period  of  January  1,  1971  to  April  6,  1972,  and  in  super-
vising  the

[000004 |

analysis  of  reports  filed  with  the  General  Accounting  Of-
fice  under  the  Federal  Election  Campaign  Act  of  1971  by
1972  Presidential  candidates  and  by  political  committees
supporting  1972  Presidential  candidates.

2.

The  Conway  affidavits  filed  by the  defendants  present
statistical  data  from  a  study  by  the  Common  Cause  Cam-

paign  Finance  Monitoring  Project  of  the  1972  Presidential,

House  and  Senate  elections  and  the  1974  House  and  Sen-
ate  elections.  The  statistical  data  were  extracted  from  the
reports  filed  pursuant  to  the  Federal  Election  Campaign
Act  of  1971  for  the  period  April  7,  1972  through  Feb-
ruary  1,  1975.
The  contributions  and  expenditures  fig
ures  for  the  1972  Presidential,  House,  and  Senate  elections
cover  the  period  from  April  7,  1972,  the  effective  date
of  the  Federal  Election  Campaign  Act  of  1971,  through
December  31,  1972.
The  contributions  and  expenditures
figures  for  the  1974  House  and  Senate  elections  cover  the
period  from  September  1,  1973,  through  December  31,
1974.  The  calculations  and  conclusions  are  based  on  the
following  assumptions  and  definitions:

(a)  Expenditures  are  defined  as  total  campaign
disbursements  less  funds  invested  in  Government
bonds  or  certificates  of  deposit,

[000005 }

and  less  funds  which  have  been  used  to  repay  loans
made  to  the  candidate  or  his  political  committees.
Specifically,  where  loans  are  received  by  the  candi-
date  or  his  political  committees,  the  use  of  the  pro-
ceeds  of  those  loans  is  considered  a  campaign  ex-
penditure,  but  the  repayment  of  that  loan  is  not
considered  a  separate  and  additional  campaign  ex-
penditure.

(b)  On  the  assumption  that  expenditure  figures
for  primary  elections  cannot  be  segregated  from  ex-
penditures  for  general  elections  with  reasonable  ac-
curacy  from  the  reports  filed  pursuant  to  the  Fed-
eral  Election  Campaign  Act  of  1971,  expenditure
figures  for  such  elections  are  not  segregated.

266

267

(h)  Debts  carried  over  from  previous  campaigns  were

not  included  in  the  contribution  or  expenditure  com-
putations.

3.

The  following  list  contains  the  total  contributions
and  expenditures  for  all  major  party  candidates  and  signif-
icant  third  party  or  independent  candidates  for  November
The  winning  candidate  is  listed  first.
1972  House  races.
The  contests  printed  in  italics  are  those  in  which  there
wa:  no  incumbent.
Incumbents  are  indicated  by  an  as-
terisk.
gressional  Quarteriy.
of a  runoff  where  a  runoff  occurred.  Double  asterisks  (**)
indicate  a  runoff.

The  source  of  vote  percentage  figures  is the  Con-

Primary  vote  percentages  are  those

(c)

Transfers  between  committees  exclusively  sup-
porting  a  candidate  or  between  such  committees  and
the  candidate  are  not  treated  as  either  contributions

or  expenditures.

(d)  Contributions  are  defined  as  total  campaign
receipts  less  refunds,  rebates  and  loans  which  have

been  repaid.

[000006 }

(e)

In  determining  the  amounts  of individual  con-
tributions  over  and  above  $1000  per  contribution  in
the  1974  House  and  Senate  elections,  the  following
(i)  loans  (see  paragraph  115,  in-
was  not  included:
fra),  (ii)  cash  on  hand  as  of  September  1,  1973,
since  it  is  not  possible  to  determine  how  much  of
these  funds  represent  contributions  over  $1000,  and
(iii)  contributions  by  candidates  to  their  own  cam-
paigns  since  under  the  Federal  Election  Campaign
Act  of  1971  and  the  1974  Amendments  to  the
Federal  Election  Campaign  Act,  House  candidates
can  contribute  up  to  $25,000  and  Senate  candi-
dates  up  to  $35,000  for  their  own  campaigns.

(f)

In  determining  the  amounts  of  individual
contributions  over  and  above  $1000  per  contribu-
tion  in  the  1972  Presidential  election,  candidate  con-
tributions  to  their  own  campaigns  up  to  the  $50,000
limit  were  not  included,  nor  was  cash  on  hand  as
of  January  1,  1971.

(g)  Contributions  and  expenditures  for

{000007  |

special  elections  were  not  included  in  the  computa-
tions.

[000032]

5.

The  following  list  contains  the  total  campaign  ex-
penditures  for  all  major  party  candidates  and  significantly
financed  minor  party  or  independent  candidates  in  1974
House  races.

On  the  list,  winning  candidates  are  listed  first.

Incum-
bents  are  indicated  by  an  asterisk  (*).  The  races  printed
in  italics  are  those  in  which  there  was  no  incumbent  in
the  general  election.
Candidates  indicated  by  a  double
asterisk  (**)  won  their  primary  after  a  runoff  election.
The  primary  vote  percentage  listed  for  those  candidates
is  for  the  runoff.
Congressional  Quarterly  is  the  source
of  all  data  on  vote  percentages.

The  figures  in  the  column  headed  (A)  are  based  upon
additional  information  regarding  expenditures  acquired  sub-
sequent  to  the  filing  of  the  Conway  affidavit  of  April  21,
1975.

These  additional  figures  are  not  included  in  the  finding

However,  the
in  paragraphs  11-22,  78-84,  97-99,  infra.
inclusion  of  this  additional  information  would  not  alter
any  of the  aggregate  figures  in  the  above-stated  paragraphs
to  a  statistically  significant  extent.

,;

6.

;
The  following  list  contains  the  total  campaign  ex-
penditures  for  all  major  party  candidates  and  significantly
financed  minor  party  or  independent  candidates  in  the
1974  Senate  races.

hake

;

.

,

On  the  list,  winning  candidates  are  listed  first.

Incum-
bents  are  indicated  by  an  asterisk  (*).  The  races  printed
ar
in  italics  are  those  in  which  there  was  no  incumbent  in
the  general  election.
Candidates  indicated  by  a  double
asterisk  (**)  won  their  primary  after  a  runoff  election.
The  primary  vote  percentage  listed  for  those  candidates
is  for  the  runoff.
Congressional  Quarterly  is  the  source
of  all  data  on  vote  percentages.

;

;

The  figures  in  the  column  headed  (A)  are  based  upon
additional  information  regarding  expenditures  acquired  sub
sequent  to  the  filing  of  the  Conway  affidavit  of  Apnil  21.
1975.

These  additional  figures  are  not  included  in  the  find-
ings  in  paragraphs  11-12,  23-34,  78-84,  97-99,  infra
However,  the  inclusion  of  this  additional  information
would  not  alter  any  of  the  aggregate  figures  in the  above-
Stated  paragraphs  to a statistically  significant  extent.

407  Republicans  and  286  minor  party  and  independent

12.

Major  party  candidates  who  ran  in  the  1974  gen-

eral  election  reported  spending  $72,733,053  compared
with  $1,184,204  in  expenditures  reported  by  minor  party
and  independent  candidates.
The  expenditures  reported
by  minor  party  and  independent  candidates  represented
approximately  1.7  percent  of  the  total  spending  reported
by  Congressional  candidates  who  ran  in  the  1974  general
election.

[000089 }

House  Spending — 1974

13.  Some  $45.1  million  was  reported  spent  by  1,056
House  candidates  who  ran  in  the  1974  general  elections.
This  included  $23,697,812  by  434  Democratic  candidates,
$20,608,933  by  376  Republican  candidates  and  $825,478
by  246  minor  party  and  independent  candidates.

44]

14.  There  were  323  House  incumbents  who  ran  in  the
1974  general  election  against  major  party  challengers.  Total
expenditures  reported  by these  incumbents  was  $20,542,838
compared  with  $12,894,695  reported  by  their  major  party
challengers.

15.

There  were  52  House  “aces  in  which  no  incumbent
competed  in  the  general  election.

ported  by  the  52  Democrats  in these  races  were  $5,644,054
compared  with  $4,185,069  spent  by  their  Republican  op-
ponents.

was  unopposed  in  the  general  election  by  a  major  party

Paragraphs  18-22,  41-44  assume  that  House  candi-

18-22  are  based  on  a  comparison  of  the  total  amount

tion  with  the  total  of  $168,000  (the  $84,000  in  the  pri-
mary  and  the  $84,000  in  the  general).

in  races  not  involving  an  incumbent  in  the  general  elec-
tion  (2  of  these  9  candidates  defeated  incumbents  in  the

27.

Paragraphs  28-34,  48-50  assume  that  Senate  candi-
dates  may  spend  the  aggregate  of 8 cents  per  adult  for  the
primary  election  and  12  cents  per  adult  for  the  general
election  plus  an  additional  20  percent  in  the  primary  and
in  the  general  election  for  expenditures  relating  to  fund-
raising  in  each,  combined  for  a  total  of  24  cents  per  adult.

444

28.  Of  the  65  major  party  candidates,  17  had  total  ex-
penditures  greater  than  24  cents  per  adult.
These  17  can-
didates  included  9 incumbents,  4 challengers  to  incumbents
(3  of  whom  were  outspent  by  their  incumbent  opponents),
and  4  candidates  running  in  a  race  with  no  incumbent.
Of  the  17,  8  incumbents  won  and  | lost,  |  challenger  to
an  incumbent  won  and  3  lost,  and  2  candidates  in  open
races  (not  involving  an  incumbent)  won  and 2  lost.

29.  No  minor  party  or  independent  Senate  candidate
The

had  expenditures  higher  than  24  cents  per  adult.
highest  total  reported  spent  by  the  40  minor  party  and  in-

only  other  such  candidate  to  spend  over  $100,000  was

30.  Two  of  the  22  major  party  challengers  to  incum-
bents  won  their  election  in  1974.
Of  these  two,  one,
Governor  Wendell  Ford  (D-Ky.),  spent  more  than  24  cents
per  adult,  while  one,  Gary  Hart  (D-Colo.),  spent  less  than
this  amount.  Ford  outspent  his  incumbent  opponent  while
Hart  was  outspent  by  his  incumbent  opponent.

31.  Major  party  challengers  were  outspent  by  their  in
cumbent  opponents  in  21  of  the  22  races  involving  an  in-
cumbent  and  major  party  challenger.

candidates  for  Congress  who  ran  in  the  1972  general  elec-
This  included  460  Democratic  candidates,  422  Re-
tions.
publicans  and  233  minor  party  and  independent  candidates.

cember  31,  1972.

eral  election  reported  spending  $64,657,811  compared  with

House  Spending — 1972

36.  Some  $39.9  million  was  reported  spent  by  1,009
House  candidates  who  ran  in  the  1972  general  elections.
This  included  $19,773,619  by  427  Democratic  candidates,
$18,880,327  by 389  Republican  candidates  and  $1,230,364
by  193  minor  party  and  independent  candidates.

[000097 |

37.  There  were  321  House  incumbents  who  ran  in  the
1972  general  election  against  major  party  challengers.  Total

447

expenditures  reported  by these  incumbents  was  $16,336,981
compared  with  $10,244,723  reported  by  their  major  party
challengers.

38.  There  were  59  House  races  in  which  no  incumbent

competed  in  the  general  election.
Total  expenditures  re-
ported  by the  59  Democrats  in  these  races  were  $5,520,613
compared  with  $5,017,313  spent  by  their  57  Republican
opponents.

39.

There  were  52  House  races  in  which  the  incumbent

was  unopposed  in  the  general  election  by  a  major  party
challenger.
This  included  44  Democratic  incumbents  and
8  Republican  incumbents.  The  total  expenditures  reported
by  these  52  incumbents  was  $903,342.

general  election,  20  or  2.4  percent  reported  total  expendi-
tures  higher  than  $168,000.

dates  included  6  incumbents,  5  challengers  to  incumbents,
and  9  candidates  in  races  not  involving  an  incumbent  in
the  general  election  (1  of  these  candidates

[000098  |

bent)  won  and 6  lost.

41.  The  highest  total  amount  reported  spent  by  the
193  minor  party  and  independent  candidates  who  ran  in
the  general  election  was  $285,475  by  Allard  Lowenstein
(Lib-NY).
Lowenstein  ran  as  a  Liberal  in  the  general  elec-
tion,  after  losing  in  a  Democratic  primary  and  a  court-
ordered  second  primary  against  a  Democratic  incumbent.
Loweinstein  was  the  only  minor  party  or  independent  House

448

candidate  to  spend  more  than  $168,000.

remaining  193,  minor  party  and  independent  candidates  re-

expenditures  greater  than  $84,000.

Of  the  66  major  party  candidates,  18  had  total

These

[000100]

18  candidates  included  7 incumbents,  2 challengers  to  in-

opponent),  and  9  candidates  running  in  a  race  with  no

Presidential  Spending — 1972

51.

President  Richard  Nixon  reported  spending  a  total
of  55.2  million  in  seeking  re-election  to  the  Presidency  in
1972.
Senator  George  McGovern  reported  spending  a  to-
tal  of  approximately  $38.2  million  in  seeking  election  to
the  Presidency  in  1972.
Minor  party  committees  support-
ing  8  Presidential  candidates  and  3  independent  Presiden-
tial  candidates  reported  spending  a  total  of  $1,005,000.
The  latter  represents  |  percent  of  the  total  spending  re-
ported  by  the  two  major  party  candidates  for  President
Both  of  the  major  party  nominees  for  President
in  1972.
reported  total  expenditures  higher  than  $36,000,000  while
none  of  the  minor  party  or  independent  candidates  spent
The  figure  $36,000,000  is  a  to-
more  than  $36,000,000.
tal  of:  (1)  $10,000,000  permitted  under  the  1974  Amend-
ments  for  Presidential  primary  campaigns;  (2)  $20,000,000
permitted  under  that  statute  for  Presidential  general  elec-
tion  campaigns;  (3)  20  percent  of  both  $10,000,000  and
$20,000,000  for  expenditures  related  to  fundraising.

Congressional  Contributions  from  Individuals  —  1974

52.  The  1,161  candidates  for  Congress  who  ran  in  the
1974  general  elections  reported  contributions  of $73,483,613
for  the  period  September  1,  1973  through

{000102}

December  31,  1974.
An  additional  $3,687  503  was  held
as  cash  on  hand  by these  candidates  on  September  1,  1973
for  an  overall  total  of  $77,171,216  in  funds  available  for
the  1974  elections.
The  cash  on  hand  funds  represent
contributions  received  prior  to  September  |,  1973  and

450

451

for  the  most  part  are  funds  carried  over  from  past  elec-
tions.  Of  the  $77,171,216
in total  funds  available  reported
by  these  candidates,  major  party  candidates  reported  con-
tributions  of  $75,906,609  compared  with  $1,264,607  in
contributions  reported  by  minor  party  and  independent
candidates.

53.

If  during  the  period  September  1,  1973  through
December  31,  1974,  the  excess  of  individual  contributions
over  $1,000  were  subtracted  from  the  total  contributions,

Congressional  Contributions  —  1972

69.

$69,644,378  was  reported  in total  contributions  by
the  1,115  candidates  for  Congress  who  ran  in  the  1972
general  election  (including  cash  on  hand  as  of  April  7,
1972).

70.  Major  party  candidates  who  ran  in  the  1972  Con-
gressional  general  election  reported  total  contributions  of
$68,029,405  for  the  period  April  7,  1972  through  Decem-
ber  31,  1972  (including  cash  on  hand  as  of April  7).  This
compares  with  $1,614,973  in  contributions  reported  by
minor  party  and  independent  candidates.
The  contribu-
tions  reported  by minor  party  and  independent  candidates
represent  2.4  percent  of  the  total  contributions  reported
by  Congressional  candidates  who  ran  in  the  1972  general
election.

456

[0001  10)

Presidential  Contributions  from  Individuals  —  1972

71.

President  Richard  Nixon  reported  raising  total  con-
tributions  of  $60.2  million  with  respect  to  the  1972  Pres-
idential  election  as  of  December  31,  1972  compared  with
$38.7  million,  reported  by  Senator  George  McGovern  for
a  total  of  $98.9  million.
Minor  party  committees  support-
ing  8  minor  party  candidates  for  President  and  3  independ-
ent  candidates  for  President  in  1972  reported  contributions
of  $1,043,000.

72.

If  during  the  period  September  1,  1973  through
December  31,  1975,  the  excess  of  individual  contributions
over  $1,000  were  subtracted  from  the  total  contributions,
the  total  raised  by  Nixon  would  be  $24,470,000  com-
pared  with  $24,540,000  raised  by  McGovern.  Minor  party
committees  supporting  a  1972  Presidential  candidate  and
independent  Presidential  candidates  still  would  have  raised
a  total  of  $977,000  in  individual  contributions.

73.

If  during  the  period  September  1,  1973  through
December  31,  1974,  the  excess  of  individual  contributions
over  $1,000  were  subtracted  from  the  total  contributions,
$33,100,000  in  individual  contributions

[000i 11)

to  Nixon  would  have  been  eliminated,  $13,225,000  in  in-
dividual  contributions  to  McGovern  would  have  been  elim-
inated, and  $66,000  in individual  contributions  to all  minor
party  and  independent  Presidential  candidates  would  have
been  eliminated.
It  would  have  thus  eliminated  55  percent
of  Nixon’s  total  contributions,  34  percent  of  McGovern’s
total  contributions  and  6  percent  of  the  total  contribu-
tions  to  all  minor  party  and  independent  candidates  for
President.

[000113]

1973  through  December  31,  1974.

78.

If contributions  in  excess  of  $5,000  from  registered
special  interest  groups  (i.e.,  registered  political  committees
that  are  not  political  committees  of  political  parties  and
that  are  not  political  committees  exclusively  supporting
one  candidate)  were  eliminated,  this  would  have  eliminated
$923,887  in  contributions  to  these  1,161  candidates  (not
counting  loans).

79.  The  $923,887  given  by  registered  interest  groups
over  a  $5,000  limit  per  candidate  represents  1.3  percent
of  the  $73,483,613  raised  by  general  election  candidates
during  this  period.

a  $5,000  limit  per candidate,  $513,560  went  to  incumbents
compared  with  $167,601  which  went  to  challengers  to  in-
cumbents.  The  remaining  $242,726  went  to  major  party
candidates  in  open  races  not  involving  an  incumbent.  None
of  these  funds  went  to  minor  party  or  independent  candi-
dates.

iod.

93.  During  the  period  from  September  1, 1973  through
December  31,  1974,  H.  Ross  Perot  made  26  contributions
to  1974  Congressional  candidates  in  amounts  of  $500  and
over,  totaling  $75,900.
Twenty-four  of  these  26  dona-
tions  were  made  after  October  24,  1974,  and  thus  were
not  available  as  part  of  the  public  record  prior  to  the
1974  general  elections  (the  final  campaign  disclosure  re-
ports  prior  to  the  November  elections  covered  a  period
ending  on  October  24).  Twenty-five  of  the  26  contribu-
tions  went  to  incumbent  members  of  Congress  (including
2  who  were  defeated  in  the  November  election).
is Chairman  of the  Board  of  Electronic  Data  Systems,  Inc.,
of  Dallas,  one  of  the  largest  suppliers  to  the  government
of  data  processing  for  medicare  and  medicaid  programs.
The  Ways  and  Means  Committee  has  jurisdiction  in  the
House  over  medicare  and  medicaid  programs,  which  are
administered  by  the  Department  of  Health,  Education
and  Welfare.

Perot

94.

Perot  made  contributions  to  12  members  of

462

[0001  18)

the  Ways  and  Means  Committee  during  this  period  totaling
$31,900.  Two  of the  contributions  went  after  the  election
to  members  of  the  Committee  who  were  unopposed  in the
primary  or  general  election,  Omar  Burleson  (D-Tex.),  who
received  $5,000,  and  Joe  Waggoner  (D-La.)  who  received

to  Joe  Karth  (D-Minn.),  $2,500  to  Dan  Rostenkowski  (D-

defeated  in  the  general  election  and  both  received  their

In  addition,  Perot  contributed  $2,500  to  Rep.  James

Jones  (D-Okla.)  and  $500  to  Rep.  James  Martin  (R-NC).
Both  received  their  contributions  on  December  30,  1974,
approximately  2  weeks  after  they  were  named  to  serve
on  the  Ways  &  Means  Committee  in  the  upcoming  Con-
gress.

Appropriations  Subcommittee  for  the  Department  of

[000119]

Health,  Education  and  Welfare.  HEW  has  responsibility
for  administering  medicare  and  medicaid  programs.  Perot
also  contributed  $2,500  each  to  Reps.  John  Young  (D-

Jim  Wright  (D-Tex.),  Bob  Wilson  (R-Cal.)  and  James  Col-

after  the  election.

463

95.  The  Finance  Committee  has jurisdiction  in the  Sen-
ate  over  medicare  and  medicaid  programs.
Perot  gave
$16,000  to  members  of  the  Senate  Finance  Committee
who  were  running  for  re-election,  including  $5,000  each
to  Mike  Gravel  (D-Alaska),  Robert  Packwood  (R-Oreg.)
and  Herman  Talmadge  (D-Ga.),  and  $1,000  to  Russell
Long,  Chairman  of the  Finance  Committee,  who  was  un-
opposed  in  the  general  election.

In  addition,  Perot  gave  $5,000  each  to  Senators  Mil-
ton  Young  (R-ND)  and  Daniel  Inouye  (D-Hawaii),  and
$2,500  to  Richard  Stone  (D-Fla.).
Stone,  the  successful
Florida  candidate  for  Senate,  received  his  contribution  af-
ter  he  won  the  election.

Of  the  $75,900  in  total  contributions  given  by  Perot,

$50,900  went  to  members  of  Congress  associated  with
either  the  Ways  and  Means  Committee  or  the  Finance

Committee.
contributions  were  associated  with  one  of  these  two  com-

96.

October  14,  1974,  registered  interest  groups  (not  includ-
ing  political  parties)  contributed  $8.5  million  directly  to
candidates  in  the  1974  Congressional  elections.

from  agriculture  groups.

99.

During  the  period  from  September  1, 1973  through
October  14,  1974,  the  interest  groups  that  made  the  lar-
gest  amount  of  contributions  to  Congressional  candidates
through  political  action  committees  included:  the  American
Medical  Association,  $1,093,860;  the  AFL-CIO,  $918,284;
the  United  Auto  Workers,  $651,426;  the  maritime-related
unions,  $479,014;  the  International  Association  of  Machin-
ists  and  Aerospace  Workers,  $301,405;  financial  institutions,

of  America,  $215,925;  the  National  Association  of  Manu-
facturers,  $214,800;  and  the  National  Association  of  Real-
tors,  $197,200.

104.

During  the  1974  Congressional  elections,  there

were  120  registered  interest  groups  that  gave  contributions
on  278  occasions  to  two  or  more  candidates  running  for

totalled  $676,897.

gress.

All  but  one  of the  contributions  (a
securities  industry.
$100  donation  to  Rep.  Donald  Brotzman  (R-Colo.))  were

ber  elections  covered  a  period  ending  on  October  24,  1974,

cember  31,  1974,  individuals  affiliated  with

The  computations  of  paragraphs  52-60  and  91-92

based  on  the  additional  assumptions  and  information  are
as  follows:

52.  The  1161  candidates  for  Congress  who  ran  in

the  1974  general  elections  reported  contributions  of
$75,269,291  for  the  period  September],  1973  through
December  31,  1974.  An  additional  $3,687,603  was
held  as  cash  on  hand  by  these  candidates  on  September
1,  1973,  for  an  overall  total  of  $78,965,894  in  funds
available  for  the  1974  elections.
The  cash  on  hand
funds  represent  contributions  received  prior  to  Sep
tember  1,  1973,  and  for  the  most  part  are  funds  car-
ried  over  from  past  elections.
Of  the  $78,956,894  in
total  funds  available  reported  by  these  candidates,
major  party  candidates  reported  contributions  of
$77,677,287  compared  with  $1,279,607  in  contributions

reported  by minor  party  and  independent  candidates.

53.  If during  the  period  September  1, 1973  through
December  31,  1974  the  excess  of  individual  contribu-
tions  in  excess  of  $1,000  per

{000135}

candidate  were  subtracted  from  the  total  raised,  the
total  contributic»  figure  to  these  1161  candidates
would  be  $5,521,469  less.

54.  The  $5,521,469  given  by  individuals  over  and
above  a  $1,000  limit  per  candidate  represents  7.3  per-
cent  if the  $75,269,291  in  contributions  raised  during
the  period  September  1,  1973,  through  December  31,
1974.

436.  The  major  party  challengers  therefore  reported  rais-
ing  35.95  percent  of  the  $56,400,436  total  and  56.13  per-
cent  of  the  funds  raised  by  incumbents.

58.

If  during  the  period  September  1,  1973  through

December  31,  1974,  the  excess  of  individual  contributions
over  $1,000  were  subtracted  from  the  total  contributions,
the  totals  raised  by  incumbents  would  be  $34,300,898
compared  with  $18,958,111  raised  by  major  party  chal-
lengers  to  incumbents  in  the  general  election  for  a  total
of  $53,259,009.

Major  party

[000137]

challengers,  therefore,  would  have  raised  35.60  percent  of
the  $53,259,009  total  and  55.27  percent  of  the  funds
raised  by  incumbents.

59.  Major  party  candidates  who  ran  in  the  general  elec-
tion  reported  contributions  during  the  period  of  September
1,  1973,  through  December  31,  1974,  of  $73,988,684  com-
pared  with  $1,279,607  reported  by  minor  party  and  inde-
pendent  candidates  for  a  total  of  $75,269,291.
party  and  independent  candidates  therefore  reported  rais-
ing  1.70  percent  of  the  $75,269,291  total  and  1.73  per-
cent  of  the  funds  raised  by  major  party  candidates.

Minor

over  $1,000  were  subtracted  from  the  total  contributions,
the  totals  raised  by  major  party  candidates  would  be
$68,562,187  compared  with  $1,185,635  raised  by  minor
party  and  independent  candidates  for  a  total  of  $69,747,-
822.  Minor  party  and  independent  candidates  therefore
would  have  raised  1.70  percent  of  the  $69,747,822  total
and

[000138]

1.73  percent  of  the  funds  raised  by  major  party  candi-
dates.

91.  During  the  period  from  September  1,  1973  through
December  31,  1974,  21  individuals  each  gave  contributions
aggregating  more  than  $25,000  in  amounts  of  $500  or
more,  to  Congressional  candidates  for  a  total  of  $879,665.
This  amount  represents  1.2  percent  of  the  total  contribu-
tions  given  during  that  period.
If  aggregate  contributions
from  an  individual  to  all  candidates  in  excess  of  $25,000
during  this  period  were  eliminated,  this  would  have  elimi
nated  $354,665  in  contributions  from  these  individuals.

92.

If during  the  period  September  1,  1973  through

December  31,  1974,  the  excess  of  individual  contributions
over  $1,000  were  subtracted  from  the  total  contributions,
$675,456  given  by  these  21  contributors  of  more  than
$25,000  each  would  have  been  eliminated.
This  represents
less  than  1 percent  of  the  total  contributions  of  $75,269,-
291  given  during  the  period.  Of  the  $675,465  in  amounts
over  and

[000139]

60.

If  during  the  period  September  1,  1973  through

December  31,  1974,  the  excess  of  individual  contributions

above  $1,000  per  contribution,  $192,650  went  to  incum-
bents  compared  with  $156,942  which  went  to  challengers

476

477

to  incumbents.
over  $1,000  per  candidate  were  eliminated,  about  one  and
one-fourth  as  much  would  be  eliminated  from  the  incum-
bent  totals  as  from  the  totals  for  challengers  to  incumbents.
An  additional  $325,873  was  given  to  candidates  in  open
races.

119.  Analysis  to  Contributors  to  the  Finance  Coim-

mittee  to  Re-Elect  the  President

Attached  is  the  Common  Cause  identification  of  contrib-
utors  disclosed  in  the  report  filed  September  28,  1973  by
The  re-
the  Finance  Committee  to  Re-elect  the  President.
port  was  ordered  filed  by  Judge  Joseph  Waddy  of  the  U.S.
District  Court  for  the  District  of  Columbia  in  Connection

_with  a  Common  Cause  lawsuit.

Pages  2-7

Identification  of  contributors  $40,000  and

above.

Pages  8-17  Identification  of  contributors  $10,000  -

$39,000.

Pages  18-24  Identification  of  contributors  $5,000  -

The  dollar  amount  set  forth  for  each  listed  do-

$9,999.
nor  in  the  right  corner  represents  the  amount  given  for
the  total  reporting  period  (January  1,  1971  -  April  6,
1972).  The  figure  in  the  left  corner  represents  the  amount
given  prior  to  March  10,  1972,  and  already  made  public.
If  there  is  no  figure  in  the  left  hand  corner,  then  the
amount  in  the  right  corner  is  the  total  given  during  the
previously  secret  period  of  March  10,  1971,  to  April  6,
1972.

[000158]

The  letter  “c”  next  to  the  amount  means  it  was  a  cash

contribution.

[000182]

B.  COLE  FINDINGS

1.  Roland  Cole  began  active  work  on  his  dissertation
in  early  1972,  having  taken  a  reading  course  in  the  fall
of  1971  in  preparation  therefor.
He  studied  the  broad
areas  of  campaigns  including  considerable  material  on  elec-
tion  laws,  theories  of  voting,  and  other  related  topics.

In  researching  and  writing  his  dissertation,  Cole  particu-
lar'y  wanted  to  complement  the  complete  and  documented
studies  on  contributions  that  he  understood  Common  Cause
would  produce.

Cole  relied  on  three  basic  sources  of  data  in  researching

literature  in  the  field  that

and  writing  his  dissertation:
would  provide  an  indication  of  what  candidates  might
spend  money  on,  why  they  would  so  spend  it,  and  how
they  had  spent  it  in  past  campaigns;  the  official  reports
of  campaign  spending  in  all  67  major  party  campaigns  for
the  United  States  Senate  in  1972  and  the  coding  thereof
for  computer  analyses;  and  interviews  with  personnel  in
twelve  of  the  1972  campaigns  and  with  observers  and
other  individuals  associated  with  the  field

[000183]

of  campaign  finance.

In  the  process  of  writing  his  dissertation,  Cole  wrote
letter  to  over  200  political  consultants,  received  about
100  replies,  and  continued  correspondence  with  many  of
those  who  replied  about  what  they  did  in  political  cam-
paigns.
over  450  separate  sources.

Also,  in  preparation  for  his  dissertation,  Cole  read

Although  Cole  employed  the  assistance  of  other  persons
in  preparation  for  the  dissertation,  for  such  things  as  cod-
ing,  Cole  at  one  time  or  another  looked  at  every  single
line  of  code  in  the  process  itself  and  every  single  expendi-
ture  report  summary  page.

The  12  campaigns  in

Cole  interviewed  the  campaign  manager  or  someone  of
comparable  stature,  the  campaign  treasurers  in  most,  and
one  candidate  in  the  12  campaigns.
which  he  conducted  these  interviews  were:
Pucinski  and  Percy  campaigns;  the  lowa  Clark  and  Miller
campaigns;  the  Michigan  Kelly  and  Griffin  campaigns;  the
Rhode  Island  Pell  and  Chafee  campaigns,  and  the  Texas
Cole  also  inter-
Sanders  and  Tower  campaigns.
veiwed  personnel  involved  in  the  Biden  (D-Del.)  Senatorial
campaign,  and  the  campaign  manager  of  the  Brooke  (R-
Mass.)  Campaign.

the  Illinois

Tr.  9.

[000184]

In  selecting  the  twelve  particular  campaigns  for  interviews
with  key  personnel,  Cole  sought  a  wide  range  of  campaigns:
(1)  campaigns  which  exceeded  the  expenditure  limits  and
those  which  did  not;  (2)  campaigns  which  won  and  those
which  lost;  (3)  campaigns  which  resulted  in  close  elections
and  campaigns  which  resulted  in  victories  by  substantial
margins;  (4)  campaigns  in  different  regions  of  the  country;
(5)  campaigns  which  appeared  to  have  different  kinds  of
strategies;  (6)  campaigns  whose  candidates  had  differing
personalities;  (7)  campaigns  in  states  with  different  geo-
graphical  and  social  patterns;  and  (8)  campaigns  in  states
of  different  sizes.

Other  factors  entering  the  selection  of  campaigns  for  in-

terviews  with  key  personnel  were  (1)  different  ranges  of

the  amounts  of  money  available  to  the  candidates:  (2)
state  population  size;  (3)  the  media  structure  of  the
states  (the  material  for  which  was  obtained  from  stand-
ard  reference  works);  (4)  whether  or  not  gubernatorial
races  were  being  run  concurrently  with  the  senatorial
races  studied;  (5S)  the  amount  of  information  the  states
did  or  did  not  provide  to  campaigns:  (6)  the  extent  to
which  campaigns  in  states

[000185]

felt  the  need  to  conduct  vigorous  election  day  activities;
(7)  population  distribution;  (8)  differences  in  candidates’
Capacities  as  refelected  in  the  expenditure  reports;  (9)
the  nature  of  the  internal  information  flows  within  cam-
paign  organizations;  (10)  the  extent  to  which  campaigns
paid  for  outside  media  consultants;  (11)  relative  reliance
upon  media  spending;  (12)  the  generation  of  free  media
coverage;  (13)  the  allocations  of  campaign  moneys  among
various  campaign  activities;  (14)  prior  offices  held  by  can-
didates;  (15)  the  extent  to  which  services  were  provided
by  campaign  consultants  and  the  prior  affiliations  in  cam-
paign  activities  of  the  consultants  themselves:  (16)  incum-
bency;  and  (17)  party  affiliations.

Many  of  these  variables  interrelate.

For  example,  incum-

bents  and  challengers  have  different  kinds  of  staffs,  differ-
ent  backgrounds,  and  different  campaign  structures.
political  culture,  population  and  geography  of  a  state,  and
the  diversity  within  it  tend  to  be  associated  with  different
kinds  of  campaign  structure.

The

The  distribution  of  voter  registration  lists  and  electoral

information  by  the  State  of  Massachusetts,

566

567

[000186]

one  of  the  most  generous  in  the  country  in  terms  of
State-provided  services,  did  not  appear  to  make  a  major
The  distribution
difference  in  that  State’s  Senate  race.
of  such  material  appeared  to  have  more  impact  on  local
races  in  the  State.

i

~~

The  Cole  Affidavit  filed  by  plaintiffs  presents  statis-
The  statistical

tical  data  from  the  1972  Senate  elections.
data  were  extracted  from  the  reports  filed  pursuant  to
This  Act
the  Federal  Election  Campaign  Act  of  1971.
took  effect  on  April  7,  1972  and  the  reports  covered
from  April  7,  1972  to  February  1,  1973.
tions  and  conclusions  are  based  on  the  following  assump-
tions  and  definitions:

The  calcula-

(a)  “General  Election  Spending”  includes  total  ex-
penditures,  as  defined  below,  reported  to  the
Secretary  of  the  Senate  as  made  subsequent  to
the  date  of  nomination  (unless  denominated  as  a
primary  expense),  plus  total  expenditures  report-
ed  to  the  Secretary  of  the  Senate  as  made  prior
to  the  date  of  nomination  (unless  denominated  as
a  primary  expense)  where  the  candidate  was

[000187]

unopposed  in  the  primary  or  where  the  candidate
had  no  serious  opposition  in  the  primary  election
(i.e.,  where  the  candidate’s  opponent(s)  received
less  than  10%  of  the  primary  vote).

(b)  Primary  spending  includes  total  expendi-
tures,  as  defined  below,  reported  to  the  Secre-
tary  of  the  Senate  as  made  prior  to  the  date  of

568

the  primary  (or  primary  runoff,  unless  such  spend-
ing  is  considered  general  election  spending  as  de-
fined  in  the  preceding  paragraph).

(c)  The  definitions  of  general  election  spend-
ing  and  primary  spending  in  the  preceding  para-
graphs  are  based  on  the  further  assumption  that
it  is  possible  to  segregate  expenditure  figures  for
such  elections  with  reasonable  accuracy,  but  not
with  certainty,  from  the  reports  filed  pursuant  to
the  Federal  Election  Campaign  Act  of  1971.

(d)  Expenditures  are  defined  as  total  campaign

disbursements  including  funds  which

[000188]

have  been  used  to  repay  loans  made  to  the  can-
didate  or  his  political  committees.
The  use  of
the  proceeds  of those  loans  is  considered  a  cam-
paign  expenditure  and  the  repayment  of  that
loan  is  considered  a  separate  and  additional  cam-
(See  Exhibit  1  to  Cole  Depo-
paign  expenditure.
sition  at  Vol.  II,  pp.  298-299  for  a  tabulation  of
loans  and  loan  repayments  by  1972  Senate  can-
didates. )

(e)  Expenditures  are  further  defined  to  in-
clude  transfers  to  organizations  outside  the  cam-
paign.

(f)  Experditures  are  further  defined  to  exclude
Specifically,  debts  incurred  for  pur-

unpaid  debts.
poses  of  the  campaign,  but  not  yet  paid,  are  not
considered  campaign  expenditures.

(g)  Transfers  between  committees  exclusively

569

supporting  a  candidate  or  between  such  commit-
tees  and  the  candidate  are  not  treated  as  either
contributions  or  expenditures.

certain  amount  of  money  to  run  their  campaigns.
also  stated  that  the  anmfount  of  money  they  wished  to
spend  was  unrelated  to  the  amount  available  to  their  in-
However,  none  of  these  people  who
cumbent  opponent.
Cole  interviewed  knew  what  the  figure  was  that  they
thought  they  needed  to  raise.

individuals  were  not  able  to  set  a  figure  on  the  amount
they  thought  was  necessary  for  their  campaigns  because

the  amount  of  money  that  a  challenger  needs  to  mun  his
