[000193]

8.

In  order  to  determine  whether  or  not  a  candidate

had  gained  widespread  name  recognition,  Cole  relied  on
discussions  with  political  observers  based  on  what  the  ob-
servers  learned  from  polls  and  other  information  obtained
before  and  after  the  elections.

9.

Name  recognition  was  closely  examined  in  the

twelve  campaigns  in  which  key  personnel  were  interviewed
plus  the  Biden  campaign,  the  campaign  of  Brooke’s  oppo-
nent  and  the  Tetzlaff  campaign  in  Kansas.

10.  The  manager  of  the  Pucinski  Illinois  campaign  felt

that  the  candidate  was  fairly  well-known  in  his  Chicago
district  and  somewhat  well-known  in  other  districts  in

Chicago,  but  not  known  at  all  “down  state.”  The  candi-
date’s  name  recognition  was  greater  after  the  campaign
Key  personnel  in  the  campaign  believed  they
than  before.
did  not  have  enough  money  to  make  contact  through  radio
and  newspaper  ads.

11.  Key  personnel  in  the  Pucinski  campaign  stated  that
some  small  unions  or  smaller  locals  of  large  unions  in  Illi-
nois  did  not  contnbute  because  they  believed  they  were
unable  to  handle  the  FECA’s  disclosure  and  accounting  re-
quirements.
These  personnel  also  stated  that  many  of  the
traditionally  Democratic  unions  in

[000194]

Illinois  were  committed  to  Percy  from  the  outset  of  the

campaign.

12.

In  the  1972  Michigan  Senate  race,  candidate  Kelley,

in  the  view  of  his  key  campaign  personnel  and  other  ob-
servers,  had  high  name  recognition  at  the  beginning  of  the
campaign  and  a  very  high  name  recognition  at  its  end.
They  felt  they  did  not  have  enough  money  to  counteract
the  importance  of  the  busing  issue  with  the  issue  of  the
economy.
The  Kelley  campaign  did  not  raise  more  than
the  greater  of  14.4  cents  per  adult  or  $180,000.

13.  In  the  Sanders-Tower  1972  Texas  Senate  race,  Sand-

ers’  campaign  personnel  felt  that  at  the  outset  of  his  cam-
paign  Sanders  was  fairly  well-known  in  northern  Texas  but
lacked  that  degree  of  recognition  elsewhere  in  the  State.
At  the  end  of  his  campaign,  they  felt  that  Sanders  was  on
a  “rising  curve”  and  that  they  had  increased  his  name  rec-
ognition,  but  not  as  much  as  they  desired.

14.

In  the  1972  Senate  campaigns  for  which  Cole  con-

ducted  interviews,  Cole  discovered  that  a  number  of  people

21.  The  67  major  party  Senate  campaigns  in  1972

spent  a  total  of  $2.3  million  for  mailing.

22.

In  the  1972  Senate  campaigns,  television  advertise-
ments  cost  more  than  $6  per  minute  for  each  1,000  vot-
ers  they  reached.

$1,000,000  for  telephone  calls  plus  an  estimated  $100,000
for  telephone  banks  listed  under  non-broadcast  media.

24.

In  1972,  the  67  major  party  Senate  campaigns

spent  $24.5  million  in  the  general  election.

25.  In  1972,  the  10  challengers  who  defeated  incum-
bents  in  general  elections  for  the  House  of  Representatives
spent  an  average  of  $125,521.
spent  an  average  of  $86,075.

31.  The  Griffin  campaign  for  the  U.S.  Senate  in  1972

found  its  mail  drives  for  small  contributions  were  recov-
ering  much  more  than  the  cost  of  the  solicitation  letters,
but  little  more  than  that  cost  plus  the  cost  of  the  letters,
telephone  calls  and  other  efforts  necessary  to  gather  the
required  information  about  those  who  gave  money.

The  Griffin  campaign  made  much  more  extensive  use

of  direct  mail  than  others.
ard  computer  list  that  had  been  built  up  over  the  years,
as  did  other  incumbents,  Griffin  tried  a  series  of  mailings

In  addition  to  using  the  stand-

[000199 |

Incumbents  other  than  Griffin  made  extensive  use  of
direct  mail  solicitations  to  raise  small  contributions  and  re-
covered  much  more  than  all  the  costs  incurred  in  that  ef-
fort,  including  any  costs  incurred  for  letters,  telephone
calls  and  other  efforts  to  gather  the  information  required
by  the  disclosure  statute  then  in  effect.

32.  In  the  1972  campaigns  for  the  Senate,  campaigns
which  spent  more  than  the  greater  of  14.4  cents  per  adult
or  $180,000  received  27.26  percent  of  their  total  contri-
butions  in  gifts  of  less  than  $100.  Campaigns  which  spent
less  than  the  greater  of  14.4  cents  per  adult  or  $180,000
received  19.74  percent  of  their  total  contributions  in  gifts
of  less  than  $100.

33.

In  the  1972  campaigns  for  the  Senate,  campaigns

which  spent  the  greater  of  14.4  cents  per  adult  or  $180,000
received  many  more  small  contributions  than  did  campaigns
that  spent  below  those  amounts.

34.  In  the  1972  U.S.  Senate  campaigns,  money  was
employed  by  many  candidates  to  poll  the  electorate  and
to  research  issues  perceived  as  problems  by  the  electorate.

35.  In  1972,  candidates  for  the  U.S.  Senate  employed
money  to  conduct  voting  registration  drives  and  canvasses

to  get  out  voters.

{000200}

36.  Those  1972  U.S.  Senate  campaigns  that  recruited
and  used  volunteers  spent  money  to  recruit,  supervise  and
supply  them.

37.  Campaigns  also  spend  money  for  telephone  calls

and  letters  used  to  solicit  contributions.

578

579

38.  A  professional  poll  of  the  electorate  in  the  U.S.
Senate  campaigns  in  1972  cost  between  $3,000  and  $25,000.

39.

In  the  1972  U.S.  Senate  races,  volunteers  who

joined  the  campaign  were  supervised  by  paid  staff.
Campaigns  hired  a  total  of  about  2500  people.

44.

The  relation  of  campaign  expenditures  for  cam-

paigns  for  nomination  to  campaign  expenditures  for  the
general  election  varies  widely  from  one  area  of  the  coun-
try  to  anothey,  from  one  candidate  to  another.

580

45.  The  concept  of  marginal  utility,  as  usually  used  by

economists,  refers  to  the  idea  that  an  increment  of  some
thing,  often  money,  differs  in  value  to  the  individual  or
group  receiving  it  depending  upon  how  much  of  that  thing,
often  money,  the  individual  or  group  already  has.
That  is
to  say,  if you  have  a very  little  bit  of  money,  for  almost
all  purposes,  slightly  more

[000202]

would  be  much  more  valuable  to  you  than  would  the
same  amount  be  to  a  person  having  a  lot  of  money.

46.  The  marginal  utility  of  campaign  money  is  not
identical  from  state  to  state  or  from  congressional  district
to  congressional  district.

47.  The  Consumer  Price  Index  is  based  on  the  prices
of  a  wide  variety  of  items  of  which  campaign  items  rep-
Furthermore,  campaign  items  tend
resent  small  subsets.
to  be  those,  historically,  whose  prices  increase  faster
than  the  inflation  rate,  in  particular,  items  such  as  wages
and  other  payments  for  personal  services,  and  advertising
costs  of  various  sorts.

48.

Small  contributions  are  generally  raised  through
mailings  by  the  use  of  lists  obtained  by  purchase,  loan,
or  donation,  of  people  who  the  campaign  thinks  might
be  willing  to  contribute  to  the  candidate  or  the  campaign
for  some  reason.  A  list  of  that  sort  may  be  broken  down
into  types  of  contributors  and  different  letters  may  be
sent  to  the  different  types.
ter  will  be  sent  to  all  the  types.  On  the  basis  of  the
response  to  the  initial  mailing  or  series  of  mailings  the
list  will  be  culled  to  eliminate  therefrom  those  people  as
to  whom  resolicitation  would

Or,  perhaps,  one  general  let-

581

{000203 |

Furthermore,  if  names  of  other  poten-

not  be  fruitful.
tial  contributors  are  requested  from  this  solicitation,  such
names  might  be  added  to  the  list.
In  these  ways  the  list
is  expanded  and  contracted  in  different  directions  for  fu-

ture  resolicitations.

49.  A  very  high  percentage  of  campaigns  that  seek  to

raise  small  contributions  rely  significantly  on  direct  mail
expenditures.

50.

Costs  of  fundraising,  particularly  direct  mail  or
telephone  solicitation  of  a  large  number  of  people  for
small  contributions,  tends  to  average  around  40  percent
of  the  money  raised.

51.

Incidental  out-of-pocket  expenses  connected  with

volunteer  work  and  independent  expenditures  of  other
kinds  which  aid  a  candidate  were  not  included  in  Cole’s
figures  for  expenditures  in  Senate  races  in  1972.
Such  expenditures  occurred  in  1972.

Tr.  395.

52.

In  Cole’s  research  for  his  dissertation,  he  was  told

of  independent  expenditures  in  campaigns  for  incidental
cots  which  were  in  some  cases  over  $500.

53.

In  attempting  to  draw  inferences  from  1972  elec-

tion  figures  as  to  the  effect  of  the  FECA  Amendments,
inflation  should  be  taken  into  account.

(000204 |

54.  The  ease  or  difficulty  with  which  campaign  money

can  be  raised  is  one  of  several  factors  affecting  the  deci-
sion  of  individuals  to  run  for  office.

55.  The  availability  of  money  for  a  campaign  is  a

factor  in  the  decision  of  whether  or  not  a  person  seeks
public  office.

56.  One  mailing  to  a  household  cannot  be  assumed
fully  to  inform  the  voting  members  of  that  household  of
all  campaign  issues.
In  many  cases,  the  voting  members
of  the  household  will  have  independent  interests  and  re-
late  to  different  issues.
For  example,  a  direct  mailing  to
a  husband  may  not  address  issues  of  most  concern  to  his
wife.

57.  A  reduction  in  political  campaign  activity  will  de-

crease  the  amount  of  information  voters  receive  directly
from  candidates.

58.  Candidates  cannot  rely  on  the  media  to  get  to  the
voters  through  news  reports  the  information  that  the  can-
didates  would  like  to  get  to  the  voters.

59.  A  reduction  in  news  generating  campaign  activity

will  tend  to  reduce  the  amount  of  news  coverage

[000205]

of  a  campaign.

60.  Incumbents  have  a  better  opportunity  than  chal-
lengers  to  engage  in  activities  which  generate  news  cover-
age  which  does  not  cost  money.

61.

It  is  possible  that  significant  amounts  of  expendi-

tures  in  Senate  races  in  1972  were  made  prior  to  April  7,
1972.

62.

Items  which  might  be  major  costs  in  a  campaign

would  include  wages,  television,  mailings,  newspaper  adver-
tising,  consulting  fees,  computer  processing,  and  salaries
for  technical  services  such  as  accountants  and  lawyers.

582

583

Campaigns  differ  in  the  percentage  of  resources  allo-
cated  to  various  purposes.
involve  personal  services  or  effort,  both  of  which  have  risen
in  cost  faster  than  in  the  Consumer  Price  Index.

Most  of  these  costs,  however,

63.

Direct  mail  specialists  indicate  that  the  rate  of

return  on  mailing  lists  of  incumbent  senators  was  in  many
cases  greater  than  the  rate  of  return  on  lists  the  specialists
could  devise  for  challengers.

64.  Numerous  factors  affect  the  level  of  spending  in

any  campaign,  including  resources  available

[000206 |

to  candidates,  population,  media  structure  in  election  dis-
tricts,  campaigns  for  other  offices  occurring  at  the  same
time,  state  services,  campaign  structure,  information  flow,
media  contracts,  campaign  strategy,  paid  and  free  media,
background  of  candidate,  political  popularity  of  candidate,
use  Of  campaign  consultants,  and  incumbency.

65.  The  amounts  each  campaign  expended  for  par-

ticular  campaign  activities  such  as  mailings,  phone  use,
televison,  etc.,  varied  widely.

66.

In  1972,  14  of  the  Senatorial  candidates  who

exceeded  14.4¢  per  adult  or  $180,000  in  the  general  elec-
tion  were  incumbents;  13  were  non-incumbents  (i.e.,  there
was  no  incumbent  in  the  race);  and  5  were  challengers  to
incumbents.

67.  The  fact  that  a  candidate  in  1972  spent  more
than  his  opponent  and/or  more  than  the  limit  which  the
1974  laws  would  impose  was  only  one  Of  a  number  of
factors  which  had  an  effect  on  the  outcome  of  the  elec-
tion.

68.  Many  challengers  do  not  raise  private

[000207]

contributions  to  the  extent  incumbents  do,  and  are  there-
by  severely  disadvantaged.

69.  Challengers  vary  widely  in  the  nature  and  extent

of  the  resources  with  which  they  commence  a  potential
campaign,  such  as  political  appeal  or  name  -  recognition.

70.

Cole  does  not  know  how  many  full-time  volun-

teers  worked  in  any  of  the  1972  Senatorial  elections.

71.  Many  campaigns  felt  that  disclosure  did  not

lower  the  amount  they  could  raise.

72.

More  research  is  needed  before  the  complete
answer  to  the  relationship  between  money  and  votes  is
known.

73.  Much  more  research  is  needed  before  one  can
know  the  extent  of  the  relationship  between  votes  and
expenditures,  how  much  it  varies  from  campaign  to  cam-
paign,  and  whether  there  is  any  causal  relationship  between
the  two  factors.
ing  statement:
paign  win  most  of  the  time,  its  share  of  the  votes  cast
is  closely  correlated  with  the  share  of  the

These  reservations  apply  to  the  follow-
‘“‘Not  only  does  the  higher  spending  cam-

dollars  spent.”

[000208 }

74.  Many  factors  other  than  and  including  the  level

of  spending  may  be  important  in  determining  the  outcome
of  all  elections.

75.

In  many  races  in  which  an  incumbent  is  running

agairst  a  challenger  who  does  not  present  opposition

584

585

aaa  ieee  aie,  oie  ieee  ei  iain

2.  Attachment  B  to  the  Answers  to  Plaintiffs’  Inter-

rogatories  filed  by  the  intervening  defendants  Common
Cause  and  John  Gardner  in  this  court  is  hereby  adopted
and  incorporated  by  reference  as  Exhibit  B.  Attachment
B  includes  a  table  of  Expenditures  for  All  November,
1974  Senate  Races,  and  a  table  of  Expenditures  for  All
“No.”  on  that  document
November,  1974  House  Races.
refers  to  the  number  of  contributions  in  excess  of  $1,000.
“Overage”  refers  to  the  total  amount  of  contributions  in
excess  of  $1,000.

3.  The  following  is  a  copy  of  the  summary  page

of  the  financial  report  filed  by  the  1974  Gary  Hart  Sen-
ate  Campaign  Committee,  Inc.,  as  required  by  the  FECA:

neither  candidate  spends  very  much  money.

76.

No  one  connected  with  the  14  campaigns  Cole
studied  most  closely  told  Cole  that  he  knew  of  a  poten-
tial  contributor  who  did  not  contribute  because  he  feared
disclosure  would  lead  to  reprisals  against  him  other  than
in  connection  with  intra-family  disputes.

77.  One  of  the  factors  determining  how  much  a
candidate  can  raise  is  the  voters’  perception  of  whether
he  can  wage  a  credible  campaign.

78.

Profitable  mailing  lists  have  been  developed  for

candidates  who  are  not  incumbents  for  use  in  direct  mail

solicitation  efforts.

79.

If  money  is  precluded  from  entering  the  elective
process  in  the  form  of  campaign  contributions,  individuals
and  organizations  will  find  other  ways  to  use

it  in  that  process.

[000209 }

80.

In  1972,  many  campaigns  were  able  to  raise  sub-

stantial  sums  by  asking  for  small  amounts  of  money  by
mail  or  telephone.

[000210]

and  incorporated  herein  by  reference  as  Exhibit  A.  Attach-

1974  Senate  Races,  and  a  table  of  Expenditures  for  all
November,  1974  House  Races.

[000249]

6.  The  computer  print-out,  supplied  by  Common
Cause,  reflecting  individual  contributions  (including  loans)
in  excess  of  $500  by  candidates  for  the  1974  Congress-
ional  campaigns,  in  response  to  plaintiffs’  request  for
production  of  documents  is  hereby  adopted  and  incorpor-
ated  herein  by  reference  as  Exhibit  C.  This  Exhibit  will
be  furnished  to  this  court  by  agreement  with  defendants
and  intervening  defendants  at  a  later  date.
and  intervening  defendants  have  requested  that  this  docu-
ment  remain  under  seal;  plaintiffs  have  no  present  objec-
tion  to  this  request.

Defendants

lengers  had  $68,051,  as  of  September  1,  1973.

of  $1,000  per  individual  contribution  not  including  cash
on  hand.

Challengers  received  $181,253.

tion  not  including  cash  on  hand.

L.  BUCKLEY
JAMES

699

1. James  L.  Buckley  is  a  United  States  citizen  and  a

citizen  of  the  state  of  New  York*.
He  is  registered  to
vote  in  New  York  City  and  maintains  a  residence  there.
He  is  a  federal  taxpayer,  and  he  is  eligible  to  vote  in
elections  for  the  office  of  President  of  the  United  States.
Affidavit  41.

2.  In  1968  James  Buckley  chose  to  run  for  the  US.
Senate  to  provide  the  voters  a  meaningful  opportunity  to
choose  between  differing  philosophical  and  practical  ap-
proaches  to  government.
Senator  Buckley  chose  to  run
knowing  he  could  not  win  and  that  he  could  not  raise
substantial  contributions  because  he  was  seen  as  not
“viable”  and  unlikely  to  receive  a  large  percentage  of
the  vote.

Affidavit  995,  8.

3.  In  1968,  Senator  Buckley  accepted  the  Conserva-

He  had  never  run  for

tive  Party’s  Senate  nomination.
public  office  before,  but  he  was  interested  in  politics
and  the  issues  of  concern  to  New  York  voters  that  year.
He  was  also  convinced  that  the  people  of  New  York
would  not  have  a  meaningful  opportunity  to  choose
between  differing  philosophical  and  practical

*  Although  not  formally  qualified  as  such,  by  reason  of  Senator
Buckley’s  experience  as  a  campaigner  and  as  a  Member  of Con-
gress,  the  Court  has  treated  him  as  an  expert  and  permitted  his
opinion  testimony.

[002]
approaches  to  government  unless  the  Conservative  Party
fielded  a  camdidate.
Affidavit  45.

4.  Senator  Buckley  was  elected  to  the  United  States

Senate  on  November  3,  1970  and  has  served  as  New
Although  he
York’s  junior  Senator  since  January,  1971.
was  enrolled  as  a  Republican  at  the  time,  he  ran  in  1970
as  the  candidate  of  the  Conservative  Party  of  the  State
of  New  York.  On  his  arrival  in  Washington  he  asked
for  and  received  permission  to  join  the  Republican  Con-

700

ference  in  the  Senate.
six-year  term  next  year.

Affidavit  42.

He  proposes  to  run  for  a  second

5. Senator  Buckley  studied  the  Senate  version  of

the  FECA  Amendments  with  an  eye  toward  its  possible
impact  on  Senate  races  in  states  like  New  York.
Affi-
davit  43.

6. Senator  Buckley  has  stated  that  substantial  con-

tributions  are  not  forthcoming  to  a  campaign  unlikely  to
It  was  not  lack  of  funds  that  caused  a  low  per-
succeed.
centage  of  the  vote  for  James  Buckley  in  his  1968  race
for  the  Senate;  rather  it  was  the  widespread  perception
that  he  was  going  to  receive  a  low  percentage  of  the
vote  that  caused  the  lack  of  funds.

Affidavit  48.

7. The  Republican  Party’s  candidate  was  Jacob  Javits

and  the  Democratic  candidate  was  Paul  O’Dwyer.  By
accepting  the  Conservative  nomination,  Senator  Buckley
was  able  to  present  the  voters  with  a  clear  alternative  to
the  views  represented  by  his  opponents.

Affidavit  46.

8. Senator  Buckley  knew  he  could  not  win  in  1968.
He  nevertheless  ran  so  that  the  Conservative  Party  could

[003]

register,  to  a  degree,  the  extent  of  voter  support  for  its
views  and  thus  could  influence  the  major  parties.
received  more  than  14  percent  of  the  vote.
Tr.  10.

He
Affidavit  47;

9. Senator  Buckley  knew  he  could  not  win  in  1968
because  he  was  not  a  “viable”  candidate,  because  he  was
unknown  and  no  one  felt  a  candidate  of  a  third  party,
with  its  limited  resources,  could  win.

Affidavit  48.

10.  After  the  1968  election,  Senator  Buckley  never

intended  to  run  for  office  again.

Affidavit  410.

11.  In  1970  Senator  Buckley  set  three  conditions

on  his  entry  into  the  Senate  race:  (1)  that  a  poll  would
show  that  he  could  conceivably  win;  (2)  that  he  could
raise  $100,000  in  three  weeks’  time  as  a  test  of  whether

on
eee
Vee eee nee

701

he  could  raise  sufficient  money  to  run  a  credible  race;
and  (3)  that  he  would  be  taken  seriously  by  the  press.
Affidavit

411;  Tr.  8.

12.  In  1970  James  Buckley  received  contributions

from  a  “handful  of  people”  in  amounts  totaling  $10,000
That  money  was  used  to  finance  a  survey
to  $20,000.
in  Spring  1970  which  convinced  Buckley  that  he  could
plausibly  enter  the  race  for  Senate  if he  decided  he  had
Tr.  6-7;  Affidavit  4 12.
the  financial  support  to  do  so.
13.  Senator  Buckley  faced  the  need  to  assure  him-
self  of  the  adequate  early  financing  that  he  and  his  ad-
visors  felt  was  essential  to  launching  a  successful  cam-
paign.

Affidavit  414.

[004]

14.  At  this  point  a  friend,  Joseph  Koopman,

He  told  Senator  Buckley  and  his

offered  to  lend  the  Conservative  Party  $50,000  earmarked
for  his  campaign.
advisors  that  while  he  could  afford  to  lend  them  the
money  he  could  not  simply  give  it  to  the  campaign.
Senator  Buckley  decided  to  take  the  loan,  knowing  that
they  could  use  the  money  both  to  launch  Senator
Buckley’s  candidacy  and  to  generate  the  funds  to  repay
it.
915;  Tr.  6-7.  That  money  allowed  him
to  hire  initial  staff,  rent  a  campaign  headquarters,  and
to  utilize  a  direct  mail  approach  to  raising  more  money
and  support.

Affidavit  4 16.

Affidavit

15.  Senator  Buckley  believes,  on  the  basis  of  his

experience,  that  the  challenged  legislation,  among  other
things,  will  force  political  candidates  to  depend  on  large
numbers  of  small  contributors  for  support  by  depriving
them  of  the  ability  to  solicit  the  larger  contributions
that  are  especially  important  in  launching  the  campaign
of  a  relatively  unknown  independent.
Tr.  10-11.

Affidavit  418;

|

7

702  -

16.  On  the  basis  of  his  political  expertise,  Senator
Buckley  is  of  the  opinion  that  the  dynamics  of  political
campaigning  and  fund-raising  are  such  that  the  ability  to
raise  significant  amounts  of  “up  front”  money  is  often
crucial  to  the  initial  momentum  of  a  candidate’s  efforts,
and  that  small  contributions  do  not  ordinarily  materialize
at  the  beginning  of  a  campaign,  but  come  in  only  after
the  candidate  and  his  managers  have  been  able  to  attract
public  attention

[005]

to  his  effort  or  to  finance  mailings.
—  money  that  Senator  Buckley  believes  the  challenged
legislation  makes  it  difficult  if  not  impossible  to  raise.
Affidavit  419.

This  requires  money

17.  Most  of  the  $50,000  Koopman  loan  went  into
a  direct  mail  campaign.
The  direct  mail  campaign  raised
$650,000  of  the  more  than  $1,800,000  raised  that  year.
Eventually  more  than  40,000  persons  contributed  to  the
campaign.

Affidavit  420;  Tr.  1416.

18.  More  than  35  percent  of  those  who  gave  to

Senator  Buckley  in  1970  contributed  less  than  $20.
Weil’s  list  of  contributions  shows  that  180  individuals
(including  a  very  few  organizations)  gave  $1,000  or  more.
Affidavit  421;  Weil  Tr.  10.  Both  large  and  small  contri-
butions  were  greater  in  Senator  Buckley’s  1970  campaign
than  in  his  1968  campaign.
Tr.  21-23.

Tr.  10;  Leon  Weil  Deposition

initial  ability  to  secure  the  money  it  needed  to  conduct
a  direct  mail  campaign.

32.  Marvin  Liebman  is  in  the  political  fund-raising

The

business  and  is  a  specialist  in  direct  mail  solicitation.
Friends  of  Jim  Buckley  has  retained  Marvin  Liebman  to

per  month  while  the  relationship  continues,  terminable  at
any  time.

became  an  established  political  party  under  the  election

laws  of  New  York

has  nominated  in  the  past  and  intends  to  nominate  in

States,  United  States  Senator  from  the  State  of  New  York,
and  Members  of  the  United  States  House  of  Representatives
from  all  or  nearly  all  of  the  Congressional  Districts  of  the
State  of  New  York.  The  Party  will  seek  to  place  its  can-
didate  for  President  only  on  the  ballot  in  New  York.

5.  The  Conservative  Party  believes  it  is  essential  for

“seed  money”  to  finance  direct-mail  fund-raising  appeals
to  small  contributors,  to  open  and  operate  campaign  head-
.quarters,  to  make  initial  funding  commitments  to  advertising
agencies,  and  to  buy  media  time  before  it  is  preempted
by  political  opponents.

approximately  $190,000  primarily  in  small  contributions.

as  provided  by  the  Viguerie  Organization.
did  not  demand  payment  in  advance,  but  collected  their

group  to  put  money  up  front.

used  the  Viguerie  Organization  in  1973  and  1974,  but

8.  In  1962,  the  Conservative  Party  circulated  petitions

to  establish  the  party  as  a  legal  entity  in  the  State.
During  that  effort,  there  was  a  campaign  of  harassment
against  people  signing  that  petition  during  which  individuals
in  various  departments  of  the  state  government  participated
by  talking  to  people  in  regulated  businesses  who  had
signed  the  petition.  Two  major  newspapers  in  the  state
ran  editorials  deploring  this  practice  and,  as  a  result  of
these  articles,  the  campaign  of  harassment  against  the
Conservative  Party  was  dropped.

Tr.  11-12.

3.  In  1967-68  Senator  McCarthy  challenged  an

incumbent  President  of  the  United  States  for  the
presidential  nomination  of  the  Democratic  party.
Senator  McCarthy  was  then  an  incumbent  Senator.
major  issue  in  that  contest  was  the  war  in  Vietnam,

McCarthy  opposed.

The

*  Although  not  formally  qualified  as  such,  by  reason  of  Senator
McCarthy’s  experience  as  a  campaigner  and  as  a  former  Member
of  Congress,  the  Court  has  treated  him  as  an  expert  and  permitted
his  opinion  testimony.

(018)

Upon  the  basis  of  his  political  expertise,  Senator

McCarthy  expressed  the  opinion  that  an  incumbent
President  has  significant  advantages  over  a  challenger  in
that  the  incumbent  exercises  control  over  his  party’s
national  committee;  the  incumbent  can  call  upon  the
Vice  President  and  members  of  his  cabinet  to  campaign
for  him;  the  incumbent  has  easier  access  to  radio  and
television  time;  and  is  able  to  use  government  programs

15.  Political  parties  use  national  nominating  con-
ventions  for  numerous  publicity  purposes  quite  apart
from  nominating  Presidential  candidates.
concern  with  favorable  publicity  explains  the  prime-TV
time  convention  sessions  and  the  seemingly  endless  parade
of  speakers  being  “shown”  to  the  public.  The  height  of
publicity-seeking  is,  of  course,  when  an  incumbent  Presi-
dent  is  to  be  renominated.
In  1964  President  Johnson,
and  in  1972  President  Nixon,  successfully  used  the  con-
ventions  in  their  reelection  efforts.

Constitutional  Presidency  does  not  intend  to  hold  a

(024)

MARY  MONROE
1.  Mary  Monroe  is  the  Treasurer  of  the  Committee
for  a  Constitutional  Presidency  —  McCarthy  °76  (herein-
after  “The  Committee.’’)

2.  The  Committee  for  a  Constitutional  Presidency  was

established  in  July,  1974.
At  a  Chicago  conference  in
August  of  1974,  the  Committee  announced  its  intention
to  field  an  Independent  ticket  for  President  and  Vice
President  of  the  Lnited  States  in  the  1976  election.
In
January,  1975  the  Committee  asked  Eugene  J.  McCarthy
to  be  its  presidential  candidate.
He  accepted;  the  Com-
mittee  then  became  his  principal  campaign  committee
and  added  “McCarthy  °76”  to  its  name.  The  Committee’s
vice  presidential  candidate  will  be  selected  by  the  spring
of  1976.

Affidavit  4 2.

3.  The  Committee  for  a  Constitutional  Presidency  —

McCarthy  °76  is  a  registered  political  committee  under
the  FECA,  and  has  filed  reports  of  receipts  and  expendi-
tures  for  the  period  from  July  1974  to  the  present.

4.  The  Committee  has  been  informed  by  officials  of

Idaho,  Kansas,  Michigan,  Nebraska,  and  Utah  that  their
states  do  not  permit  Independent  candidates  for  President
to  appear  on  the  ballot  in  the  general  election  or  that
there  are  no  provisions  in  their  laws  for  placing  Indepen-
dent  candidates  for  President  on  the  ballot.
The  Com-
mittee  expects  to  challenge  such  laws  on  constitutional
grounds.
The  Committee  may  also  challenge  the  ballot
placement  laws  of  several  other  states.
expense  that  such  legal  challenges  involve  are  problems

The  time  and

719

7418

—

unique  to  candidates  running  outside  the  Democratic  and
Republican  parties.

Affidavit  4 4.

[025]

5.  The  Committee  for  a  Constitutional  Presidency  —

McCarthy  °76  raised  $38,559  prior  to  January  1,  1975.
Tr.  14.

who  would  vote  to  extricate  the  U.S.  from  Vietnam.

about  Vietnam.
On  a  Friday  before  the  Monday  broad-
cast  date,  he  asked  Stewart  R.  Mott  to  lend  $35,000  to

on.

14.  During  February  and  March  of  1972,  Mott
undertook  to  find  out  where  Edmund  Muskie’s  campaign
money  came  from.  The  project,  which  cost  him  some
$40,000  was  not  authorized  by  the  Muskie  campaign.
In  order  to  acquaint  the  public  and  the  press  with  his
findings,  Stewart  R.  Mott  placed  advertisements  in
dozens  of  newspapers  in  various  parts  of  the  country,
asking  Muskie  to  make  full  disclosure  of  his  campaign
finances  and  asking  him  the  question:  “Where  does  your
money  come  from?”  Affidavit  § 21.

Tr.  7.
In  comparing  the  costs  of  campaigning  among
Congressional  districts,  the  factors  to  consider  are  how
a  candidate,  in  a  particular  kind  of  district,  tries  to
reach  the  constituency,  what  the  resources  are  that  are
available  for  campaigning,  and  the  different  costs  involved.
Congressman  Steiger,  however,  has  not  analyzed  or  sur-
veyed  the  costs,  over  time,  of  campaigns  for  federal
office  in  various  congressional  districts.  Congressman
Steiger  believes  that  historical  analysis  does  not  meet
the  issues.

ate  of  the  American  Civil  Liberties  Union  (hereinafter

under  the  laws  of  New  York.

Affidavit  41.

2.  The  NYCLU  is  a  non-partisan  organization,
dedicated  to  defending  the  rights  guaranteed  to  persons
under  the  Constitution  of  the  United  States  and  having
as  a  principal  function  the  dissemination  of  information
concerning  such  rights  within  the  State  of  New  York.
Affidavit  4 2.

3.  As  the  New  York  State  affiliate  of  the  ACLU,
the  NYCLU  is  barred  by  the  ACLU  Constitution  and
policies  from  endorsing  or  opposing  any  candidate  for

732

733

public  office.  NYCLU  is,  however,  allowed  to  criticize
public  officials  whose  conduct  violates  civil  liberties  and
to  publicize  the  civil  liberties  record  of  any  government
official.

Affidavit  § 2.

LAURA  WERTHEIMER

1.  In  1972,  Laura  Wertheimer  managed  the  congres-
sional  campaign  of  Avi  Nelson  in  the  Republican  primary
in  the  Fourth  Congressional  District  of  Massachusetts.
She  is  also  active  in  the  Libertarian  Party  and  has  engaged
in  campaign-funding  solicitation  for  that  Party  in  New
York.

Affidavit  41;  Tr.  32-33;  38-39.

2.  In  the  1972  Republican  primary  in  the  Fourth

Congressional  District  of  Massachusetts,  there  were  four
candidates.  Two  were  incumbent  State  elected  officials;
one  was  a  former  Congressman.
Avi  Nelson,  the  fourth,
was  a  graduate  student,  had  no  connections  with  Repub-
lican  organizations  in  the  state,  and  had  never  held  pud-
lic  office,  and  was  living  on  student  loans  and  savings.
Affidavit  442,  3.

Nelson’s  name  recognition  factor  among  district  voters  was
less  than  one  percent.

Affidavit  4 3.

1.  The  Conservative  Victory  Fund  (hereinafter  CVF)

is  a  registered  political  committee  under  the  FECA  and
has  disclosed  the  names  of  contributors  of  over  $100
since  the  effective  date  of  the  FECA  of  1971.
Affidavit
43;  Tr.  5-6.  CVF  and  the  American  Conservative  Union
(hereinafter  ACU)  are  affiliated.  CVF  and  ACU  have
most  of  their  officers  in  common,  they  share  office
space,  they  work  on  common  matters  in  some  cases,  and
both  organizations  share  adherence  to  the  same  principles.
Tr.  89.

2.  CVF  is  an  unincorporated  association  of  the
District  of  Columbia,  established  in  1970  for  the  purpose
of  raising  funds  to  aid  in  the  election  of  candidates  for
public  office  who  are  dedicated  to  the  principles  of
conservatism.
Affidavit  €2.  One  of  the  purposes  of
the  ACU  is  to  lobby  on  behalf  of  or  against  specific
legislation  in  Congress.

Tr.  73.

CVF,  and  executed  the  affidavit  filed  by  CVF  in  this
action.

Affidavit  { 2.

3.  For  approximately  five  months  in  every  election
year  since  1962,  HUMAN  EVENTS  runs  a feature  called
“Races  of  the  Week”  in  which  it  spotlights  selected
electoral  contests  that  it  feels  would  be  of  interest  to  its

745

———————————————————————_————————

readers.
To  date,  it  has  run  over  400  of  these  stories  and

congressional  election  year.

Winter.

Affidavit  4 4.

5.  HUMAN  EVENTS  endorses  explicitly  or  by  impli-

cation  certain  candidates  for  federal  offices  or,  where  it
deems  it  necessary  and  appropriate,  it  refrains  from  en-
dorsing  any  candidate  for  a  particular  federal  office.
Affidavit  4 5.

5.  HUMAN  EVENTS  readers  are  active  politically.

Its  most  recent  readership  survey  showed  that  in  the  12
months  prior  to  the  survey,  14  percent  of  its  subscribers
held  office  in  a  political  group  or  organization;  30  percent
had  been  volunteer  workers  in  a  political  campaign,  32
percent  had  attended  regular  meetings  of  political  groups
or  organization;  70  percent  had  written  letters  to  educators,
editors  or  elected  representatives;  and  73  percent  had  made
at  least  one  financial  contribution  to  a  political  party,  a
candidate  or  a  political  organization.

Affidavit  47.

7.  HUMAN  EVENTS  perceives  its  harm  as  arising
from  the  fact  that  the  statutory  definition  of  “expenditure”
in  2  U.S.C.  § 431(f)  and  18  U.S.C.  §  591(f)  explicitly
exempts  any  “news  story,  commentary,  or  editorial  dis-
tributed  through  the  facilities  of  any  broadcasting  station,
newspaper,  magazine,  or  other  periodical  publications,
unless  such  facilities  are  owned  or  controlled  by  any
political  party,  political  committee,

[058]

or  candidate”  in  contrast  to  the  statutory  definition  of
“contribution”  in  2  U.S.C.  § 431(e)  and  18  U.S.C.  §
591(3)  which  does  not.

Tr.  4.

8.  HUMAN  EVENTS  has  explicitly  or  implicitly
endorsed  candidates  for  federal  office  in  the  past  and
intends  to  do  so  in  the  future.

service.

whether  these  incumbents  supported  or  opposed  positions

PART  IV.  CONSTITUTIONAL  QUESTIONS

[002]

May  19,  1975

[001]

CONSTITUTIONAL  QUESTIONS

1.

Does  the  first  sentence  of  8315(a)  of  the  Federal

Election  Campaign  Act,  as  amended,  2 U.S.C.  §437h(a),  in
the  context  of  this  action,  require  courts  of  the  United
States  to  render  advisory  opinions  in  violation  of  the  “case
or  controversy”  requirement  of  Article  III,  82,  of  the  Con-
stitution  of  the  United  States?

2.

Has  each  of  the  plaintiffs  alleged  sufficient  injury

to  his  constitutional  rights  enumerated  in  the  following
questions  to  create  a  constitutional  “case  or  controversy”
within  the  judicial  power  under  Article  III?

3.

Does  any  statutory  limitation,  or  do  the  particular
limitations  in  the  challenged  statutes,  on  the  amounts  that
individuals  or  organizations  may  contribute  or  expend  in
connection  with  elections  for  federal  office  violate  the

rights  of  one  or  more  of  the  plaintiffs  under  the  First,
Fifth,  or  Ninth  Amendment  or  the  Due  Process  Clause
of  the  Fifth  Amendment  of  the  Constitution  of the  United
States?

|

that  it  forbids  the  solicitation,  receipt  or  making  of
contributions  on  behalf  of  political  candidates  in  excess
of  the  amounts  specified  in  18  U.S.C.  8608(b)?  (FAC
qq  55,  59,  60,  61,  62,  86,  87,  88.)

and  608(b)?  (FAC  9 @  57,  59,  60,  61,  62,  86,  87,  88.)

PREFACE

Over  the  last  several  years,  far-reaching  changes  have  been  made  in
the  laws  affecting  Federal  elections  and  election  campaign  practices.
These  reforms,  because  they  touch  on  so  many  varied  aspects  of  the
electoral  process,  are  only  to  be  found  scattered  among  the  different
titles  of  the  United  States  Code,  among  the  volumes  of  the  United
States  Statutes  at  Large,  or  in recent  slip  laws.

The  Senate  Committee  on  Rules  and  Administration  perceived  the
need  and  usefulness  of  a  small  pamphlet  that  could  present  to  candi-
dates  for  Federal  office  the  most  pertinent  of these  new  laws  in  a  single
compilation  and  in  the  most  up-to-date  form.  The  Committee  accord-
ingly  requested  the  Office  of  the  Secretary  of  the  Senate  to  prepare
such  a  booklet  in  order  that  candidates  might  be  better  able  to  deter-
mine  and  meet  the  requirements  demanded  by current  legislation.

This  document  is, therefore,  a  compilation  of  Federal  election  cam-
paign  laws,  gathered  together  in  a  codified  form.  This  style  was
adopted  because  it  was  considered  the  most  suitable  for  presenting
the  laws  in  the  most  timely  manner,  unhampered  by  provisions  that
have  been  repealed  or  are  otherwise  irrelevant.  The  citations  to  the
United  States  Code  have  been  retained  in  order  to  facilitate  cross-
references  among
this  pamphlet,  the  United  States  Code,  and  the
United  States

Code  Annotated.

Users  of this  compilation  should  be aware  that  many  terms  relating
to elections  (such  as  “election,”  “candidate,”  “contribution,”  “expendi-
ture,”  etc.)  are  used  repeatedly  in  the  law,  but  are  frequently  rede-
fined  from  title  to  title.  Thus,  the  meaning  of  a  particular  term  may
not  be consistent  throughout  this
pamphlet.  Care  should  be  exercised
to  check  for  the  definitions  provided  in  any  given  title  or  section.

It  should  also  be  understood  that  the  omission  of  any  section  or
sections  from  the  numerical  sequence  indicates  that  the  content  of  the
missing  part  is  irrelevant  to  the  purposes  of  this  compilation,  that
the  section  has  been  repealed,  or  that  the  section  number  does  not
exist  at  the present  time.

Every  effort  has  been  made  to  incinde  the  most  recent  legislation
on  elections,  especially  the  Federal  Election  Campaign  Act  of  1971
(P.L.  92-225)  and  the  Federal  Election  Campaign  Act  Amendments
of  1974  (P.L,  93-443).  A number  of other  important  election  laws  are
also  included,  such  as  the  Hatch  Act  and  the  relevant  sections  of  the
Communications  Act  of  1934,  both  as  most  recently  amended.

Generally,  the  Federal  Election  Campaign  Act  Amendments  of
1974  became  effective  January  1,  1975.  Those  provisions,  however,
dealing  with  the  effect  on  State  law  became  effective  October  15,  1974,
while  those  amending  Title  26  apply  with  respect  to  taxable  years  be-
ginning  after  December  31.  1974.

This  compilation  can  be  regarded  as  comprehensive  and  accurate  as
of  the  end  of  the  Ninety-third  Congress  in  December  1974.  The  com-
piler  was  Patrick  T.  Ortiz,  Reference  Assistant  on  the  staff  of  the
Senate  Library.

Francis  R.  Varro,
Sec  retary  of the  Senate.

(111)

TITLE  2.  THE  CONGRESS

Cuaprer  1—E.ection  or  SENATORS  AND  REPRESENTATIVES

§ 1.  Time  for election  of Senators

At  the  regular  election  held  in any  State  next  preceding  the  cxpira-
tion  of  the  term  for  which  any  Senator  was  elected  to  represent  such
State  in  Congress,  at  which  election  a  Representative  to  Congress  is
regularly  by  law  to  be chosen,  a  U.S.  Senator  from  said  State  shall  be
elected  by the  people  thereof  for  the  term  commencing  on  the  third
day of January  next  thereafter.
§ la.  Election  to be certified  by Governor

It  shall  be  the  duty  of  the  executive  of  the  State  from  which  any
Senator  has  been  chosen  to  certify  his  election,  under  the  seal  of  the
State,  to the  President  of the  Senate  of the  United  States.
§ 1b.  Same;  countersignature  by Secretary  of  State

The  certificate  mentioned  in section  la of this  title  shall  be counter-

signed  by the  Secretary  of State  of the  State.
§ 7.  Time  of election

The  Tuesday  next  after  the  first  Monday  in November,  in every  even
numbered  year,  is established  as  the  day  for the election,  in each  of the
States  and  territories  of  the  United  States,  of  Representatives  and
Delegates  to  the  Congress  commencing  on  the  third  day  of  January
next  thereafter.  This  section  shall  not  apply  to  any  State  that  has  not
yet  changed  its  day  of  election,  and  whose  constitution  must  be
amended  in order  to effect  a  change  in the  day of the  election  of State
officers  in said  State.
§ 8  Vacancies

The  time  for holding  elections  in any  State,  district,  or  territory  for
a  Representative  or  Delegate  to  fill  a vacancy,  whether  such  vacancy  is
caused  by a  failure  to  elect  at  the  time  prescribed  by law,  or  by the
death,  resignation,  or  incapacity  of a person  elected,  may  be prescribed
by the laws of the several  States  and territories  respectively.
§ 9.  Voting  for  Representatives

All  votes  for  Representatives  in  Congress  must  be  by  written  or
printed  ballot,  or  voting  machine,  the  use  of  which  has  been  duly  au-
thorized  by  the  State  law;  and  all  votes  received  or  recorded  contrary
to this section  shall  be of no effect.

{N.B.  Chapter  8,  known  as  the  Federal  Corrupt  Practices  Act,  was  repealed  b

Election  Campaign  Act  of  1971  (P.L.  92-225) }

pealed

by  the  Federal

§ 381.  Definitions

For  purposes  of this chapter—
(a)  The  term  “election”  means  an  official  general  or  special  election
to  choose  a  Representative  in  or  Resident  Commissioner  to  the  Con-
(1)

2

3

gress  of the  United  States,  but does  not  include  a primary  election,  ora
caucus  or  convention  of a political  party.

(b)  The term  “candidate”  means  an  individual—

(1)  whose  name  is printed  on  the  official  ballot  for  election  to

the  House  of Representatives  of the United  States;  or

(2)  notwithstanding  his name  is not  printed  on  such  ballot,  who
secks  election  to  the  House  of  Representatives  by write-in  votes,
provided  that  he is qualified  for such  office  and  that, under  the  law
of the  State  in which  the  congressional  district  is located,  write-in
voting  for  such  office  is  permitted  and  he  is  eligible  to  receive
write-in  votes  in such  election.

(c)  The  term  “contestant”  means  an  individual  who  contests  the
election  of  a  Member  of  the  House  of  Representatives  of  the  United
States  under  this  chapter.

(<1)  The  term  “contestee™  means  a  Member  of the  House  of  Repre-
sentatives  of  the  United  States  whose  election  is contested  under  this
chapter.

( Y The  term  “Member”  means  an  incumbent  Representative  in  or
Resident  Commissioner  to  the  Congress  of the  United  States,  or  an  in-
dividual  who  has been  elected  to either  of such  offices  but  has not  taken
the oath  of office.

(f)  The  term  “Clerk”  means  the  Clerk  of the  House  of Representa-

tives of the  United  States.

(gz)  The  term  “committee”  means  the  Committee  on  House  Adminis-

tration  of the  House  of Representatives  of the United  States.

(h)  The term  “State”  includes  territory  and possession  of the  United

States.

(i)  The  term  “write-in  vote”  means  a  vote  cast  for  a  person  whose
name  does  not  appear  on  the  official  ballot  by writing  in the  name  of
such  person  on  such  ballot  or  by any  other  method  prescribed  by the
law of the State  in which  the election  is held.
§ 382.  Notice  of contest

(a)  Filing  of notice.  Whoever,  having  been  a  candidate  for election
to  the  House  of  Representatives  in  the  last  preceding  election  and
claiming  a  right  to such  office,  intends  to contest  the election  of  a Mem-
ber  of  the  House  of  Representatives,  shall,  within  30  days  after  the
result  of such  election  shall  have  been  declared  by the  officer  or  board
of  canvassers  authorized  by law  to  declare  such  result,  file  with  the
Clerk  and  serve  upon  the  contestee  written  notice  of  his  intention  to
contest  such  election.

(bb)  Contents  and  form  of notice.  Such  notice  shall  state  with  par-
ticularity  the  grounds  upon  which  contestant  contests  the  election  and
shall  state  that  an  answer  thereto  must  be  served  upon  contestant  un-
der  section  383  of this  title  within  30 days  after  service  of such  notice.
Such  notice  shall  be  signed  by contestant  and  verified  by his  oath  or
affirmation.

(c)  Service  of notice;  proof of service.  Service  of the  notice  of con-

test  upon  contestee  shall  be made  as  follows:

(1)  by delivering  a  copy  to  him  personally;
(2)  by  leaving  a  copy  at  his  dwelling  house  or  usual  place  of
abode  with  a  person  of  discretion  not  less  than  16  years  of  age
then  residing  therein;

(3)  by leaving  a copy  at  his principal  office  or  place of business

with  some

person  then  in charge thereof;

(4)  by  delivering  a  copy  to  an  agent  authorized  by appoint-

ment to receive  service of such notice ; or

(5)  by mailing  a copy  by registered  or  certified  mail  addressed
office  or  place of business.

to contestee  at his residence  or  principal
Service  by mail  is complete upon  mailing;

(6)  the  verified  return  by the  person  so  serving  such  notice.
setting  forth  the  time  and  manner  of such  service  shall  be  proof
of  same,  and  the  return  post  office  receipt  shall  be  proof  of  the
service  of  said  notice  mailed  by  registered  or  certified  mail  as
aforesaid.  Proof  of service  shall  be  made  to  the  Clerk  promptly
and  in any  event  within  the time  during  which  the  contestee  must
answer  the notice  of contest.  Failure  to make  proof of service  does
not affect the validity of the service.

§ 383.  Response  of  contestee

(a)  Answer.  Any  contestee  upon  whom  a  notice  of  contest  as  de-
scribed  in section  382  of this  title  shall  be served.  shall.  within  30 days
after  the  service  thereof,  serve  upon  contestant  a  written  answer  to
such  notice.  admitting  or  denying  the  averments  upon  which  contest-
ant  relies.  If contestee  is without  knowledge  or  information  sufficient
to  form  a  belief  as  to  the  truth  of  an  averment,  he  shall  so  state  and
this  shall  have  the  effect  of  a  denial.  Such  answer  shall  set  forth  af-
firmatively  any  other  defenses,  in law  or  fact,  on  which  contestze  relies.
Contestee  shall  sign  and  verify  such  answer  by oath  or  affirmation.

(b)  Defenses  by motion  prior  to answer.  At  the  option  of contestee,
the  following  defenses  may  be made  by motion  served  upon  contestant
prior to contestee’s  answer :

(1)  insufficiency  of service  of notice  of contest  :
(2)  lack  of standing  of contestant:
(3)  failure  of  notice  of  contest  to  state  grounds  sufficient  to

change  result  of election:

(4)  failure  of contestant  to  claim  right  to  contestee’s  seat.
(c)  Motion  for  more  definite  statement.  If  a  notice  of  contest  to
which  an  answer  is  required  is  so  vague  or  ambiguous  that  the  con-
testee  cannot  reasonably  be  required  to  frame  a  responsive  answer,
he  may  move  for  a  more  definite  statement  before  interposing  his
answer.  The  motion  shall  point  out  the  defects  complained  of and  the
details  desired.  If the  motion  is granted  and  the  order  of the  commit-
tee  is  not  obeyed  within  10  days  after  notice  of  the  order  or  within
such  other  time  as  the  committee  may  fix,  the  committee  may  dismiss
the  action,  or  make  such  order  as  it deems  just.

(dl)  Time  for serving  answer  after  service  of motion.  Service  of  a
motion  permitted  under  this  section  alters  the  time  for  serving  the
answer  as  follows,  unless  a  different  time  is fixed  by order  of the  com-
mittee  : If the  committee  denies  the motion  or  postpones  its disposition
until  the  bearing  on  the  merits,  the  answer  shall  be  served  within  10
days  after  notice  of such  action.  If the  committee  grants  a  motion  for
a  more  definite  statement  the  answer  shall  be  served  within  10  days
after  service  of  the  more  definite  statement.
§ 384.  Service  and  filing  of  papers  other  than  notice  of contest

(a)  Modes  of service.  Except  for  the  notice  of contest.  every  paper
required  to  be  served  shall  be  served  upon  the  attorney  representing

shall  be accompanied  by proof of service  showing  the time  and  mamer

;
§ 385.  Default  of contestee

The  failure  of contestee  to  answer  the  notice  of contest  or  to other-
wise  defend  as  provided  by this  chapter  shall  not  be deemed  an  admis-
sion  of  the  truth  of  the  averments  in  the  notice  of  contest.  Notwith-
standing  such  failure,  the  burden  is upon  contestant  to  prove  that  the
election  results  entitle  him  to  contestee’s  seat.
§ 386.  Deposition

(a)  Oral eramination.  Either  party  may  take  the  testimony  of any
person,  including  the  opposing  party,  by  deposition  upon  oral  exam-
ination  for  the  purpose  of discovery  or  for  use  as  evidence  in the con-
tested  election  case,  or  for  both  purposes.  Depositions  shall  be  taken
only  within  the  time  for  the  taking  of  testimony  prescribed  in this
section.

(b)  Scope  of examination.  Witnesses  may  be  examined  regarding
any  matter,  not  privileged,  which  is  relevant  to  the  subject  matter
involved  in  the  pending  contested  election  case.  whether  it  relates  to
the  claim  or  defense  of the  examining  party  or  the  claim  or  defense  of
the  opposing  party.  including  the  existence.  description.  nature.  cus-
tody.  condition,  and  location  of any  books,  papers,  documents,  or  other
tangible  things  and  the  identity  and  location  of persons  having  know]-
edge  of  relevant  facts.  After  the  examinirg  party  has  examined  the
witness  the  opposing  party  may  cross-examine.

(c)  Order  and  time  of taking  testimony.  The  order  in  which  the

parties  may  take  testimony  shall  be  as  follows:

(1)  contestant  may  take  testimony  within  39  days  after  serv-
ice  of the  answer.  or  if no  answer  is served  within  the  time  pro-
vided  in  section  383  of this  title,  within  30 days  after  the time  for
answer  has  expired:

(2)  contestee  may  take  testimony  within  30  days  after  con-

testant’s  time  ee testimony  has  expired;

—

(3)  if contestee  has taken  any  testimony  or  has  filed  testimonial
affidavits  or  stipulations  under  section  387(c)  of  this  title,  con-
testant  may  take  rebuttal  testimony  within  10  days  after  con-
testee’s  time  for  taking  testimony  has  expired.

(d)  a. before  whom  testimony  may

be  taken.  Testimony  shall
before  an  officer  authorized  to  administer  oaths  by the  laws

he taken
of the  United  States  or  of the  place  where  the  examination  is held.

(e)  Subpena,  Attendance  of witnesses  may  be compelled  by subpena

as  provided  in section  388  of this  title.

(f)  Taking  of testimony  by party  or  his  agent.  At  the  taking  of
testimony,  a  party  may  appear  and  act  in  person,  or  by his  agent  or
attorney.

(g)  Conduct  of examination;  recordation  of testimony;  notation  of
objections ; interrogatories.  The  officer  before  whom  testimony  is to  be
taken  shall  put  the  witness  under  oath  and  shall  personally,  or  by
someone  acting  under  his  direction  and  in  his
presence,  record  the
testimony  of the witness.  The testimony  shall  be taken  stenographically
and  transcribed.  All  objections  made  at  the  time  of examination  to  the
qualifications  of  the  officer  taking  the  deposition,  or  to  the  manner  of
taking  it, or  to  the  evidence  presented,  or  the  conduct  of  any  party,
and  any  other  objection  to the proceedings,  shall  be noted  by the  officer
upon  the —— Evidence  objected  to  shall  be taken  subject  to  the
objections.
In  lieu  of  participating  in  the  oral  examination,  a  party
served  with  a notice  of deposition  may  transmit  written  interrogatories
to  the  officer,  who  shall  propound  them  to  the  witness  and  record  the
answers  verbatim.

(h)  Examination  of deposition  by witness;  signature  of witness  or
officer;  use  of deposition.  When  the testimony  is fully  transcribed,  the

§ 387.  Notice  of depositions

(a)  Time  for service;  form,  A party  desiring  to  take  the  deposition
of any  person  upon  oral  examination  shall  serve  written  notice  on the
opposing  party  not  later  than  2 days  before  the  date  of the  examina-
tion.  The  notice  shall  state  the time  and  place  for taking  the  deposition
and  the  name  and  address  of  each  person  to  be  examined.  A  copy  of
such  notice,  together  with  proof  of  such  service  thereof,  shall  be  at-
tached  to  the  deposition  when  it is filed  with  the  Clerk.

(b)  Testimony  by stipulation,  By written  stipulation  of the  parties.
the  deposition  of  a  witness  may  be  taken  withont  notice.  A  copy  of

(c)  Testimony  by affidavit;  time  for filing.  By written  stipulation
of the  parties,  the testimony  of any  witness  of either  party  may  be filed
in  the  form  of  an  affidavit  by such  witness  or  the  parties  may  agree

$388.  Subpena  for attendance  at deposition

(a)  Jssvance,  Upon  application  of any  party,  a  subpena  for  attend-

ance  at  a  deposition  shall  be  issned  by-—

(1)  a  judge  or  clerk  of  the  U.S.  district  court  for  the  district

in which  the  place of examination  is located ;

(2)  a  judge  or  clerk  of any  court  of record  of the State  in which

the  place of examination  is located;  or

(3)  a  judge  or  clerk  of  any  court  of  record  of  the  county  in

which  the  place  of examination  is located.

(b)  Lime,  method,  and  proof  of  service,  Service  of  the  subpena
shall  be  made  upon  the  witness  no  later  than  3 days  before  the  day  on
which  his  attendance  is directed.  A subpena  may  be served  by any per-
son  who  is not’a  party  to  the  contested  election  case  and  is not  less  than
18  vears  of  age.  Service  of  a  subpena  upon  a  person  named  therein
shall  be  made  by  delivering  a  copy  thereof  to  such  person  and  by
tendering  to  him  the  fee  for  one  dav’s  attendance  and  the  mileage
allowed  by  section  389  of  this  title.  Written  proof  of  service  shall  be
made  under  oath  by the  person  making  same  and  shall  be  filed  with
the  Clerk.

(c)  Place  of eramination,  A  witness  may  be  required  to  attend  an
examination  only  in  the  county  where  he  resides  or  is employed.  or
transacts  his  business  in person,  or  is served  with  a  subpena,  or  within
40  miles  of the  place  of service.

(d)  Form.  Every  subpena  shall  state  the  name  and  title  of the  officer
issuing  same  and  the  title  of the  contested  election  case,  and  shall  com-
mand  each  person  to  whom  it is directed  to  attend  and  give  testimony
at  a  time  and  place  and  before  an  officer  specified  therein.

(e)  Production  of documents,  A  subpena  may  also  command  the
person  to  whom  it is directed  to  produce  the  books.  papers,  documents,
or  other  tangible  things  designated  therein.  but  the  committee,  upon
motion  promptly  made  and  in any  event  at  or  before  the  time  specified
in  the  subpena  for  compliance  therewith,  may—

(1)  quash  or  modify  the  subpena  if  it  is  unreasonable  or

oppressive : or

(2)  condition  denial  of the motion  upon  the advancement  by the
party  in whose  behalf  the subpena  is issued  of the  reasonable  cost
of  producing  the  books,  papers,  documents,  or  tangible  things.
In  the  case  of  public  records  or  documents,  copies  thereof,  certi-
fied  by the
person  having  official  custody  thereof,  may  be produced
in lieu  of  the originals.

g

§ 389.

Officer  and  witness  fees

(a)  Each  judge,  clerk  of court.  or  other  officer  who  issues  any  sub-
pena  or  takes  a  deposition  and  each  person  who  serves  any  subpena  or

other  paper  herein  authorized  shal]  be  entitled  to  receive  from  thie
party  at  whose  instance  the  service  shall  have  been  performed  such
fees  as  are  allowed  for  similar  services  in  the  district  courts  of  the
United  States.

(b)  Witnesses  whose  depositions  are  taken  shall  be  entitled  to  re-
ceive  from  the  party  at  whose  instance  the  witness  appeared  the  same
fees  and  travel  allowance  paid to witnesses  subpenaed  to appear  before
the  House  of Representatives  or  its committees.
§ 390.  Penalty  for  failure  to  appear,  testify,  or  produce  docu-

ments

Every  person  who,  having  been  subpenaed  as  a  witness  under  this
chapter  to  give  testimony  or  to  produce  documents,  willfully  makes
default,  or  who,  having  appeared,  refuses  to answer  any  question  per-
tinent  to  the  contested  election  case,  shall  be deemed  guilty  of a  misde-
meanor  punishable  by fine  of  not  more  than  $1,000  nor  less  than  $100
or  imprisonment  for  not  less  than  1 month  nor  more  than  12  months,
or  both.
§ 391.

Certification  and  filing of depositions

(a)  Sealing  of papers;  deposit  with  Clerk.  The  officer  before  whom
any  deposition  is taken  shall  certify  thereon  that  the  witness  was  duly
sworn  by him  and  that  the  deposition  is a true  record  of the  testimony
given  by the  witness.  He  shall  then  securely  seal  the  deposition,  to-
gether  with  any  papers  produced  by the  witness  and  the  notice  of
deposition  or  stipulation,  if the  deposition  was  taken  without  notice,
in  an  envelope  endorsed  with  the  title  of  the  contested  election  case
and  marked  “Deposition  of  (here  insert  name  of  witness)”  and  shall
within  30  days  after  completion  of the  witness’  testimony,  file  it with
the  Clerk.

(b)  Notification  of filing.  After  filing the deposition,  the officer  shall

promptly  notify  the  parties  of its filing.

7

(c)

Copy  of deposition  to  parties  or  deponents.

Upon  payment  of
reasonable  charges  therefor,  not  to  exceed  the  charges  allowed  in  the
district  court  of the  United  States  for the  district  wherein  the  place  of
examination  is located,  the  officer  shall  furnish  a  copy  of deposition  to
any  party  or  the  deponent.
§ 392.  Record

(a)  Hearing  on  papers,  depositions,  and exhibits.  Contested  election
eases  shall  be  heard  by the  committee  on  the  papers,  depositions,  and
exhibits  filed  with  the  Clerk.  Such  papers,  depositions,  and  exhibits
shall  constitute  the  record  of the  case.

(b)  Appendix  to  contestants  brief.  Contestant  shall  print  as  an
appendix  to  his  brief  those  portions  of the  record  which  he desires  the
committee  to  consider  in order  to  decide  the  case  and  such  other  por-
tions  of the  record  as  may  be prescribed  by the  rules  of the committee.
(c)  Appendix  to  contestee’s  brief.  Contestee  shall  print  as  an  ap-
pendix  to his brief  those  portions  of the  record  not  printed by contest-
ant  which  contestee  desires  the  committee  to  consider  in  order  to
decide  the  case.

;

8

9

(d)  Contestant’s  brief ; service  on  contestee.  Within  45  days  after
the  time  for  both  parties  to  take  testimony  has  expired,  contestant
shall  serve  on  contestee  his  printed  brief  of  the  facts  and  authorities
relied  on  to  establish  his  case  together  with  his  appendix.

(e)  Contestee’s  brief ; service  on  contestant.  Within  30 days of serv-
ice of contestant’s  brief  and  appendix,  contestee  shall  serve  on  contest-
ant  his  printed  brief  of the  facts  and  authorities  relied  on  to establish
his  case  together  with  his  appendix.

(f)  Reply  brief  of contestant.  Within  10  days  after  service  of con-
testee’s  brief  and  appendix,  contestant  may  serve  on  contestee  a  printed
reply  brief.

(g)  Form  of briefs;  number  of copies  served  and  filed.  The  form
and  length  of the  briefs,  the  form  of the  appendixes,  and  the  number
of copies  to  be served  and  filed  shall  be in accordance  with  such  rules
as  the  committee  may  prescribe.
§ 393.  Filing  of  pleadings,  motions,  depositions,  appendixes,

briefs,  and  other  papers

(a)  Filings  of  pleadings,  motions,  > |  —emgaiane appendixes,  briefs,

and  other  papers  shall  be  accomplished  by—

(1)  delivering  a  copy  thereof  to  the  Clerk  of  the  House  of
Representatives  at  his  office  in Washington,  D.C.,  or  to  a  member
of  his  staff  at  such  office:  or

(2)  mailing  a  copy  thereof,  by  registered  or  certified  mail,
addressed  to  the  Clerk  at  the  House  of  Representatives,  Wash-
ington,  D.C.:  Provided,  That  if such  copy  is not  actually  received,
another  copy  shall  be  filed  within  a  reasonable  time;  and

(3)  delivering  or  mailing,  simultaneously  with  the  delivery  or
mailing  of  a  copy  thereof  under  paragraphs  (1)  and  (2)  of this
subsection,  such  additional  copies  as  the  committee  may  by rule
prescribe.

(b)  All  papers  filed  with  the  Clerk  pursuant  to  this  chapter  shall

be  promptly  transmitted  by him  to  the  committee.
§ 394.  Computation  of time

(a)  Method  of computing  time.  In  computing  any  period  of  time
prescribed  or  allowed  by this  chapter  or  by the  rules  or  any  order  of
the  committee.  the  day  of  the  act,  event,  or  default  after  which  the
designated  period  of time  begins  to  run  shall  not  be included.  The  last
day  of  the  period  so  computed  shall  be  included,  unless  it is a  Satur-
day.  Sunday,  or  a  legal  holiday.  in  which  event  the  period  shall  run
until  the  end  of the  next  day which  is neither  a  Saturday,  Sunday,  nor
a  legal  holiday.  When  the  period  of time  prescribed  or  allowed  is less
than  7 days,  intermediate  Saturdays.  Sundays,  and  legal  holidays  shall
be  excluded  in  the  computation.  For  the  purposes  of  this  chapter,
“legal  holiday”  shall  mean  New  Year’s  Day,  Washington’s  Birthday,
Memorial  Day. Independence  Day, Labor  Day, Veterans  Day, Thanks-
giving  Day, Christmas  Day,  and  any  other  day appointed  as  a holiday
by the  President  or  the  Congress  of the  United  States.

(bY  Service  by mail.  Whenever  a  party  has  the  right  or  is required
to  do  some  act  or  take  some  proceeding  within  a  prescribed  period
after  the  service  of  a  pleading,  motion,  notice.  brief.  or  other  paper
upon  him,  which  is served  upon  him  by mail,  3 days  shall  be  added  to
the  prescribed  period.

(c)  Enlargement  of time.  When  by this  chapter  or  by the  rules  or
required  or  allowed  to  be  done
any  order  of  the  committee  an  act  is
at  or  within  a  specified  time,  the  committee,  for  good  cause  shown,
may  at  any  time  in  its discretion—

(1)  with  or  without  motion  or  notice,  order  the period  enlarged
if  request  therefor  is  made  before  the  expiration  of  the  period
originally  prescribed  or  as  extended  by a  previous  order;  or

(2)  upon  motion  made  after  the  expiration  of  the  specified
period,  permit  the  act  to  be done  where  the  failure  to  act  was  tiie
result  of  excusable  neglect,  but  it  shall  not  extend  the  time  for
— and  filing  the  notice  of  contest  under  section  382  of  this
title.

§ 395.  Death  of contestant

In  the  event  of  the  death  of  the  contestant,  the  contested  election

case  shall  abate.
§ 396.  Allowance  of party’s  expenses

The  committee  may  allow  any  party  reimbursement  from  the  con-
tingent  fund  of  the  House  of  Representatives  of  his  reasonable  ex-
penses  of  the  contested  election  case,  including  reasonable  attorneys
fees,  upon  the  verified  application  of  such  party  accompanied  by  a
complete  and  detailed  account  of his expenses  and supporting  vouchers
and  receipts.

Crarrer  14—Feperat  Evection  CAMPAIGNS

§ 431.  Definitions

When  used  in this chapter—
(a)  “election”  means—

(1)  a  general,  special,  primary,  or  runoff  election ;
(2)  aconvention  or caucus  of a political  party held  to nominate

a  candidate;

(3)  a  primary  election  held  for  the  selection  of delegates  to  a

national  nominating

convention  of a political  party ; and

(4)  a  primary  election  held  for the  expression  of a  preference
~~ the  nomination  of  persons  for  election  to  the  office  of  Presi-
dent;

(b)  “candidate”  means  an  individual  who  seeks  nomination  for
election,  or  election,  to Federal  office,  whether  or  not  such  individual  is
elected,  and,  for  purposes  of  this  paragraph,  an  individual  shall  be
deemed  to  seek  nomination  for  election,  or  election,  if he has—

(1)  taken  the  action  necessary  under  the  law  of  a  State  to
qualify  himself  for nomination  for election, or election,  to Federal
office ; or

(2)  received  contributions  or  made  expenditures,  or  has  given
his  consent  for  any  other  person  to  receive  contributions  or  make
expenditures,  with  a  view  to  bringing  about  his  nomination  for
election,  or  election,  to  such  office;

(c)  “Federal  office”  means  the  office  of President  or  Vice  President
of the  United  States;  or  of Senator  or  Representative  in, or  Delegate
or  Resident  Commissioner  to, the Congress  of the United  States ;

or  other  group  of  persons  which  receives  contributions  or  makes  ex-

(g)  “Commission”  means  the  Federal  Election  Commission;
(h)  “person”  means  an  individual,  partnership.  committee,  associa-
tion,  corporation,  labor  organization,  and  any  other  organization  or
group  of persons;

(i)  “State”  means  each  State  of the  United  States,  the  District  of
Columbia,  the  Commonwealth  of  Puerto  Rico,  and  any  territory  or
possession  of the United  States;
(j)  “identification”  means—

(1)  in  the  case  of  an  individual.  his  full  name  and  the  full

address  of his principal  place of residence  ; and

of  such  person;

.

(k)  “national  committee”  means  the  organization  which,  by virtue
of  the  bylaws  of  a  political  party,  is  responsible  for  the  day-to-day
operation  of such  political  party  at  the  national  level,  as  determined

(1)  “State  committee”  means  the  organization  which,  by virtue  of
the  bylaws of a political  party.  is responsible  for the day-to-day  opera-

tion  which  nominates  a  candidate  for  election  to  any  Federal  office.
whose  name  appears  on  the  election  ballot  as  the  candidate  of  such
association,  committee.  or  organization:  and

(n)  “principal  campaign  committee”  means  the  principal  campaign
committee  designated  by a  candidate  under  section  432(f)  (1)  of  this
title.
§ 432.  Organization  of political  committees

(a)  Chairman;  treasurer;  vacancies;  official  authorizations.  Every
political  committee  shall  have  a  chairman  and  a  treasurer.  No  con-
tribution  and  no  expenditure  shall  be  accepted  or  made  by or  on  be-
half  of a  political  committee  at  a  time  when  there  is a  vacancy  in the
office  of chairman  or  treasurer  thereof.  No  expenditure  shall  be made
for  or  on  behalf  of a  political  committee  without  the  authorization  of
its chairman  or  treasurer.  or their  designated  agents.

13

(b)  Account  of contributions;  segregated  funds.  Every  person  who
receives  a  contribution  in excess  of $10  for  a  political  committee  shall,
on  demand  of  the  treasurer,  and  in  any  event  within  5  days  after
receipt  of such  contribution,  render  to the  treasurer  a  detailed  account
thereof,  including  the  amount  of  the  contribution  and  the  identifica-
tion  of  the  person  making  such  contribution,  and  the  date ‘on  which
received.  All  funds  of a  political  committee  shall  be segregated  from,
and  may  not  be  commingled  with,  any  personal  funds  of  officers,
members,  or  associates  of such  committee.

(c)  Recordkeeping.  It  shall  be  the  duty  of  the  treasurer  of  a  po-

litical  committee  to  keep  a  detailed  and  exact  amount  of —

(1) all contributions  made to or  for such  committee  ;
(2)  the  identification  of every  person  making  a  contribution  in
excess  of $10, and  the  date  and  amount  thereof  and,  if a  person's
contributions  aggregate  more  than  $100,  the  account  shall  include
occupation,  and

the  principal  place  of  business  (if any)  ;

¢ 3)  all  expenditures  made  by or  on  behalf  of such  committee;

an
_
(4) the  identification  of every  person  to  whom  any  expenditure
is made, the date  and amount  thereof  and  the name  and address  of,
and office  sought  by, each  candidate  on  whose  behalf  such  expendi-
ture  was  made.

(d)  Receipts;  preservation.  It shall  be the  duty  of the  treasurer  to
obtain  and  keep  a  receipted  bill,  stating  the  particulars,  for  every
expenditure  made  by or  on  behalf  of a political  committee  in excess  of
$100  in  amount,  and  for  any  such  expenditure  in  a  lesser  amount.  if
the  aggregate  amount  of such  expenditures  to the  same  person  during
a calendar  year  exceeds  $100.  The treasurer  shall  preserve  all  receipted
bills  and  accounts  required  to  be  kept  by this  section  for  periods  of
time  to  be determined  by the  Commission.

(e)  Unauthorized  activities  ; notice.  Any political  committee  which
solicits  or  receives  contributions  or  makes  expenditures  on  behalf  of
any  candidate  that  is not  authorized  in writing  by such  candidate  to
do  so  shall  include  a  notice  on  the  face  or  front  page  of all  literature
und  advertisements  published  in connection  with  such  candidate’s  cam-
paign  by such  committee  or  on  its behalf  stating  that  the committee  is
not  authorized  by  such  candidate  and  that  such  candidate  is  not
responsible  for the  activities  of such  committee.
(f)  Principal  campaign  committee;  reports,

filing.  (1)  Each  in-
dividual  who  is a  candidate  for Federal  office  (other  than  the  office  of
Vice  President  of the  United  States)  shall  designate  a  political  com-
mittee  to serve  as  his principal  campaign  committee.  No political  com-
mittee  may  be  designated  as  the  principal  campaign  committee  of
more  than  one  candidate.  except  that  the  candidate  for  the  office  of
President  of  the  United  States  nominated  by  a  political  party  may
designate  the  national  committee  of such  political  party  as  his  prin-
cipal  campaign  committee.  Except  as  provided  in  the  preceding  sen-
tence,  no  political  committee  which  supports  more  than  one  candidate
may  be designated  as  a  principal  campaign  committee.

(2)  Notwithstanding  any  other  provision  of this  title,  each  report
or  statement  of contributions  received  or  expenditures  made  by a polit-
ical  committee  (other  than  a  principal  campaign  committee)  which  is

14

15

required  to  be  filed  with  the  Commission  under  this  title  shall  be  filed
instead  with  the  principal  campaign  committee  for  the  candidate  on
whose  behalf  such  contributions  are  accepted  or  such  expenditures  are
made.

(3)  It  shall  be  the  duty  of  each  principal  campaign  committee  to
receive  all  reports  and  statements  required  to  be  filed  with  it under
paragraph  (2)  of this  subsection  and  to  compile  and  file  such  reports
and  statements,  together  with  its own  reports  and  statements,  with  the
Commission  in accordance  with  the provisions  of this title.
§ 453.  Registration  of political  committees

(a)  Statements  of  organization,  Each  political  committee  which
anticipates  receiving  contributions  or  making  expenditures  during  the
calendar  year  in an  aggregate  amount  exceeding  $1,000  shall  file  with
the  Commission  a  statement  of  organization,  within  10  days  after  its
organization  or,  if  later.  10  days  after  the  date  on  which  it  has  in-
formation  which  causes  the  committee  to  anticipate  it  will  receive
contributions  or  make  expenditures  in  excess  of  $1.000.  Each  such
coumittee  in existence  at  the  date  of enactment  of this  act  shall  file  a
statement  of  organization  with  the  Commission  at  such  time  as  it
prescribes,

(hb)  Contents  of statements,  The  statement  of  organization  shall

include—

(1)  the name  and  address  of the committee;
(2)  the  names.  addresses,  and  relationships  of affiliated  or  con-

nected  organizations;

(3)  the area, scope,  or  jurisdiction  of the committee  ;
(+)  the  name,  address,  and  position  of  the  custodian  of  books

and  accounts;

(5)  the  name,  address,  and  position  of other  principal  officers,
including  officers  and  members  of the  finance  committee.  if any:
(6)  the  name,  address,  office  sought,  and  party  affiliation  of—
(A) each  candidate  whom  the  committee  is  supporting:

anc

(B)  any  other  individual,  if any,  whom  the  committee  is
supporting  for  nomination  for  election,  or  election,  to  any
public  office  whatever;  or,  if the  committee  is supporting  the
entire  ticket  of  any  party,  the  name  of the  party;

(7)  astatement  whether  the  committee  is a  continuing  one;
(8)  the  disposition  of  residual  funds  which  will  be made  in the

event  of dissolution:

(9)  a  listing  of all  banks,  safety  deposit  boxes,  or  other  reposi-

tories  used:

(10)  a  statement  of the  reports  required  to  be filed  by the  com-

§ 434.  Reports  *

(a)  Receipts  and  expenditures;  completion  date,  exception.
(1)  Except  as  provided  by  paragraph  2,  each  treasurer  of  a
political  committee  supporting  a  candidate  or  candidates  for  election
to  Federal  office,  and  each  candidate  for  election  to  such  office,  shall
file  with  the Commission  reports  of receipts  and expenditures  on  forms
to  be  prescribed  or  approved  by it.

The  reports  referred  to  in  the  preceding  sentence  shall  be  filed  as

follows:

(.A)(i)  In any  calendar  vear  in which  an  individual  is  a  candi-
date  for  Federal  office  and  an  election  for  such  Federal  office  is
held  in  sueh  year,  such  reports  shall  be  filed  not  later  than  the
10th  day  before  the  date  on  which  such  election  is held  and  shall
le  complete  as  of  the  15th  day  before  the  date  of  such election:
except  that  any  such  report  filed  by  registered  or  certified  mail
must  be postmarked  not  later  than  the  close  of the  lzth  day  before
the date  of such  election:

(ii)  such  reports  saall  be  filed  not  later  than  the  30th  day after
the  date  of such  election  and  shall  be complete  as  of the  2oth  day
after  the date of such  election.

(B)  In  any  other  calendar  year  in  which  an  individual  is  a
candidate  for  Federal  office,  such  reports  shall  be  filed  after
